high.situation:
Although it may seem a very broad task, our main need as a group was to break the ice and get to know each other util we felt comfortable enough to work in our unit assignments. Improve our communication was the key if we wanted to succeed in the unit’s projects (Team meetings, RGM project planning).

The problem that happens to us is that nobody spoke, there was no initiative, and we weren’t sure what the next step was. This was not because we did not want to work, but because no one knew where to start. Therefore, needed a leader who could step up and guide the meetings and tasks. There was one of the members of the group who had good leadership traits and he was able to sort out were, when and how we were going to have the meetings. At first there was not much contribution from the rest, and we just nod along with the plan.
high.task_action: To overcome this challenge I read the requirements of each week (to understand what our short-term objective was) and started engaging more in the leadership of the tasks. I then realised that the more I contributed the I motivated others to do so. For example, as a group we struggled to find a theme for the RGM project, so I sent a couple of videos with RGM ideas, and we then discussed and inspired from them.  Each of us had to come up with one theme and then we anonymously voted the one we liked the most. I had the advantage that my group was open and made big efforts to understand me despite the language barrier.
high.result: As a result, we created more enriched ideas because they had contributions from all of us, not only from one perspective. Each of us has different approaches and skills to face tasks, so our projects reflect a variety of personal opinions.

high.situation: We needed to think of possible Rube Goldberg machine events while considering identified constraints discussed in class and as a group.
high.task_action:
Brainstorming possible RGM events during our weekly team meeting by creating a jovial, non-judgmental environment and using brainstorming techniques such as mind-mapping and round-robin brainstorming to generate ideas.

During week 5, our team met at the library to discuss conceptual ideas for our RGM. The meeting started off slow with few ideas being proposed. Some members expressed confusion about the task and needed clarification about what ideas should be generated.

By adopting a first-principles thinking framework, we first thought about our Rube Goldberg machine's goal and how we could achieve that. We started with a broad spectrum of ideas first so as to not limit ourselves to any particular type of event. We then asked each member to create an event that incorporates something unique about them. For example, using their hobby as a source of inspiration for an event or perhaps a pet if they have on etc. Following that, we used mind-mapping to relate our ideas to the main goal and promote out of the box thinking. We then used a round-robin style of brainstorming where we each took turns describing what ideas we thought worked best for our RGM and why.

Based on our collective input and pre-defined success metrics, a group of twelve events were chosen to be implemented in our RGM.

high.situation: Before we started on any conceptual design for our Rube Goldberg Machine we first had to figure out the purpose of our machine. What was it going to do in the end?
high.task_action: We underwent a brainstorming session as a group, numbering six, all members newly acquainted. The session lasted about half an hour in an online environment. With all six members sharing similar personalities. Working with a well defined end goal and a rough understanding of what was expected of us as students.
high.result: we decided to use the first and only proposal that was forwarded by our members with everyone agreeing very quickly to said proposal which was to pour some juice into a glass or cup.

high.situation: We needed to come up with a sequential series of events for the conceptual design of our Rube Goldberg machine.
high.task_action: During zoom meetings we discussed what items we had around our house that we could possibly use and the concepts we had without being degrading to ideas presented. We weighed up the possible trade-offs of our ideas respectfully towards finding a resolution using our judgements to piece together the best ideas for the machine.
high.result: The result was formulating a cohesive conceptual design with an idea that we were all satisfied with to be able to refine with the preliminary design.

high.situation:
We had to decide upon an innovative yet functional RGM theme.

Tasks/Action:

Assignments were set out sequentially by weeks, with certain tasks concerning the RGM theme requiring a high level of creative thinking. The RGM theme needed to be completed by the week 3 workshop, hence a pre-workshop meeting was needed to brainstorm ideas. Whilst tutors recommended completing a LinkedIn Learning course on group ideation, I didn’t complete this till after the pre-meeting. During the brainstorm session we set up a Miro board with the topics ‘theme’, ‘events’, and ‘materials’, allocating coloured sticky notes to put ideas up for each part. Approximately an hour was spent completing this process and therefore a large quantity of ideas were produced. We proceeded to present these theme ideas back in our week 3 workshop, highlighting the theme concepts we were more interested in however not a singular theme.

Following the creation and presentation of our problem statements in the subsequent week 4 workshop, we had the task of refining our problem statement to be more connected with our theme as well as producing a conceptual design for the week 5 workshop. Both required a definite theme, which our group had not decided upon. Our pre-workshop meeting for week 5 was allocated to refining our week 3 theme brainstorm into one theme. This was done by each individual selecting 2 ideas they liked in order to lower the quantity. Breakfast was a favourite however it was a common theme amongst other groups. Hence to be more creative we conducted a small 10 minute brainstorm session to communicate other possible meal times (e.g lunch and dinner) and how we could do a theme around them. We continued to then refine these ideas for the remainder of the session.
high.result: We were able to form 3 key themes surrounding meal times such as breakfast, lunch and dinner. We performed a group vote where a unanimous decision was in favour for the theme I had presented, an RGM to set up a dinner date for a couple. Thus we were able to tailor our problem statement to the theme, and begin our conceptual design.

high.situation:
Communication is a vital part of teamwork that we are being tested on throughout this semester.

In this semester we will design, construct and test a Rube Goldberg Machine completing individual sections individually, all contributing to the team’s final product.

With a project such as a RGM, that has so many small moving parts, it is vital that communication is strong and effective for our group to function correctly.

TASK:

The task at hand is to effectively create a communication structure that allows individuals to have a voice and work collectively on straying on-time and on-task.

ACTION:

Over the past 6 weeks, we have implemented technology to our advantage to create a strong and effective network of communication. Social applications such as ‘discord’, allowed us to share ideas on an online communication server, joining calls for online group meetings and having specific ‘chat’ channels to discuss certain topics.

We also implemented the social media platform ‘Snapchat’, as it meant we could easily communicate ideas when we are away from our computers, notifying our group chat of urgent meetings or tasks due. This tool will also be useful for efficiently sharing images as we can easily take photos on our phone-cameras of prototypes and ideas, then instantaneously upload them to the group.
high.result:
The result of these methods of communication were very effective as we were able to very easily communicate ideas without wasting time hosting multiple formal meetings.

Discord was vital for supporting our virtual meets and allowed us to complete tasks collectively using the call feature.

Snapchat was even more important especially in coordinating who would upload certain tasks and allocating roles to individuals. Organising set meeting times became very easy using the group chat function as everyone could share their availabilities so we could plan our meet times.

These technologies also had their negative impacts, often leading to distractions and off-topic conversations. Using our mobile phones also allowed for distraction from the group altogether, as people were easily persuaded to use other applications during meetings.

high.situation: We all need to communicate and organise ourselves to successfully construct a functional Rube Goldberg Machine and complete a series of weekly assessments and presentations in order to pass our course
high.task_action:
Approaching each weeks assignments and being able to function well as a team to get better marks; through weekly meetings (outside of session time), we were able to communicate and organise ourselves to approach the weekly assignments and evenly distribute the workload amongst each other in order to be prepared for each session. Additionally we were able to formulate a roster that clearly articulated to each member their role for each week.

RESULTS: This allowed us to keep us all on the same page on each others weekly duties and making sure everyone is happy with the groups decisions, giving us the opportunities to learn and figure out how to problem solve between a team in order to improve.

high.situation: We needed to develop a team communication style which allows all members to contribute maximally in accordance with their unique strengths.
high.task_action: Completing DISC and OCEAN personality tests and discussing the results with the team, we became aware of the personality traits of each team member. We then compiled a team charter which assessed our strengths, weaknesses and goals to allow us to collaborate effectively and assume roles consistent with our skills and personalities.
high.result: We were able to consider the different styles of each team member before undergoing the design process, and we were able to assign roles based on individual skillsets.

high.situation: After an ENGG 1050 workshop, documents have to be submitted by the end of the day. That it was my job to submit those completed documents. In addition to that, I also had a math quiz due that day which was 12% of my overall grade. To add more complexity to the day had an 8-hour shift ahead of me for that day. I would regularly finish work at 8:30-9 o’clock at night. I had known about the math quiz and work shift in advance. Completing all of my university tasks on time and meeting the requirements set seemed impossible.
high.task_action: Talked to my manager about my current situation. I broke down the rest of my day into 15-minute segments while on the bus to work. Putting in time for transport, dinner and work exposed how much time I really had for my tasks. Asking some of my ENGG team if they could also help though out the day on small parts of the documents. Additionally coming to an arrangement with my manager on when my breaks were and for how long also further expose the true amount of time I have.
high.result: Using all the time and resources at my disposal resulted in the completion and submission of the documents on time and to a high standard, completing the math quiz to the best of my ability and working for 8 hours with various breaks throughout that time.

high.situation: As a part of a team, I needed to think creatively and communicate possible ideas for the creation of a Rube Goldberg Machine.
high.task_action: Through completing a brainstorming session as a team using creative thinking we were able to draw up a list of possible themes and task ideas for our machine. Once we had several themes and task ideas, we conducted a group discussion to determine the overall viability of each idea and what was achievable for each team member.
high.result: The overall result was being able to clearly communicate and discuss possible ideas as a team to ultimately select one solution to put into action. From that solution, we were able to think creatively and develop 12 unique events to make up the machine.

high.situation: The situation was we were put into a group where we were left to design an RGM from scratch where we haven’t had much experience in mechanisms or project planning. We had easily planned what our RGM was supposed to achieve however struggled to come up with mechanisms/events to set up in our RGM. To add to this problem, we had to do majority of the planning and designing online which didn’t make explaining events easy to other team members.
high.task_action:
I recognised that we were lacking in ideas for events so I set the task for each member to do some research and come up with 3 of their own events and once this was done, we would write these down and discuss how they would interact with each other, and which would be the best order to put them. Our team did their research by googling other RGM’s and adapting some of their events to our theme of rubbish, replacing balls for rolling events with cardboard toilet paper rolls and cut up milk cartons instead of cups for our pulley systems etc...

The next problem of getting the other members to understand our 3 events through a zoom call was delt with by drawing each event on the zoom white board and if that failed sending a link and timestamp to where they got the inspiration for the event.
high.result: The result of these tasks led to the creation of all our events and for all our members to understand what each event involves and how it works so that we can then work on making each event interact with each other smoothly thus fine tune.

high.situation: During week five our group brainstormed our conceptual design for the Rube Goldberg Machine (RGM) which would involve various modularized systems culminating in pressing the space bar on a laptop.
high.task_action: Using the problem definition from the previous group meeting, we identified the design constraints for our RGM machine: each member creates two unique events with each lasting a minimum 3 seconds, each event flows seamlessly to the next, materials are supplied using household items and the total system lasts a maximum 5 minutes. Next, we established the sequence of system in terms of member, who has initiating system and who finishes the system. Subsequently, we brainstormed various ideas and systems we could use for different events such as dominoes, pulley systems, ramps, balls/marbles, tubes, fans and so on. From there, we collectively brainstormed variations how each item/idea could be implemented in an event. Once all the suggested ideas were thoroughly assessed, each idea was ranked against different variations in terms of feasibility given time constraint, complexity, and uniqueness.
high.result: The result was collectively selecting 10 conceptual ideas from the list of 20 ideas brainstormed. Further, two mechanisms were given to each member depending on their assigned position within the RGM system to maximise a seamless transition between each event. This was done collectively after the team discussed and assessed each idea in various positions along the RGM machine.

high.situation: During the first 3 weeks of ENGG1050, we were getting to know each other as a team while planning ahead for the upcoming weeks. As a part of this process, we needed to manage our times, in order for everyone to do equal amounts of work each week without it being unfair on anyone. Furthermore, we needed to organise a weekly meeting before each workshop.
high.task_action: At first, to decide on our weekly meeting time, we were just guessing and brute-forcing a time and a day that everyone was available. However, as more and more guesses were being rejected due to one or more team member not being available at the selected time and day, we soon figured out there has to be a better way of managing our times. To fix this inconvenience, we decided to create an excel spreadsheet, with the columns being the days of the week. Each of these columns were further separated to include each of our names. The rows were hours, starting from 9 AM and ending at 8 PM. As a group, we all filled out the cells with red if we were unavailable due to personal and/or educational reasons, and filled it with green if we were free. After observing this method, I also decided to make a similar spreadsheet for myself to organise times where I am free to work on my part of the project for each week.
high.result: As a result, we were able to quickly conclude a time and a day where everyone was free for the team meeting. Thus, this significantly helped us manage our times and make it fair on everyone. Furthermore, for my own spreadsheet, I was able to organise times each week where I was free to work on my part of the project without restricting any aspect of my lifestyle.

high.situation:
In my example, the experience took place at the Macquarie University within the context of a team/group project that is directed towards the Rube Goldberg Machine. This project was undertaken as part of an Engineering unit (Engg1050 - Engineering Design). My team consisted of six team members, including myself. During week 3, our team as a whole contributed to propose a suitable availability for our team meetings in order to enhance our internal communication on a weekly basis. 

TASK: Undergone a meeting session prior to our workshop, formed a small operational group and interacted on a cloud-based collaboration tool 'Miro', we constructed an organised schedule plan. This consisted hours and days we were available throughout a week. Essentially, our task was to resolve the communication barriers that arose as a result of dissimilar availability slots. 

ACTION: Consequently, through the use 'Miro' and our schedule plan, we evaluated each availability slots suggested via each team member and placed them in order of majority. This was by means of a majority based upon the amount of hours and team members that were able to contribute. In conjunction to this context, we had also nominated the person with the most time slots to create and upload links to the video conference platform on a weekly basis, so that we reduce the confusion amongst our group.
high.result: The result of the actions mentioned above resulted in successful conflict management, better and eased communication, and allowing us to advance into completing several tasks related to the project. The existence of our group meetings not only boost our team morale, but also played a major part in contributing to the satisfactory completion of tasks. At its core, the outcomes had significantly met the purpose of a team meeting, which is to share information efficiently and to provide scope for discussion around what is being shared.

high.situation: After individual introduction, our group consisted of very like minded individuals based off of our DISC and OCEAN results. This resulted in a limited amount of diversity affecting our creativity, understanding how to tackle an issue from multiple views & overall forming a strong link between each individual as we all had characteristics of seeking guidance rather than giving it.
high.task_action: As weeks passed, all members became more comfortable with sharing their opinions which gave me and two other members with more perseverance an opportunity to analyse which member needs guidance in different aspects of our Rube Goldberg Machine assignment. Pin pointing our weaknesses helped us lead a systematic approach towards who could pick up what would be dropped off by someone else. For example my strength as chairman and leading makes up for one of our more reserved members who claims to struggle in that department. in addition, to prevent unfair loads of responsibility based upon our strengths we take a moment within our extra meeting to work on each others flaws and address where a member could improve and how they could achieve development.
high.result: Out of the action taken, our team has been able to correlate & take advantage of our individuality & create strong links between each other. As levels of communication improved the groups creativity has had the opportunity to shine, further presenting the RGM to become a representation of each member, especially considering each problem faced became near inferior as the team worked in unison to provide an effective solution.

high.situation: In week 4 to 6, my team began the creation of the teams Rube Goldberg Machine conceptual design to establishing a thorough preliminary design for our machines end goal of making a coffee and incorporating them of coffee into different staged and events of the machine.
high.task_action:
Within the team meetings, myself and my group members began the construction of our conceptual design by brainstorming. high.situation: During week 6 our group had a meeting to discuss the feasibility and implementation of our actions that make up the RGB mechanism.

high.result: The result was implementing an alternate design and allocating a deadline in which each member of the group would test a part of the machine. At the deadline, we will communicate our individual evaluations of our parts.

high.situation: We needed to communicate ideas of RGM design to the group and ensure each section is unique.

To resolve this conflict, as a group we listened to each team member's capabilities with their creativity, materials available, and skill level and collectively decided what mechanisms would suit each team member.
high.result: The result was being able to effectively distribute ideas and mechanisms fairly and thus ensuring each team member was happy, content, and able to perform as fair as any other member.

high.situation: In the ENGG1050 week four workshop, I was required to present my group’s problem definition to my tutor group.
high.task_action: Using my group’s Miro board as a written and visual aid, I explained our client scenario, spoke about some of our metrics for measuring success and failure, and detailed the different constraints which pertain to our project. I also gave a brief run-through of the incomplete draft chain of events our group had devised so far.
high.result: The result was an approximately 5 minute presentation delivering a summary of our problem definition and draft conceptual design to my tutor and peers.

high.situation: We need to come up with a conceptual design for a Rube Goldberg Machine that turns on a kettle.
high.task_action: Undergoing a brainstorming session; we came up with ideas for a conceptual design. All of the actions mentioned were done with a kitchen theme to make it more practical. high.result: The result was being able to select 12 actions from the list generated in the brainstorming action. We broke down the required tasks into two for each member to work on.

high.situation: To plan and construct a Rube Goldberg Machine.
high.task_action: during this process, my group has had to get together and communicate effectively in order to put together a creative design. We each did research on ideas and looked at inspiration from other machines/past experiences in order to inspire us and think creatively. We gave constructive feedback to each other and eventually narrowed our ideas down while also trying to mitigate risks, coming to a final conclusion of our RGM.
high.result: the result of this was coming up with a unique idea with a theme that stands out. Being creative has also allowed for the reduction of wasteful resources as we have gathered materials that are reusable, recyclable, that can be repurposed, as well as items that we already had. Not only has this encouraged us to think creatively while also meeting the criteria as we had limits on resources, but it also helped to improve communication skills and critical thinking.

high.situation:
In the ENGG1050 unit, I have worked in a team consisting of four other undergraduate engineering students on a bi-weekly basis with the goal of ultimately constructing a Rube Goldberg Machine. Within this group, we have been required to produce deliverables on a weekly basis. This often involves working on the project outside of the allocated weekly meetings. Team members are expected to come prepared for each meeting by completing all of the pre-workshop activities and any additional tasks assigned to them during the previous meeting. During our group meetings, we work through the activities listed on the agenda. We use Miro to brainstorm ideas and take notes during team brainstorming sessions. We also plan, create and rehearse team presentations, reflect on our meetings as a group and discuss potential improvements we can make as a group to ensure we are communicating our ideas effectively and being productive.

TASK: Our task is to complete the design, build and optimization of a Rube Goldberg Machine. We will solve an engineering problem in line with the task constraints, apply structured processes to solve these problems, utilise the engineering design process and practice organisational and professional skills. Each team member in the group will construct part of the RGM and record it so the project can be compiled in one video. Each week we are required to present to our tutor on a given topic related to the design and build of our RGM. We also complete an agenda prior to each meeting and take meeting minutes during each workshop. Each week we reflect on our team’s progress towards the RGM as well as our effectiveness as a group. The agenda, meeting minutes and reflection are sent to our tutor on a weekly basis.

ACTION: In order to complete the above weekly deliverables (agenda, meeting minutes, reflection, presentation and pre-workshop activities), we have created project roles which are assigned to team members each week. These roles include the Chairperson, Presenter, Minute Taker, Project Manager and Tutor Liaison. The Chairperson speaks on behalf of our group when communicating in the tutor room or in Posse A. The Chairperson also takes any questions or enquiries we have as a group to either our tutor or unit convenor and then reports back to the group with the answer. The presenter is responsible for presenting our group PowerPoint or Miro Board at our Friday workshop meetings. Whilst we all work together to brainstorm and create these presentations, ultimately the presenter is responsible for ensuring that the presentation is ready and rehearsed in time for the workshop. The Minute Taker creates an agenda for both our Tuesday and Friday team meetings and then circulates the agendas to each team member before our meetings. The Minute Taker also takes meeting minutes at both our Tuesday and Friday meetings. The project manager is responsible for ensuring that our group stays on track during team meetings and makes it through all of the tasks listed on the agenda. The Tutor Liaison is in charge of compiling our agendas, meeting minutes and reflection in an email and then sending the professional email to our tutor. This is done on Friday afternoons immediately following the conclusion of the workshop. All team members are required to view the agenda circulated by the Minute Taker and complete the required activities before a meeting so that we all come prepared and ready to work productively as a group. These assigned roles rotate each week and have already been assigned for the rest of the semester.
high.result: Because we are required to turn in numerous deliverables on a weekly basis, it is crucial that all team members stay on top of their responsibilities each week. Even if just one group member forgets to complete part of their activities, it can set back the whole group significantly. While the system we have created works well most of the time, there have been a few occasions where team members have not completed their assigned tasks. On two occasions, the responsible team member did not email our tutor on the day of our Friday workshop. In one of the earlier weeks this semester, the presenter did not check the presentation document or rehearse their presentation which left our group scrambling last minute to throw something together. Overall, this system works very well because it is very clear and particular about who is completing what. When team members are proactive and check their weekly allocations, we are able to produce tasks to an excellent standard and work effectively during group meetings.

high.situation:
This unit ENGG1050 requires students to work in groups of around 6 people to create a Rube Goldberg Machine Project. We are to work together to complete this project. I knew that I had to improve my teamwork related skills in order to play my part as a one of six team members. I wanted this project to be done well, and I am sure that the other members want that too. The problem was during the first two workshops, I noticed some issues within our group, such as poor communication amongst each other (especially when trying to share ideas), that needed to be resolved in order to form an effective team.

 

TASK: For the project to be completed well, as a group, we all decided that it was best to form an effective team in order to work better and more efficiently. This requires each person to individually become a more effective team member, which was my goal.

 

ACTION: In the first workshop, my team members introduced themselves to the group. We spent some time learning some useful information about each member, such as their Engineering specialisation, skills, strengths, weaknesses, and the way they collaborate in a group setting. Some of this information was put on The Team Canvas on Miro. With this information, we were able to create an effective learning team by using this to assist each member.

We also created a group chat on Discord, because we all believe that one of the many aspects required to be an effective team is good communication amongst the members. We also made a timetable allocating roles to each member weekly.

Over the weeks, as a group, we tried to improve the way we communicate and work during the workshops. We used information such as the lectures provided, LinkedIn Learning, and other websites to gain a better understanding of being more effective and efficient in a group. I played my part in improving the team effectiveness and efficiency by focusing on myself, and the way I collaborated with others. Over the next few workshops, I began making myself aware of the way I communicated with others, and the areas where I could improve on, took note, and used the information I have gathered to improve myself for the next workshop. I believe that if each team member was aware of their weaknesses and improved, as a group, we would improve too, and that was what I was hoping for.

The weekly reflections in the workshops allowed us to pick up some issues in terms of our group structure and the way we get work done. As we all discussed this we began coming up with solutions. For example, a problem we found was that we often spoke on top of each other, so our solution was for the chairperson to call out each member to speak, rather than everyone speaking at once.
high.result:
I did notice a difference with my performance during the workshops, as I was able to get my ideas across in an appropriate and well-spoken manner. Other members were also able to get their ideas across. Conversations and decisions were made in a structured manner, where everyone was heard. More work was being done in the workshops as less time was wasted, and in shorter periods of time, more work was being done as tasks were being completed more efficiently. Communication definitely improved as we used Discord to communicate, which helped every member be aware of meeting times and assigned work.

Overall, as a group, we definitely improved in our communication, efficiency and effectiveness by taking on advice and learning from each other.

high.situation:
Throughout the first 5 weeks of Eng1050, as part of team C15 we’ve been through the first few stages of engineering design including brainstorming, conceptual design, and preliminary designing. Becoming acquainted with my teammates, Nathan, Keith, Jake, and Rita has been a rewarding and new experience and has opened my eyes to the importance of teamwork in an engineering setting

Task: We’ve been tasked to design and create a Rube Goldberg Machine under certain restrains and within a time limit. The machine has 10 separate events, 2 for each person, within a 13-week period (by the end of the semester)

Action: As a group we first needed to becoming familiar with each other through the use of some ice breakers and working together on our ENG1050 project. Through the use of Miro and Zoom Whiteboards, we have successfully completed our brainstorming and conceptual designs to a satisfactory standard. We have also begun the preliminary design phase, designing and finalising what we are planning to build for our RGM and the materials required.
high.result: The brainstorming included our problem definition, theme, and events of our RGM. Our problem definition is “Create an RGM with strict criteria (including materials and rubric) and time sensitivity (13 weeks)”. Our theme is “trash/garbage” to fit in with our material restrictions. As a team we thought that since we could only use items found around the house we would try to stick to this theme. The events have been a major accomplishment so far for our team, creating a system of flowing events in the RGM that we believe would work the best with each other. We also use a rotating system of roles, including chair, minute taker, and presenter to let everyone try different aspects of team roles and to build a more effective team, highlighting strengths and weaknesses of each team member.

high.situation: Starting from week 1 during the ENGG 1050  unit, we were tasked to creatively complete a Rude Goldburg Machine and each week we are to to choose one person within our team to conduct a oral presentation within our tutor groups.
high.task_action: Each week during a 3 hour long engineering online session, we brainstorm and complete required tasks for the week. We as a team brainstorm creatively and communicate possibilities in developing our RGM, each team member was given an opportunity to present ideas to the team and we would finalise our miro board to conduct a oral presentation to our tutor group.
high.result: The result was us being able to have all team members being able to creatively and successfully communicate their opinions and ideas within the group, and have each week a rotational structure for each member to conduct a oral presentation to the tutor group on behalf of the team.

high.situation: The innovation process of a project involves specific skills and challenges within a group setting, in the aims of achieving set goals and objectives. The group members all found this to be a new and eye opening experience, being first year students with little experience. Creating a Rube Goldberg Machine consists of many steps, concepts, ideas and challenges. The innovation process is the most fundamental part of the project, as it will be the backbone of the overall project. All ideas, planning and communications will be made during this stage of the project. The first couple of weeks, meeting online over Zoom and Discord meetings, is when the group carefully planned the project, discussing different innovative ideas and stages in order to achieve the goal, which is to create a successful Rube Goldberg Machine which pours fruit juice into a cup in the set amount of time.

This had initially presented small problems as we did not have many ideas initially, or ones that we all agreed on. After some brainstorming, we settled on an idea, which made the rest of the process easier. The individual stages of the RGM were then created by writing down all the ideas we had, then deciding the best twelve, based on availability of materials and difficulty. Part of the innovation process was also to draw and map out the whole project by diagrams and pictures to have a holistic view. This also allowed us to delegate roles and responsibilities to certain members of the group.
high.result: The result of the innovation stage is having decided the theme of the Rube Goldberg Machine, with each stage clearly decided and planned out. Each member had an active role and responsibility for the innovation of each stage.

high.situation: We needed to brainstorm a design mechanism through weeks 1-6 for the creation of the Rube Goldberg machine which involves dropping a Berocca tablet into water. The tablet falls out of its vessel and undergoes various complex situations and processes until it finally falls into water and dissolves.
high.task_action: We went through various brainstorming sessions throughout the weeks and came up with the Berocca tablet idea for the RGM. Our group came up with a problem statement for this idea, which was that the Berocca tablet cures headaches, releases energy, reduces tiredness, and helps support immunity, which would help a university student get through university. We went through the gallery method of brainstorming, wherein we all put forth our ideas and started building ideas on them, and finally came up with our final design for the RGM machine. We also spoke about various other ways we could have designed our machine but came up with the most creative and fun way in which we could complete our project, which included 3d printed pipes, marbles, cardboards, umbrella, folder, dominoes, track, lego truck, shovel, coins, cup, windmill, balance, Berocca track bearing, water bottle, a cup of water. After analyzing the weight, complexity, availability, manufacturability, etc we decided to go ahead with our final idea, based on all these factors.
high.result: The result was being able to select a design solution that included the Berocca tablet being dropped into water, and after undergoing various other solutions, we shortlisted and came up with our final design.

high.situation: For our ENGG1050 classes this session, we were divided into small groups of 5-6 members who were required to work together to build a Rube Goldberg Machine.
high.task_action: After our class started, we introduced ourselves. We talked about what we were studying and what we liked to do during our free time. During this time, we realised that we were very introverted and had difficulties opening up, which could harm building an effective team. After we became a little bit familiar with each other, we assigned roles for next week's meeting and set up a rotating roster. We made sure everyone understood their goals and then ended our meeting for that week.
high.result: The result was being able to communicate much more freely than we were initially able to do. I believe this helped us to create a more effective team capable of completing complex tasks.

high.situation: In week three of our workshop for ENGG1050, we as a group brainstormed of ways to conceptually design a Rube Goldberg Machine that would consist of a minimum of 10 action events with each action event lasting 3 seconds minimum.
high.task_action: We came up with two different, but flawed solutions to the problem presented before finally deciding upon a better alternative towards the end of our brainstorming session. The first flawed solution being to make a software that simulates the actions of the machine, but due to the complexity of creating such a software and questions as to whether it violates any of the constraints presented by the problem, the idea was abandoned. The second idea was for each of us to design two action events and that we would later physically compile those action events into one machine, but this idea was also flawed as one member wouldn’t be able to participate in the physical creation of the machine and would have to burden other group members with the responsibility of physically creating the individual’s design.
high.result: After researching some more, we came to the conclusion that each individual member would be responsible for creating and recording a video of a working version of our Rube Goldberg machine consisting of 2 action events and that the ending of each action event would be a transition from one group member’s video to the other member’s.  Through video editing, we would make it seem as one complete Rube Goldberg machine that at the time satisfies most of, if not all the constraints presented by the task.

high.situation: Now that I’ve started working, I’ve had to manage the increased load and try to find time to do assessments/study while still managing a healthy sleep routine.
high.task_action: Some methods I have utilised to help manage these increased loads is to create a task list with due dates. This way I’m not surprised when an assessment is due, and I need to rush to complete it. Furthermore, another method I employed was to complete tasks as early as possible and buffer assessments to allow ample time to make adjustments/work on other things and reduce stress as the due date approaches.
high.result: As a result of these precautions, I’ve been able to work 20+ hour weeks while studying and getting a healthy amount of sleep. The main method of improvement was creating a task list, this helped me to stay informed and on top of all my assessments.

high.situation: During the making of my stages in the RGM I discovered that working with dominoes was harder than I thought.
high.task_action: I began working on my sections; a spiral ramp on which a circular object would roll down and hit a piece of domino and start the chain reaction of falling dominoes. Initially I planned to make the domino chain loop 2 times around my spiral ramp. However very quickly I discovered while arranging the dominoes that when I made a mistake; perhaps my hand slipped or I shook the table I was working on too roughly or I didn't get the spacing right, the whole domino chain would fall and I would have to make it all from scratch again. I did this about 8 times and quickly realized the longer I made the domino chain the more fail points there were in the system and the more stuff I would have to rebuild when it failed.
high.result: I decided to make the domino chain only loop around my ramp 1 time which drastically reduced the amount of dominoes used and therefore the amount of failure points however it made it less impressive as well.

high.situation: We needed to design a Rube Goldberg Machine (RGM) as a team of six, with each section of the machine to be completed by one person. high.task_action: Each member of the team chose their own section of the RGM, in which we came up with our design individually, and built the section individually. high.result: The result was a somewhat choppy series of videos in which some sections of the RGM were vastly more reliable than others.

high.situation: Improving the reliability of my RGM action
high.task_action: I had to improve the reliability of my RGM's action from 70% to a higher amount. This was done cooperatively where as a group we brainstormed ideas for solutions to problems to our individual actions. My second action where I had an object(wood glue) swing on a string fail when it did not swing straight, i.e. in a curved fashion, or not at all. During my failure analysis, I identified the issue as the object having too many dimensions to move in as it could move forward and backwards which would cause the action to fail. To resolve this my team suggested I have the object swing against the wall to stop it from moving forwards and backwards.
high.result: Although this solution seems good, it resulted in lower overall reliability of 60%.  This initially seems counterintuitive as the problem of swinging back was solved, however, a new problem was added and the initial true problem which was not identified still existed. The new problem was that the object now "bounced" off the wall thus causing more of the curved motion to fail more tests. After further failure analysis, I identified the problem to be the way in which I initiated the action wherein I pushed the object off a ledge to begin the swing. When pushing I would sometimes push to the side causing a curved motion and the bounce off the walls.

high.situation: Leading up to week 7 we had been presenting every week in tutor/posse rooms to help further our presentation skills. high.task_action: To solve this issue, we had a group discussion where we came to the agreement that to improve our presentations, we should have more prioritisation towards rehearsingas a group to get a feel for timing and flow.
high.result: As a team, we have now come up with a schedule to complete the presentation well before needing to present so we can work on practising and perfecting it. This clearly being the best way to become better at presentations has given our group more confidence in the following week 13 presentation.

Once we did meet and attempted to construct the RGM, we were met with problems such as the ball bouncing too far off some events due to the solid wood tables we used for the foundation. Being adaptable and using improvisational solutions allowed us to save time in the design process but meant that nothing was lost in the actual design.

high.situation:
In my example, the experience recently took place at the Macquarie University within the context of a team/group project that is directed towards the Rube Goldberg Machine. This project was undertaken as part of an Engineering unit (ENGG1050 - Engineering Design). My team consisted of six team members, including myself. As a key part of the development of professional identity and personal development, us students were required to participate in weekly online workshop presentations. An effective presentation makes the best use of the relationship between the presenter and the audience. It takes full consideration of the audience’s needs in order to capture their interest, develop their understanding, inspire their confidence and achieve the presenter’s objectives. These presentations specifically involved a member from each group to present on weekly based objectives infront of other groups and an assigned tutor. In a nutshell, the weekly presentations were not only focused on the group's visual aspects, but also examined the oral presentation. 

TASK: Undergoing weekly presentations, for week's 7 presentation we were tasked with a team/group presentation consisting of 3 members from each group and restricted with the time constraint of 10 minutes per group. This presentation had to consist our group's complete detailed design and an in-depth discussion on how the individual scenes of our project led to a fulfilled Rube Goldberg Machine. As the name suggests, the task was to be completed through the use of several systematic diagrams to enable the audience to visually capture our group's RGM model. 

ACTION: Consequently, through the use of 'Miro' and our schedule plan a week before the presentation submission, we allocated 3 members of our group to conduct the presentation for week 7. As a team we assigned and distributed the work evenly so that we could work at a fast pace and also have fair contribution towards the project itself. This was by the means of having 3 presenters to create the visual presentation and an oral script. Whilst, the rest of the team members were tasked to achieve the weekly objective which was to construct the detailed design of our RGM model.
high.result:
The result of this situation was being able to present our group presentation and also meeting our weekly objective for week 7. In our viewpoint, we strongly believe that due to our presentations being commenced online, this gave us less room to engage and interact with the presentation overall. We are also of the opinion that a good presentation should be concise and should be focused on the topic. It should not move off-track. Essentially, this led up into the main context of the ‘effectiveness of oral presentations within remote learning’ and what makes the best use of the relationship between the presenter and the audience. We felt that it takes full consideration of the audience’s needs in order to capture their interest, develop their understanding from the early preface, inspire their confidence and achieve the presenter’s objectives. Subsequently, we learnt a lot about conducting effective presentations, such as, how to speak confidently in front of a small/large audience, control your voice projection, reflect on your body language and many more communication skills. As a group we had come to a similar ground of how face-to-face presentations can lead to a better and an effective presentation compared to online presentations.

We also felt that 'Miro' was a great opportunity and addition to make working a lot more streamlined. We felt like communication was one of the largest setbacks as most of us were used to face to face communication with many thinking that less interaction with each other made the work harder. The presentations were difficult and in some cases hard to gauge facial expressions and emotions while presenting on Zoom.

high.situation: I’ll be focusing on the planning of the building and recording of each member’s individual components for my first skill. high.result:
The use of a storyboard meant we knew what had to be done without wasting unnecessary time, hence efficient time management. Resource management connects by how we plan to connect our individual videos with methodical transitions. An example was including the same or similar materials in each video to show continuity, for example, we agreed to use a tennis ball from event 2 onto event 3. The formation of our storyboards aided us tremendously in order to showcase the salient object in operation within the recording. This will hopefully result in a comprehensive video which showcases a competent RGM presentation which demonstrates the effective use of time and resource management. 

(2) UNDERSTANDING NEEDS AND SETTING GOALS 

SITUATION: I'll be focusing on work distribution and how we overcome absent members for my second skill. My aim was to explicitly outline the expectations from each member and the work they must complete by a given deadline. This would ensure that we are all aware of each member’s assigned work, which includes what has been completed and what still needs to be completed.

TASK/ACTION: Communication is essential for the completion of our task so we can complete our targets effectively and on time. This will make us feel more confident with our workload and less stressed as we would each complete the designated individual work we planned on accomplishing. Effectively communicating expectations from each member ensures that we have a functional and well established understanding of our personal tasks we need to complete. The absence and negligence of completing work is one of the main issues concerning our project as we needed all members to contribute evenly. Communication was an excellent way to understand what needed to be completed and by what time. An example was the completion of the individual event recordings. We planned in advance when we would record each event as we understood that the videos were an important aspect of our project so we knew what we needed to improve on.

This means that explicitly outlining the expectations from each member and the work they must complete by a given deadline is an effective way of setting reasonable goals.

high.task_action:

In terms of resourcefulness, we came up with a concept that everyone should use similar materials for their own recordings/clips such as the same colour of dominos and marbles. We also taken into consideration of members with limited access to the material and whether they can afford it without any financial issues.

high.situation: During the reliability testing that each member of the team conducted for their respective actions, I came across an issue in one of my actions. When initially brainstorming our actions I needed something that would allow for an elevation change. I needed an action that could gain elevation from the floor up onto a wall using resources available to me. I tried to come up with something not many other groups would be able to do. Usually some sort of pulley system would be able to move from a low to high elevation but that's too common in my opinion. As effective and appealing that a pulley system may be I wanted a wow-factor to my actions in the RGM.
high.task_action: The easiest, unique thing I could think of to get the level change I needed was flying a drone. My brother has a drone which made it available to me and he would be able to fly it for one of my actions. But after reliability testing, although it actually was reliable enough to proceed with, it didn't look seamless and it would break up the flow of the RGM. This realisation made me have to come up with a new action and seamlessness and high tempo is a must in a RGM that retains the audience's attention and remains interesting.
high.result: Reliability testing occurs in week 9-10 which is towards the end of the semester. So one I decided to change the action even though I knew I would have less time to make a replacement action work. This is a clear example where being flexible and resourceful, as well as willing to persevere and have urgency comes into play, when almost 10 out of 12 weeks have gone by and pretty major changes need to be made for the benefit of the team. The replacement action has been decided – a long broom stick handle which will be held up straight, a toy car will roll and hit the broom stick which will then activate the next action on the wall. I'm using the height of the broom stick to gain the elevation onto the wall.

high.situation:
We needed to establish a system that provoked team members who were unreliable/unprincipled, ensuring all team members maintained an equal workload and maintained high standards throughout the assignment of building an RGM machine. 

Task: The greater task at hand is to build an RGM machine within the 12-week deadline. However, in this matter, the task at hand is to ensure all team members put in an equal amount of effort and maintain a steady/equal workload without individual members slacking on their part. This needed to be done to ensure individual team members were not forced upon a heavy workload and to ensure marks were not dropped through attendance and unsatisfactory completion from some members. 

Action: Subsequently, the action C12 took in order to tackle this potentially detrimental situation was to set up a multi-stage disciplinary system. This system included strikes in which a team member may have three strikes, each possessing a larger punishment. For example, if a team member were to miss one weekly meeting or scheduled class they would be issued with a warning. This warning remains between the group and is informal. However, if the offence is repeated, they may be issued with work required to be completed outside of the scheduled times or may have to do extra work in the following weeks. Lastly, if the offender has not taken any action on their mistakes they may be reported to the tutors, or handled with other punishment deemed suitable.
high.result: The results of this disciplinary program were deemed beneficial to the entire team. The program was successful at ensuring all team members participated in their duties equally and ensured all team members attended the meetings necessary. Through this program, C12 was able to complete all tasks and assignments to an above-satisfactory level and didn't need to rely on any unreliable group members. Each task was broken down equally and completed to a high level and was completed easily as it was distributed fairly.

Our group worked effectively and efficiently through forming and following roles within the team that rotated weekly. From the beginning we were tasked to take a DISC and OCEAN survey which produced results which show our own individual attributes, such as who was more of a leader and who was more of a supporter; who was more of a creative person or rather more logistic. With these results we were able to devise a rotation of roles throughout the weeks, these roles being;

The "Chair Person" - Who would take control of the meetings that week and would be in charge of keeping everyone on track, updated and on topic throughout meetings.

The "Minute Taker" - Takes notes and minutes of each pre-workshop and workshop meetings, and is also in charge of making the agenda for the upcoming week. Also responsible for writing but not creating the weeks reflection as the teams reflection is a group task, but the minute taker is in charge of typing out and emailing both the minutes and the reflection to the tutor.

The "Presenter" - Creates and presents the presentation based on the topic of that week.
high.result: By creating these roles and responsibilities, each person in the team was able to contribute effectively and evenly throughout the weeks as each person had a  chance to have their say. We as a team were able to perform our tasks mostly on time and have all contributed each our own thoughts and ideas effectively.

high.situation: I WAS LOOKING OVER THE EVENTS THAT WERE ASSIGNED TO ME FOR MY GROUP’S RGM AND I WAS TRYING TO BRAINSTORM DIFFERENT WAYS I COULD INCREASE THE RELIABILITY OF MY COMPONENTS.
high.task_action:
To give some context about my events, I will describe them. In the last inclined plane from the previous event, a ping pong ball goes down the ramp from event 5 and it knocks over some coffee beans, into an empty cup connecting to another pulley system. Once the empty cup is filled with coffee beans, it weighs down a string and lifts a glass bottle on the other end of the pulley system.  The end of the popsicle bomb has a few sticks that, if released, blow up the entire bomb. These sticks were stabilised by the glass bottle that was lifted off by the pulley system.

At first, the only change that came to mind was that the cup was too unstable and did not have a wide enough surface area to catch the coffee beans so, the cup was replaced with an open drawstring bag, which had a wider opening, and would potentially the coffee beans better.

However, when I tried to actually implement my events, I realised there were many flaws in the design. It was extremely difficult to get all the loose beans into the drawstring bag, and the glass bottle was not heavy/sturdy enough to keep the popsicle stick bomb in place without prematurely exploding. The ping pong ball was also too light to hit the coffee bag. Fortunately, by using my creativity and problem-solving skills, I was able to make use of other materials that were available around me to solve each and every problem I was facing and end the day with my final video.
high.result: I broke down the problems I was facing and tried to solve them one at a time. The first issue was the drawstring bag being too light, causing it to move about a lot from the position that would be ideal to catch the coffee beans. I looked around the Ada Lovelace room at 4RPD, and found a MQ goodie bag in the cupboard, which was way more stable and stayed in place. The coffee beans would spill everywhere, so to improve the repeatability and a higher chance of success, I decided to drop a bag of coffee rather than individual coffee beans. At times, the coffee bag would slide sideways down the cardboard ramp. To stabilise that, I taped a long wooden stick under the ramp, making it a lot more sturdy. I grabbed a big box from the cupboard and put it under the ramp, stabilising it further. The other issue was the glass bottle not being heavy enough, so I decided to fill it up with water. Those adjustments solved all the issues I was having with the design.

high.situation: Throughout the duration of our mid-semester break, we were tasked with building and testing the Rube Goldberg Machine components that each of us were responsible for designing.
high.task_action: The task is for each individual member of our group to build the Rube Goldberg machine components consisting of 2 action events, with each action event lasting a minimum of 3 seconds and that our components must be modularized, where the ending of each action event would be a transition from one group member’s video to the other member’s. It’s important that each member supply their own material and keep sustainability in mind, where the resources that are used are recyclable or items that we already have in our possession.
high.result: After finding out ways to use the resources that were readily available to me more efficiently and effectively, I was able to not only create my RGM components but I was also able to make sure that the action events for each RGM component lasted for at least 3 seconds and that the beginning of the action event I was responsible for would transition from one of my group member’s video and that the ending of my action event would transition to another member’s RGM component. The first action event created was for a marble to go down a pipe and the second action event would be triggered by that same marble, where the marble initiated a domino effect where multiple CD boxes would topple over each other and the last CD box would trigger another marble to fall down which would transition to another rube Goldberg machine component created by another team member.

high.situation:
I was working in woolworths and i have to check if the dates tag are out of date and then remove them if required.

Task: task was to critically analyze the faults and implement solutions 

Action: To address the situation, I first gathered all relevant information and data on the stocks previouse dates and the stock , I then identified the potential risks and act on removing the old stock

Using this information, I applied critical thinking skills to evaluate the feasibility and potential return on investment of the opportunity. I also consulted with subject matter experts and conducted additional research to ensure a thorough analysis.

Finally, I presented my findings and recommendation to my superiors, providing a detailed explanation of my thought process and the rationale behind my decision.
high.result: As a result of my critical thinking and analysis, i was able to save customers from buying outdated stocks and save the company from finical liability.

high.situation:
The situation that I was faced with was to produce a toolbox with given restraints on size and material. During the final assembly stages, I made a few minor errors during the measuring and bending of the material, resulting in the misalignment of the rivets.

TASK/ ACTION: Since the original placement of each panel wasn't lining up correctly, I needed to take the individual pieces and test all possible variations of interconnecting them. I managed to line up most of the rivet holes and for the remaining misalignments, I re-drilled and riveted the remaining fixture points.
high.result: The result was finally locking the individual pieces together and having enough structural integrity to both hold itself together and any tools that also could fit within.

high.situation:
When I worked as a marketing coordinator in the past, we were entrusted with creating a new ad campaign for a product that wasn't selling well. Our group had to devise a plan that would improve the product's exposure and appeal to the intended market.

Task: As a team member, it was my responsibility to assess the present advertising approach and generate fresh concepts to solve the product's drawbacks. 
To create a new advertising campaign that would be successful, I needed to utilise critical thinking to study market trends, rival strategies, and customer preferences.

Action:

I started by doing some market and product research. 
I researched the target market's preferences as well as the sector's most recent advertising trends. 
Also, I examined the current advertising strategy and pinpointed its flaws. 
I suggested a new advertising strategy based on my study that would highlight the product's special qualities and advantages. 
I presented my notion to the group, got their input, and then adjusted my plan.
high.result: Throughout the first few months of the new ad campaign's introduction, we observed a large boost in product sales. The target audience responded well to the campaign, and we had good feedback from clients and business leaders. I was able to analyse the situation objectively, assess the information at hand, and come up with an efficient solution because to the critical thinking method I used. I was able to help the team succeed in attaining our goals as a consequence.

high.situation: We needed to create a functional and attainable schedule for all tradesmen and house members during our home renovation process.
high.task_action: We assessed everyones strengths and weaknesses individually and then proceeded to categorise them in order of significance. This order had many components such as how long (in terms of time) a specific project would take, how many people are required to complete a task and which elements need to be completed in order for another element to be added on top e.g. electrician before gyprocking and painting.
high.result: We successfully designed a plan through effective communication within the team and helping one another during the process.

high.situation: The goal was to build a simple tool box out of sheet aluminium and rivets within 7 weeks.
high.task_action: The extent of my time and resource management was my efficient and well thought-out approach of just doing everything as quickly as possible. Using this method, I avoided the lines for machines that formed because everyone had reached that step at once. Fortunately, I'm ok at measuring, cutting, and sanding, so my project did not appear to be a rushed job.
high.result: The result was a toolbox finished before the deadline, without the need to put in extra hours outside of designated class time.

high.situation:
During the Arduino Circuit Building skill for ENGG1000, we were required to complete weekly coding activities / exercises on an Arduino Uno R3 microcontroller board using either Tinkercad or Arduino IDE. Each week we were introduced to new functions of the Arduino board that the coding exercises would require us to understand and implement, with the final and most extensive programming assessment in Week 6 requiring significant competency in previous features.

 

TASK / ACTION: Carefully thinking about the requirements of each activity and using coding knowledge gained from COMP1000, I was able to able to break down each exercise into two parts: the physical wiring of the board and the coding for each component that would allow the circuit to achieve the desired output.

Since for each weekly set of exercises we had a time limit of 3 hours (the duration of the workshop session) it was important for me to determine the time I should allocate to each activity in order to maximise the number of marks I would receive every week.

Furthermore, I also utilised the internet as a tool to perceive how other users of Arduino went about solving similar problems, I then incorporated this new circuitry and coding knowledge to current and future exercises to better understand them as I approached them.
high.result:
Approaching each exercise in this manner allowed me to determine how each element of the circuit would need to be built and coded, as well as how they would need to be connected together so that they could operate simultaneously in order to achieve the requirement specified in the worksheet.

The end result of the skill was being able to complete all the coding exercises set each week, including the final assignment, bar one of the earlier exercises in which the code didn’t seem to be working properly, preventing me from fully completing it. However, the overall success of this skill was due to critically thinking about the logic of the circuit and code when approaching each exercise.
