high.situation: We needed to create a model of student accommodation that met a specific design criteria that was provided by the client.
high.task_action: We formed a small group of 5 people and each member of the group created a concept model of what was going to be included on each level of the design and what the model would look like. From this point, we chose the design that we thought would be best aligned with the design criteria and have a complex exterior shape rather than a simple rectangle.
high.result: The result was prototyping a model that was refined from the 5 concept models. The tasks required to complete this task were divided between the members of the group.

high.situation:
In my Technical Drawing class, which spanned from week 1 to 7, we were taught the essential fundamentals such as "Perspective Drawing", drawing "Oblique and Isometric shapes", "Multiview Drawing", and "Sectioning and Dimensioning".

Task: One of the most significant challenges I faced in the class was the final task. We were tested on our ability to implement everything we learned in the previous weeks to demonstrate effective communication of engineering objects via a range of drawing approaches. We were also required to demonstrate conceptual design via standard engineering drawing techniques and produce Engineering standard manufacturing drawing for machining.

Action: To tackle this task, I took the initiative and was willing to make decisions in the face of uncertainty. I started by reviewing my notes and exercises from the previous weeks and identified the most critical concepts and techniques required for the task. I then developed a plan of action that included a timeline, milestones, and checkpoints to ensure I stayed on track. In the face of uncertainty whilst completing the final task, such as not knowing what angle to draw on or how to draw a specific angle. I practiced drawing different objects and shapes, incorporating various techniques to improve my accuracy and speed.
high.result: By taking the initiative and being willing to make decisions in the face of uncertainty, I was able to tackle the final task with confidence and ease. I completed the task on time, and my drawings demonstrated effective communication of engineering objects via a range of drawing approaches. I was also able to demonstrate conceptual design via standard engineering drawing techniques and produce Engineering standard manufacturing drawing for machining. This experience taught me the importance of being proactive, taking ownership of my work, and being able to make decisions in uncertain situations, which will aid me in my future career as an engineer.

high.situation: I needed to design a 3D model of a house with floorplans and 3D renders which match the 3D model precisely for a concept of a house accommodating the disabled and elderly.
high.task_action: Approaching the need for accurate design and the need for exceptional quality, I underwent a brainstorming process in which many ideas were formed over multiple days. Within those days I created lists then sketches based on the consumer’s needs, for example, large living areas and easy accessible kitchen benches for the disabled and elderly. At the end of the process a design was chosen. However, a suitable program was needed, and research was done on that. In the end a program named ArchiCAD was chosen as it fulfilled 2 of the 3 (3D rendering and floor planning) needs for the design process. As such the 3D renders and floor plans created scale for the model and allowed me to go into further detail for the physical model provided.
high.result: The resulting product was of a high standard of quality and provided the consumer to fully understand the design and the eventual look and feel of the home which they were considering building. The brainstorming process and research processes made the creation and application of skills to go a lot smoother than if decisions were made on the spot as the model was being built.

high.situation:
In my former employer, there was a lack of product(bags) innovation in the industry. The firm was finding it difficult to generate novel, inventive items that could compete in the market despite making significant investments in research and development. 

 Task: I had to determine the fundamental causes of the lack of innovation and provide strategies to deal with the issue. As a member of a group entrusted with creating a new product(bags), I realised that our team's creativity was being constrained by the fact that we were all tackling the issue in the same manner.

 Action: I recommended that we attack the issue using design thinking. The crew was split up into smaller groups, and we utilised the design thinking method to come up with fresh concepts. To gain their opinions and feedback on our concepts, we also included customers in the process.
high.result: Thanks to this, we were able to create a fresh, cutting-edge product(bags) that people loved. For the creation of next items, the firm also followed the design thinking methodology, which resulted in more creative products and higher sales.

high.situation: During the software design major project about creating a rock paper scissors Lizard Spock game. To earn the marks for extension 2 we needed to create a whole new algorithm to replace the if-else statements we began with, to allow easier maintenance for the determine winner function of the game.
high.task_action:
To achieve the goal, I first analysed the RPS decision diagram included with the report requirements. Acquiring that basis of understanding, I identified the requirements of the task. Afterwards, I  researched for more information regarding potential replacements for the if statements. With multiple different potential replacements in hand.

I created and ran the code in Replit, analysed their effectiveness and paired my understanding of how they work, I determined whether it fits within the task requirements. After a couple of runs, I finally landed on one that I can replicate and edit to my own needs in C, to which I checked with the tutors if it was acceptable and if its on task.
high.result: I have utilised the RPS decision diagram win/lose algorithm to determine the winner through a switch and case function after multiple revisions on the code, accomplishing the goal of reviving a new algorithm for the function.

high.situation: In software Design logic, our Major project is to create an RPS game with additional gestures. To earn full marks for the project, we are required to complete extension two, to create a whole new algorithm to replace the old if-else statements as it takes too much space and is to determine the output.
high.task_action: My initial design was just a simplified variant of the old code, still exclusively made out of if and else statements. Unsatisfied with the current work result. I went back to the project requirements and closely observe the tournament diagram of RPS 7. With that understanding, I  researched for different codes used instead of the if-else statements. With diverse methods to accomplish the task such as arrays or lists, I analysed their feasibility and my ability to implement them. Afterwards, I formed a new algorithm determining the win with switch and case. After completing a draft variant, I confirmed with the tutors if the code satisfies the extension two requirements.
high.result: I was able to successfully implement the new code into the project, replacing the old if and else statements, and satisfying the requirements for extension 2. Later, I revised the algorithm again to fit RPS 5.

high.situation: I struggled to manage several activities and projects during a stressful work week, while simultaneously attending several meetings and responding to urgent demands from coworkers and clients.
high.task_action: I put time and resource management principles into practice to help me prioritise my work and use my time more effectively. high.result: My activities caused the project's progress to significantly improve, and the project was finished on schedule as a consequence. high.situation:
As part of succeeding in university, we are required to effectively allocate our time, and our personal resources (energy, motivation, focus and sometimes money) to address the needs and demands of each course. However, there are numerous areas of life outside of university that further require us to dedicate our time, and personal resources.

For me personally, the distinct areas/categories of life that require the allocation of time, energy, motivation, focus, and money are:

·       University

·       Personal

·       Social

·       Family

·       Health

·       Religious

·       Workplace

Hence my objective (at the start of the semester) was to:

Achieve a balance across all these priority areas ensuring that no category suffers due to an overcommitment of time and personal resources to another.

It is important to note, that balance does not mean all categories are given the same amount of time, energy, motivation, focus and money. The position of balance is determined by the demands of each category, as well as the relative perceived importance of each category.

¨
high.task_action:
To adequately address the objective outlined in _Situation_, it is necessary to fully understand the specific requirements of each category. Hence, I expanded the list of categories presented in _Situation_ to include specific sub-categories for each area. The resulting list looked somewhat like the one presented below:

 

·       University courses

o   COMP1000

o   ENGG1000

o   MATH1010

o   PHYS1510

·       Personal

o   Hobbies

o   Interests

·       Social

o   Catchups

o   Social outings

·       Family

o   Family time

·       Health

o   Exercise

o   Nutrition

·       Religious

o   Church commitments

·       Workplace

o   Shifts

 

Note that the list above contains only a general example/s for each category. Very specific sub-categories were either left out or combined into a general sub-category to protect personal information.

Based on the demands of each subcategory as well the relative importance I placed on each category, I constructed a weekly timetable at the start of the semester, theoretically allowing me to achieve a balance in terms of the time, energy, motivation, focus and money that I dedicate to each category and sub-category.

 

RESULT

In the first half of this semester, the objective: “[To] achieve a balance across all these priority areas ensuring that no category suffers due to an overcommitment of time and personal resources”, was achieved with mixed results.

Over this past term, I dedicated significantly greater amounts of time and personal resources to the _university_ category than was timetabled for, while simultaneously disregarding other categories such as _personal_ and _health_ to a moderate extent. Hence, an imbalance occurred in terms of how time and energy, motivation, focus, and money were expended. However, some improvement in terms of time and personal resource expenditure was seen compared to High School, where an even greater imbalance prevailed.

Consequently, it would appear, that the timetable has proven to be a useful resource for planning how time and personal resources should be devoted to different categories – as an improvement in balance between different areas of life were observed when compared to high school. However, as an imbalance was still prevalent, additional solutions are needed to help myself achieve greater balance.

high.situation:
In my Rock Paper Scissors code, I found an occurrence where the player choice value and computer value would be within the 'valid' range for a round, but still wouldn't be identified as a valid round even though it was supposed to be valid.

Task:

Identify the origin of the coding error and adjust the code to make sure the values can be identified as valid inputs for a valid round.

I thought that if the incorrect 'if' statement was running, that means that the values must have been true for the if statement, therefore if I evaluate the code backwards from the origin of the incorrect lines of code that it ran, i might find a possibility for an error.

Action:

It turns out that the computer's value was at fault, originally, the program would recognise the 'round' as a tie, I had written this a while loop to fix the value while it was still an invalid input.

However, the criteria for the if statement would first identify if the value was invalid, randomise the computer's value, and then categorise it into either a tie or an outside-of-range value (with the outside-of-range value being 'else'). 

In the weird occurrence I mentioned earlier, the outcome would have been a tie, meaning the program would run the invalid if statement, randomise the computer's value to change the outcome into a "non-tie" round and state it fulfilled the 'else' criteria meaning it was an "out of range" value, despite both values being valid

I changed the code to randomise the computer's value AFTER recognising the round is a tie or out of range, that way the values would be identified accurately.
high.result: The new piece of code would never input an incorrect process for a correct value after the changes made to it.

high.situation:
I was working as a math tutor/senior staff for a nearby tutoring facility (MATRIX, education), and because of unanticipated events, we were shorthanded for a specific day due to several tutors unable to attend due to personal reasons. We also had a lot of students coming in for their booked tutoring appointments at different times.

T - TASK: 

Irrespective of staff shortfall, I had the responsibility of ensuring that all students who had appointments set for that day continued to get high-quality tutoring sessions and also to create tutoring plans for all students whose tutors could not attend therefore had to look at this issue with multiple perspectives as each student was at different stages of their learning abilities and required different forms of teaching to absorb information, amongst also keeping communication clear with students and the tutors available about what was going to happen throughout the session.

A - ACTION:

 To address the issue, I looked at the list of students who were coming in for the session and how many of their tutors were absent before evaluating the available resources, including the textbooks, online resources, and other tutors who were present alongside those who were nearby. I was able to assess the situation after receiving all the information and formulate a plan. I immediately identified the students who needed the most help because of upcoming tests or academic instability, and I gave those students' sessions top priority by assigning the most knowledgeable tutors to them. After this, I acquired the remainder of the tutors' extra students to compensate for the ones that couldn’t make it. This required moving tutors between different students and keeping them informed about the student's abilities and what they must concentrate on. For the remaining students, I compiled a list of resources and practice tasks they could use independently to sharpen their Math skills and feel more competent.

R -
high.result: Despite unexpected staffing shortages, I was able to ensure that every student received the help they needed by using my critical thinking skills to assess the problem and develop practical solutions centered on the needs of both students and tutors. I was able to view each of these difficulties from the standpoint of multiple potential solutions to the issue because of critical thinking. Students who worked with knowledgeable tutors saw improvements and value in their sessions, and those who practiced on their own also felt more confident in their capabilities and had time to catch up or improve topics that they felt unsure about or weak on.

To teach this skill my students needed to learn how to find time by finding the quarter notes in a track and continuing to listen while playing in order to keep in time. My struggle was teaching my students how to find time.

Task: 

My task was to find a method that would help my students learn how to find time in a track by listening for quarter notes. 

Action:

I first started with teaching them to play along with a metronome, when this didn't work I found that using a visual metronome would help my students to hear and visualise the quarter notes. From here I learned that if I put a visual metronome in time with a track the students could hear the quarter notes more clearly and also see them visually helping them to learn how to play in time.
high.result: My students now have the ability to listen to a track and hear the quarter notes which is a required skill to be able to play in time with the track.

Action:

An example of my slowly and steady process would be first free-balling the dimensions of objects, generalizing the length, width and height through visual cues, but with planned and spaced sessions, I was slowly introduced dimensions, allowing room for error by using bigger units and number intervals such as 'centimeters' and 'tenths' . After I got comfortable and confident in applying dimensions in one specific method of drawing. With the time I spent slowly perfected dimension on specific techniques/method of drawing (One point-perspective), it allowed me to easily transfer the key concepts and methods of dimension to other more challenging techniques such as multiview and isometric. I naturally began using exact values and smaller measurements, to create more compact and detailed drawings, which further enhanced my drawings.
high.result: With the strategic planning/method and focused time spent learning the dimensioning, I was able to transfer this skill and apply it to all my drawings and the methods of drawing, thus enabling me to further create more accurate and conventional drawings.

high.situation:
A mistake that had occurred during the scribing phase of my toolbox involved the misinterpretation of the toolbox measurements which resulted in both my end pieces having the top inside point of the fold connected to the bottom outside point of the fold (pictured below). I had also incorrectly scribed the location of the side punctures that are used to rivet the end and front pieces together - leaving a large hole in the side of my toolbox. As a result of my end pieces being incorrectly scribed, they were folded in on themselves with the bottom edge sticking out from the front pieces. I attempted to recover the pieces through reversing the folds and scribing and folding in accordance with the correct measurements. I was successful in reversing the folds, however the repeated folding had worn the aluminium, resulting in the folds snapping off. 

 

Task:

As both my end pieces were now unable to unable to be used, I now had to restart my end pieces from scratch. This took place on Tuesday, week 6 – a week before the entire toolbox was due. I had notified my workshop leads to which both expressed concern regarding time to complete as I still had to begin riveting and putting the rest of my toolbox together.

Action:

Due to the end pieces being the smallest pieces of the toolbox, I was able to successfully acquire scraps of aluminium from the scrap boxes and restart my end pieces without needing to cut an entirely new sheet of aluminium. I was also familiar with the process of creating the end pieces which helped with the speed to creating my new pieces.
high.result: From when I restarted my end pieces (about 40 minutes into the workshop), until the end of the workshop, I was able to complete the end pieces to a higher standard than all my previous pieces, begun the riveting process, and was a lot happier with the progress of my toolbox. I only had to attend one catchup session outside of workshop hours (for the time equivalent of redoing my end pieces) and I had completed my project.

high.situation: I needed to build a software which will be able to play different gestures of rock paper scissor. There will be extended version of rock paper scissor like it can play 7,9,11 gesture one.
high.task_action: I divided our project into small parts and then decided to marge them together in one big program. I did 7,9 and 11 gesture in seperate code and then i marge them together in one program. But the problem was with comparing all the gesture with one another to determine who is the winner. Because for 7 gesture there is 49 possible combination and for 11 gesture there is 121 possible combination. It was so hard to do all this code because it was taking ages to make all the line to work. So i came up with an idea of using math calculation to make the code smaller and efficient. I divided the game into three small segments and compare them with each other using a mathematical equation.
high.result: It was taking way too long time and there was so much error in the code and finding out the error was hard and time consuming as well. But after implementing the new approach it was easy and there was no errors as well.

high.situation: I had to create an algorithm for a determine winner function in a game of rock paper scissors.
high.task_action: At first this was done using multiple if-statements. After critically thinking on how it could be better and simplified so that the program would run using less memory, I was able figure out a solution which also answered an extension question.
high.result: The result was a math equation that would work for multiple gestures, if there was more added and reduced the number of if-statements from before.

The result was being able to order the gestures based on these parameters, so that a winner could be calculated using modular arithmetic.

For example:

Order:

	* Rock = 0
	* Paper = 1
	* Scissors = 2
	* Spock = 3
	* Lizard = 4

Assuming that:

	* N = number of gestures
	* A = player 1 choice
	* B = player 2 choice

The following algorithm was born: (A - B + N) % N:

	* A result of 0 would determine the outcome as a tie;
	* An odd number would determine the victory of Player 1;
	* An even number would determine the victory of Player 2.

The result was being able to order the gestures based on these parameters, so that a winner could be calculated using modular arithmetic.

For example:

Order:

	* Rock = 0
	* Paper = 1
	* Scissors = 2
	* Spock = 3
	* Lizard = 4

Assuming that:

	* N = number of gestures
	* A = player 1 choice
	* B = player 2 choice

The following algorithm was born: (A - B + N) % N:

	* A result of 0 would determine the outcome as a tie;
	* An odd number would determine the victory of Player 1;
	* An even number would determine the victory of Player 2.

The result was being able to order the gestures based on these parameters, so that a winner could be calculated using modular arithmetic.

For example:

Order:

	* Rock = 0
	* Paper = 1
	* Scissors = 2
	* Spock = 3
	* Lizard = 4

Assuming that:

	* N = number of gestures
	* A = player 1 choice
	* B = player 2 choice

The following algorithm was born: (A - B + N) % N:

	* A result of 0 would determine the outcome as a tie;
	* An odd number would determine the victory of Player 1;
	* An even number would determine the victory of Player 2.

high.situation: We were tasked with completing a variety of basic coding and circuitry problems with an Arduino Uno board and its corresponding integrated development environment. Complementary to this, we were also asked to create a LinkedIn page and talk to our surrounding peers and to expand our network.
high.task_action:
Everyone had access to a pdf file containing the problems and a supplementary guide on basic nomenclature of the key parts of the Arduino board and tools we were to use. Prior to completing the task we briefly set up a LinkedIn account and shared accounts with those around us. 

The tasks itself required us to:

	* _Use 4 LEDs to build a circuit which blinked one LED at a time consecutively_

	* _Use 10 LEDs to build a circuit that counted through each one like numbers, counting first evenly and then oddly and then in prime order_
	* _Use 4 LEDs to create a circuit that will count to 15 in binary_

We had a total of three hours to complete these tasks, with each task acting as a precedent for the knowledge required to complete the next. 

As there were varying degrees of knowledge with the Arduino board and for coding in general there was a lot of discourse amongst the class to try and work through the problems.
high.result: The result left everyone with a working circuit board tuned to solve its corresponding problem which was then marked off by the tutor and critiqued.

high.situation: For my PACE work experience I completed last year, I was attending regular meetings for CSIRO where I present the progress of my work and would open the room to feedback and recommendations.
high.task_action: With our mentor, we had arranged to spend some working time at the CSIRO Marsfield site throughout the two weeks along with these online meetings/presentations. I would be provided a wealth of feedback in the meetings and I would need to know how to navigate this feedback and separate my professional work from my personal feelings.
high.result: As I was introducing myself and our project, I was asked a stern direct question as to the actual purpose of my particular project and how if it serves a purpose for CSIRO.

high.situation:
Team work within the work environment was not performing at its best and some sort of communication barrier is occurring within the team.

Task/ Action:

We need to find the solution on how to train the new team in the fastest approach so that they are efficient and productive as they start to work with the effective team work. Also everyone engagement should be needed for all the team to be on the same page which is viable by the proper communication only.

The first approach is having the team meeting where everyone’s idea about the work happening is gathered. The working standards, work rules, what is expected from everyone, guidelines is being taught and educated. Any queries and issues are collected and written down to look upon and how we are handling the situations as we progressed. all the teams are paired up when working and informed about the task allocation and what is the expected time frame for the completion. Team are provided the further training once the task is completed and given feedback on the work as well. All the team on board are given the P.A for communication so that everyone can hear and understand what is happening at the point of time within the selected work area and they can communicate and ask or seek guidance from the leader if any help is needed.
high.result: The outcome is good. There is always a space where we can do something better. The team is working with the pace where everyone understand what they are doing and how the work is being carried out. Communication plays the important role with P.A where team can ask the question or direct anything from any point, which was very effective and productive to talk to any team onboard. Team are provided the feed back on how they performed and also motivated with constructive feedback if some one needed.

high.situation:
While building my toolbox, I encountered a problem with the side panels being incorrectly measured and cut, which made it impossible to fold them correctly.

Task: My task was to find a solution to the problem to ensure that the side panels fit correctly and securely into the toolbox.

Action: I used critical thinking to identify possible solutions to the problem. My first idea was to fold the side panels with a small fold of about three millimetres on each side. However, this solution presented the problem of the side panels appearing thinner than they should be. Then, I evaluated a second idea of using scrap metal to recut the side panels, but this solution would take more time. Finally, I realized that I could measure and cut one side piece perfectly and use it as a stencil for the second piece, saving me time and ensuring that both side pieces fit correctly into the toolbox.
high.result: My actions resulted in two perfectly cut side pieces that fit correctly and securely into my toolbox without requiring any further alterations. This experience required me to use critical thinking skills to identify and evaluate different solutions to the problem, weighing the pros and cons of each option. It also required me to think creatively about how to make the most of the resources I had available to find the best solution. Overall, this experience helped me develop my critical thinking skills and problem-solving abilities, which I can apply in future engineering situations.

high.task_action: Before beginning the construction of the toolbox, we carefully analysed the process and determined which step will hinder the next. We then planned which task will be the easiest along with the lowest queue times for that certain machine to maximise our efficiency.
high.result: We then were able to effectively construct the toolbox with minor hiccups, there was only a few mistakes in your step planning and were able to rectify it when it occurred.

high.situation:
After a lot of group work many tasks had been completed, many members of the group became rationally ignorant but did not ask for help. Workflow was being organised on a Trello Kanban board using cards.

 

TASK: The final stage of the civil modelling project involved documenting all work and assembling the concept model.

 

ACTION: Tasks were assigned by members to other members. I noticed that there was duplication in tasks with some tasks having already been completed. I acted to refocus the group activities and recommended members review the completed section on Trello.
high.result: Group members who were not highly engaged found it difficult to locate information on the Trello even though it was clearly labelled. I have concluded that for projects with Asymmetric member engagement of sufficient scale completed work must be migrated off the workflow space and into a documentation space such as confluence.

high.situation: during the ENG1000 subject I made new colleagues in my workshop who came from many backgrounds including different cultures, socio-economic backgrounds, ethnicities, religious beliefs, sexual orientations, ages and genders. We engaged in problem solving and learning together and got together after class to study and revise the material that we learnt in the workshops as well as engage in social activity such as getting lunch together and getting to know one another.
high.task_action: During our workshops we engaged in collaborative learning together. Particularly we had to form into a group and design a software flow diagram for a more complicated program that we had to develop. I made sure to respect everyone’s recommendation and contribution. I noticed that one of the colleagues is a female and she was a bit quiet and shy to communicate in front of more loud and opinionated males in the group. I made sure to give her time and space to communicate her ideas clearly in a safe and inclusive environment.
high.result: The diversity of having a female insight into the project was invaluable. The colleague who was a female gave very good contributions which improved the flow diagram and made it more efficient. This resulted in a more enriching experience for all group members in the learning process.

high.situation: With the creation of a Rude Goldberg Machine (RGM), we needed a holistic approach to encompass and recognise what different factors of our day to day lives we needed to prioritise, these factors included what available resources we had, our intended consumers and the environment. A holistic approach is the understanding of the design through various lenses as well as understanding the aforementioned factors whilst also factoring in unique challenges and constraints that each element presents.
high.task_action:
Through holistic thinking our primary task was to create an environment that was engaging, memorable and educational through various practices such as brainstorming sessions in nature not confined to a room or auditorium. To achieve the best product we can deliver we evaluated each component’s failure or success and how it might impact overall morale or the function of the RGM. Additionally, the holistic approach we sought to adapt brought the very best of each student identifying strengths and weaknesses. The elimination of such weaknesses culminated in one uniform body.  Additionally, we adapted meditation at the start of each meeting with a heavy emphasis on outdoor learning soaking up all the vitamin D we need to enhance cognitive ability.

 

The result was an RGM that was environmentally sustainable encompassing an innate love humans have for their pets. The holistic approach provided a metric for future problem solving, fostering continuous growth and improvement.

high.situation: Throughout my university experience I have had to learn how to take accountability for myself. During the Engg1050 RGM project, I have had to put my skills to the test and continuously refine them to ensure the success of both myself and my team members, with whom I have been actively collaborating.
high.task_action:
I first started my academic career at UTS, where I studied for 2 years before I took a semester off due to an ADHD diagnosis. During my time at UTS, I had the opportunity to partake in four group projects, each with its unique set of group dynamics. It was during these projects that I initially began to develop my sense of accountability. As a student with ADHD, I face ongoing challenges in holding myself accountable, particularly with respect to tasks that require time management. This is the first group project I have been in since beginning my ADHD treatment and I have resolved to maintain my organisation and continue to enhance my sense of accountability.

Since the initial workshop I have aimed to be a valuable member for my team. I took proactive steps, making a note of my team members names and reviewing their LinkedIn profiles to familiarise myself with their faces prior to meeting them. I took up position of team leader temporarily, ensuring that each member introduced themselves and that we had a platform to communicate outside of the scheduled workshop meetings. In preparation for our group sessions, I had established our Miro board and familiarised myself with the software in the event that none of my team members had done so. As it turned out, this was the case, and I took it upon myself to guide the team through the features of the platform and how to access our group board.

Assuming the role of team leader, I was not only responsible for my own accountability, but also for the successful presentation of our team. I recognised the criticality of leadership and organisation for effective teamwork, and I committed to ensuring a seamless semester for our group. As the project progressed, I continued to fulfill the duties of team manager, even though I did not actively seek this role. However, due to the needs of the team, I remained in this position to ensure our collective success.

Taking on the responsibility of organising a team in a group project requires the utmost accountability. Any oversights or failures on my part can potentially harm the team's progress and success. Additionally, it also means that I must hold others accountable for their responsibilities within the project, which can be challenging as a student, since I do not hold any formal authority. If I fail in this task, the completion of the work often falls to me.

Throughout the project, I have consistently assumed responsibility not only for my own tasks but also for encouraging effective teamwork and communication. While I acknowledge that there is always room for improvement, I am committed to further developing my professional skills and enhancing my ability to take accountability for myself and future teams.
high.result: By taking accountability for my work and responsibilities I have successfully coordinated with my team, and we have managed to produce a high quality RGM design.

high.situation: The group needed to establish a proper rotation of roles and responsibilities within the group, specifically the chairperson.
high.task_action: i was relegated to being chairperson on a whim, which then lead to decisions made on my own with minimal input from the rest of the group and made me worry that i was not making the right choices for everyone in the group. We evaluated weeks prior of who would be a good chairperson only to have meetings fall through, people not show up, and lack of communication. When i took over as the chairperson, i had to take the initiative to implement what i wanted to implement for the project as a total.
high.result: I’ve since become the chairperson directing the group and developing an agenda for everyone in the project, while also assigning what needs to be completed and when it would need to be completed.

high.situation:
As a first-year undergraduate student at Macquarie University, it’s been 6 weeks since I started grasping skills for unit ENGG1050 Engineering Design. So far, the class has been put into groups to implement a Rube Goldberg Machine for a project concerning 12 key events of 30 seconds duration. My experience with Team A12 has been nothing but exemplary since the weekly presentation and professional documentation of the meeting's agenda and minutes require a constant rotation of roles in the Team Charter. Looking back, I have had to take up the role of Minute-Taker twice and I’m not complaining since it’s a given that tasks are assigned based on the group member’s availability. However, when accepting a role, it is very important to get things done on time. The tutor-in-charge for our team has advised that it is recommended to turn in assessments before Tuesday night. Although records till week 2 show due dates having gone past Friday, it is a student’s responsibility to be aware of the content details provided in the unit guideline. I observed that procrastination is a problem that I am faced with and it took a hit on team morale.

The matter at hand became worse when the Minute Taker for week 5 didn’t come through on their end. Absences were noted and hence there was nothing much to report for said person. For ENGG1050 Workshop, I had to take the initiative to do a presentation for the same week since the team member responsible for it had personal matters to attend to. Although a failed attempt, it also gave me a chance to learn that prepared speeches go a long way. The technical difficulties I faced during that time made me a bad candidate for the Presenter role.
high.task_action:
Procrastination is a very bad habit for group assessments as one member's contribution could help to complete the given role of others. Back in year 10 high school, there was a task based on the recent Biology zoo excursion the class had to submit in writing. A group of three consisted of a Note-taker, Report-writer and Key-note Presenter. At the time, I was assigned to write the report but both of my friends were busy cramming for the upcoming Business Studies Exam. In the end, I had to stay up all night to prepare a PowerPoint Presentation as well. 

I want to avoid putting one of my team members in that kind of tough spot. Having to stay up all night is not a nice experience. So, it was decided that the Chairman role for Team A12 will also have the burden of supervising the shared Google Docs and Slides. To be targeted with deadline notifications in the group chat is rather motivational in its embarrassing way. The issue of absence led to the idea of choosing a Sub-Presenter for the Workshop Zoom Meetings. An alternate option of one person doing the slides while the sub-in prepares a speech has concurred. In case of an emergency, minutes-taking will be divided among all five members. 

Although not disclosed in Team Charter, a position for Group Leader is elected every week on a rotational basis. The person is then asked to update the Agenda Status while directing topics for future meetings. If a situation were to arise, even if inconvenient, they should be willing to take up the challenge.
high.result:
For week 6, I noticed that Team A12 has had over three meetings outside of the Workshop hours. All meetings were initiated by the Group Leader while she conveyed that Preliminary Design Diagrams for the RGM events be posted up on the Google Slides created. The seriousness of task completion was highlighted when she warned that negligence will mean that the person in question will have his/her event ideas modified in the later stages. I was surprised to see that in response, even the least active member had started to reply in group chat discussions after 6 pm. This could be a result of our Group Leader’s decision or could be due to the marks weighed on the Week 7 Presentation. 

Although I’m not selected to be one of three Presenters for the upcoming week, a request has been made to share everyone’s speech on Google Docs. I’ve learnt my lesson from experience, it’s better to have something to blabber on than to space out in front of the whole class. I will be ready to fill in for a member if things don’t go as planned.

high.situation: On week 4, many team members were incapable of attending the designated (not sgta) weekly meeting on Wednesday. This, with also a rushed emergency for one of our members not being able to present for the upcoming sgta resulted in a lacking team presentation at the workshop. The lack of structure in our communication was quickly identified and put as high importance for the upcoming week and further.
high.task_action: The lack of communication was identified and immediately put into discussion within the group, pinning the problem to not very secure weekly meeting schedule and a lacking social media platform to communicate through. Discussions within the workshop would result in the choice of moving the weekly meetings to a much more manageable time of Friday 6pm, allowing for easier access for all members. Our communication method only consisted of WhatsApp which we as a group identified to be quite lacking especially in the crunch phase of the project, so we created a discord group to also suffice, allowing for easy communication through many methods. This allowed a simpler method of creating our own weekly meetings as well, streamlining our process.
high.result: Our actions resulted in an increase in productivity and preparedness for each SGTA moving forward. Creating new forms of communication and better managed scheduled meetings also incited more proactive engagement from all members. This heavily boosted the morale and engagement of everyone within the project.

high.situation: We needed to think of a final action for our Rube-Goldberg Machine, and think of an overarching theme for the whole thing that was somehow connected to the final action.
high.task_action: We individually brainstormed many different ideas, then laid them out in a list on Miro. Once we had as many ideas as possible, we began whittling down our options, eliminating based on feasability and overall design quality. We initially had some conflicting ideas, but through proper and professional communication, we were able to decide as a team more effectively, willingly compromising if needed. Everyone’s ideas were seriously considered because it was very important to us that everyone in the team had a voice and felt that they were being heard.
high.result: We were able to come to an agreement on our machine’s design. We decided that the final action for the machine was to water a small house plant, and that to fit into the theme of watering a plant, at least every other action of the machine should involve the movement of water. Following this, we could think of the actual actions and find ways to piece them together.

high.situation:
WE AS A TEAM HAD TO MAKE A CONCEPTUAL DESIGN FOR OUR RUBE GOLDBERG MACHINE, HOWEVER NO ONE WAS READY TO WORK ON IT.

TASK:

AS NO ONE WAS WILLING TO WORK ON THE CONCEPTUAL DESIGN, I HAD TO JUMP IN AND INITIATE THE PROCESS.

ACTION:

I BRAINSTORMED A FEW IDEAS ON HOW TO ACHIEVE THE END GOAL/FUNCTION OF OUR INTENDED RGM. I WAS ABLE TO COME UP WITH THREE DIFFERENT IDEAS AND ALL OF THEM WERE EXECUTABLE, AND ALL HAD DIFFERENT COMPLEXITIES. THEN, I DID A DETAILED ANALYSIS WHICH CONSISTED OF CALCULATIONS, AND LIKELIHOOD OF SUCCESS OF ALL THREE IDEAS, AFTER WHICH I PICKED THE BEST DESIGN OUT OF THE THREE AS THE ONE I WOULD GO FOR WHEN MAKING THE PROJECT, AND KEPT THE REST OF THE TWO AS THE DESIGN ALTERNATIVES. AFTER THAT I MADE THE PRESENTATION ON MIRO AND ADDED THE INFORMATION REGARDING ALL THREE DESIGNS ON MIRO.
high.result: I WAS ABLE TO COME UP WITH THREE DIFFERENT WAYS ON HOW WE COULD ACHIEVE OUR END GOAL/FUNCTION. THE CONCEPTUAL DESIGN WAS PRESENTED TO THE TUTORS AND THEY APPRECIATED THE EFFORT.

high.situation:
During the unit ENGG1050, I experienced a drastic phase of my student career when I got involved in a team where I have to spend my entire course with them doing engineering experiments and the most intensive part is I did not know any of them then. It was for me getting in a city where I do not know anyone. But the hardest part was to come out as a team and perform the significance of us every week. We ended up having a neutral decision confusion regarding the role that we have to play. 

Action: At the very first class, we manage to get a better understanding of ourselves. We discussed mainly on our weaknesses and personal significance that can be beneficial to make a good team. We clear out the main motive of this course which is to earn skills and learn from others. Figured out the best solution is to learn to manage every role because at a time, we may have to end up doing everything. So it is a noteworthy opportunity for us to learn the each and every roles to progress in this unit whether it is presenter or minute tacker or even decorator of the theme that we present.
high.result: Fortunately, we were able to live up to the expectations. Especially we learned to work out as a team. In the engineering industry, team work is the most essential part of any project. At this point, we can clearly see the remarkable difference of ourselves from the first week of session 1. Now we feel confident enough to shine at any circumstances.

high.situation:
Every week we had to present something in our tutor group, where I got the chance or turn to do it a couple of times. It was my first time to present something in front of a large sum of people.

 

Task: After following a patterned rotation of roles in the team, when it was my turn to present, I had to prepare to speak in front of my tutor group which is a completely new thing for me. The presentation included the preliminary design of the machine which I had to demonstrate to my tutor group with a time limit of 5 minutes.

 

Action:  I had the presentation ready in the miro board which included contributions from my team. The last night before the presentation, I did a mock presentation twice to myself. I used stopwatch to see if I’m finishing on time, and used facetime in my laptop to see if my expressions look fine while presenting. I had some sticky notes stuck to my table so that I don’t forget the key things I’m gonna build upon in the presentation. The next morning when I presented it in the group, I also timed myself, presented well and finished all the points I decided to talk about.
high.result: I didn’t stutter or get nervous in the presentation rather finished it very smoothly. My tutor was happy with my presentation and decided we are gonna be the team to present in the main group that day.

high.situation: In the process of coming up with a fun and complex RGM, one of the main issues we must tackle is decision making. From as early on as week 1 we have had to implement decision making in small tasks such as assigning roles to the team members and as the weeks have progressed the subjects of our decision making have only become more and more crucial like managing time and recourses, coming up with ideas on how to use said recourses, making a preliminary design, budgeting, and more.
high.task_action: In the 1st couple of weeks, there were no major tasks. However, from week 3, we started brainstorming and soon we had an assortment of ideas and themes for the rgm that we had to pick and choose from to keep for the final design. We assessed each idea by what each of us members can execute to the best of our abilities, the amount of time and resources they would take to execute, if they were budget friendly and if they were free of any risks and hazards and most importantly if it fits the acceptance criteria demanded by our client.. After much data collection and consideration, we had to eliminate various parts and finally we ended up with a satisfactory layout of elements we wanted to include in our design.
high.result: The result was being able to create a preliminary design for the rgm that fits the client demands, adheres to our limitations and is safe to execute. Upon further evaluation of existing information about the members and with the consent of all the members roles were assigned for the week 7 presentation according to the members' best abilities.

high.situation:
Throughout the unit thus far, there have been multiple presentations, but one particular presentation on the ‘Problem Definition’ in Week 4 highlighted the importance of effective time and resource management, which I wanted to discuss in depth.

Task:

Our task was to watch a couple lectures and read a powerpoint, all on the topic of the ‘Problem Definition’, and then, as a group, create our own presentation on our understanding of the ‘Problem Definition’ and apply it to our own Rube Goldberg Machine Project. This, while a simple task, ended up being more challenging as though we had watched and read the necessary material as well as jotted some relevant information down about the ‘Problem Definition’, we hadn’t applied it to our RGM, let alone created a presentation for it. Whether it was because we had other things to do, we thought it would be simple and therefore left it to the last minute, or we forgot about it, it remained in this state until Monday morning, an hour before the presentation was due. (Note that this is no one's fault in particular.)

Action:

Because this task was still incomplete, I decided to take the task on myself and finish within the hour. The information jotted down was a good start, but required expanding, and there was a section that was entirely incomplete, so I went ahead and had another look at both the lectures and powerpoint. I expanded upon each section of the ‘Problem Definition’ and also applied it to our own RGM where relevant. Upon gathering all the information, I put it all into a powerpoint presentation and formatted it nicely and professionally. As class was beginning, I had completed the presentation, and thus the  outlined task, despite our previous poor management of time.
high.result: I told my team members about the presentation and they were grateful for my efforts. I ended up presenting it too and the feedback I received from my convenor was that I had done a really good job regarding the information presented, its relevance to our RGM and the professional look of the powerpoint, She also said that our group was going to present it to the main room as well, to which similar feedback and praise was received. Therefore, the task was completed successfully, despite the poor management of time that was mentioned above.

high.situation:
My team was tasked with creating a Rube Goldberg Machine (RGM) of our choice. The goal was to complete a simple task using multiple complicated tasks in a chain reaction. Each member would have 3 sections in the RGM and needed to have at least one unique element that was not present in another team members’ section.

 

TASK:

We began with some basic research and taking inspiration from other RGMs that we saw on YouTube. This helped our understanding of our task and helped start the conversation around our brainstorm of our own unique ideas. We came up with a variety of tasks that are usually simple, such as making breakfast and getting ready in the morning. These simple tasks can be made more complicated to fulfil the requirements of a RGM. The other aspect of our task was to ensure we had a unique idea, so after seeing similar ideas from other groups, we had a further team brainstorm. Our new task was to complicate the simple task of making breakfast, but with a unique twist of using one of our team member’s pets.

 

ACTION:

We approached the development of our RGM by breaking down the process into little steps, constantly thinking outside the box to make our RGM unique and stand out from the other groups. We all contributed to each other’s sections coming up with a range of creative ideas such as using our own pets in the RGM as well. For example, one member would use their birds by giving them food to eat off a plate which will lighten it and release a hot wheels car. I could use my cat by pointing a laser onto a button causing my cat to press it, activating an Arduino. The end result would be for the dog to receive treats, water and its favourite toy. I constantly encouraged and motivated my team to express their ideas no matter how big or small and I elaborated on their ideas in a respectful way, and encouraged them to be creative. I actively listened and provided positive feedback and suggestions to build on their ideas.
high.result:
Ultimately, through the use of creative thinking skills and effectively communicating our individual ideas to the team, we were able to create a successful RGM that achieved our goal of feeding our dog. The project was well-received by our instructor and fellow classmates, which was a positive influence on the dynamics of the team.

 

Our result was highly successful, even though we undertook this project as strangers. There was mutual respect and collaboration, and I believe my encouragement and support helped everyone feel included and empowered to contribute and be proud of our final product.

high.situation:
Prior to week six, my entire team and I hosted a team meeting to prepare a presentation on our Rube-Goldberg Machine’s conceptual design. In week six, I presented this same conceptual design to my tutor’s posse.

TASK:

My team and I outlined numerous tasks which needed to be fulfilled to guarantee success. Of these tasks, we firstly decided to discuss our previously conducted test results to decide what modules would be incorporated into our design. Following this, we decided that we should coordinate our efforts into brainstorming potential ‘talking points’ for our presentation. Finally, we chose to prepare our presentation with these discussions in mind, along with feedback which we had previously received.

ACTION:

Beginning our team meeting, we reflected on our test results, and discussed their implications on our Rube-Goldberg Machine’s overall design. We found that the modules we had created for our preliminary design were technically limiting (for example, we found that having a toy car roll onto a tube of toothpaste did not generate a meaningful force). In contrast to this, our alternative designs generally proved to be more effective at achieving our goals, and we therefore incorporated them into our updated design.

 

Following this, my team and I brainstormed several ideas on what points could be discussed throughout our presentation. One such technique we employed throughout this brainstorming session was ‘rapid ideation’. This was a methodology which we had learnt about through LinkedIn learning, where emphasis is placed on bursts of creativity instead of near-immediate critique. We used this technique as we originally intended to give a physical description of our design but found that it would likely lead to a verbose and uninteresting presentation.

 

Through this brainstorming session, we decided that our presentation would briefly touch on our experimentation for each module’s feasibility, which enabled us to explain why our current design was being used. Furthermore, we also thought to discuss ‘pros’ and ‘cons’ for each of our modules, which would allow for us to also analyse where further iterations on our designs could be used.

 

After we had established what design would ultimately be used, and how we were going to present it, we finalised our meeting by creating our presentation on Google Slides. During this process, we had taken care to ensure that our slides were concise and engaging, as we found previously that more verbose slides can lead to a reduction in audience engagement.
high.result: From our meeting, we were able to produce a presentation on our conceptual design which we were satisfied with. Refining our presentations skills further and applying our feedback now will prove to be useful as we create more presentations in the future.

high.situation:
For the unit ENGG1050 Engineering Design we were required to work with a group A17, which consists of 5 people including myself. Everyone in the group has been incredibly kind, considerate, and cooperative with one another, and they all respect each other's time. Early on, when we were just getting to know one another, we frequently discussed the importance of effective communication for the team's ability to work quickly, complete tasks on time, remain ahead of timelines, and provide our best effort throughout assessments and the RGM project.

TASK: As with every team project, there will always be some challenges. Arranging the time for our meeting after class was our first challenge. Normally, that wouldn't be a major issue, but since we all work full-time jobs in along with going to university full-time, our schedules constantly appeared to be full, and we weren't sure when anyone would be able to make it. It appeared that if two of the group could succeed, the rest would fail. So, we also advised holding two short meetings, each with one member present to discuss the actions taken at the prior meeting. It was a horrible way to conduct the meeting because it would take too much time. Working on a Google Doc and listing all the meeting's goals might be a simpler approach. ACTION: In the end, we decided to create a Google Docs to keep track of all our accomplishments, meeting goals, topics discussed, and areas that need our attention. high.situation:
For unit ENGG1050, the focus on the group project regarding the Rube Goldberg Model is a step-by-step process in which where the team’s progress is summarised in the weekly workshop through a 10-minute slideshow presentation. As a high-school graduate, I have been provided prior opportunities in preparing PowerPoint presentations and have adequate experience in making announcements in Year 12 class. However, the professional aspects involved in oral presentations are hugely different at Macquarie University. Expectations are high as the convenor for the workshop meeting has pointed out that speech as well as slide orientation needs to be concise and have a content structure. The concern regarding time constraints is strict as it is understood that real-life company meetings are not designed to suit the employee’s comfort. Students are trained to be flexible regardless of any interruption faced during a slideshow.

For Week 11, it was my turn to present for Team A12. From certain feedback, I understood that the importance of visual representation is just as important as the content in each slide. For example, in one slide, a table format was displayed to show the before and after improvements that were made on RGM event reliability. Two different colours were assigned to separate the content before and after. Although the content was well-organised, the colour coordination was chosen poorly as the green-orange mix caused reading difficulty.

Colour coordination comes naturally to a creative person. I find that I am lacking in this aspect as over the semester, my teammates have suggested other alternative methods to modify my RGM events to pass the reliability test threshold. As an aspiring engineering student, the quality to come up with original ideas is just as important as following your superior’s order. It defines capability to a certain extent.
high.task_action: There are dreamers, and then there are the realists. Both need each other to accomplish greater things in life. Back in Yr9, a poster needed to be designed for the WWII Holocaust. In a two-person group, one person is asked to type up the relevant content while the other had arrange the theme and images for the poster. The problem arose when almost all of the poster was composed of information. From this, I have understood the need for balance when compiling the 10 RGM events for Team A12. One member had his event ending with a water balloon being popped and the spilt water being collected in a bucket. To transition to the next event, a similar bucket element is required to zoom out of, but the member responsible for the said event has decided to change his focus from a pulley system to a projectile instead. Overall, the event order had to be re-arranged and this simple solution was suggested by a team member who got annoyed by a 20-minute quarrel that was wasted in zoom-meeting. The moral of this whole dilemma is that too much creative thinking can get on a realistic person’s last nerve.
high.result: For week 12, a group member who has avoided the role of the presenter was pointed out in Team A12’s group chat. high.situation:
_

_The team members discussed the function of the Rube Goldberg Machine after which the segments of the Rube Goldberg Machine were divided amongst the all team members._

_TASK:_

_I was assigned the segment which would deal with the end goal of the Rube Goldberg Machine, which was creating a confetti explosion. Furthermore each Rube Goldberg Machine needed to have at least 12 steps, which had to be equally divided amongst the team members. Our team consisted of five members, so we came to a conclusion that I and Sara would do three steps each and the rest of the team members would do two each._

_ACTION:_

_The end goal of the RGM was to create a confetti explosion and my segment required three steps to achieve that end goal. I decided to reverse engineer the process and begin with the end goal directly, I had three ideas in mind, one was using a party popper to create an explosion, second was to use pressure and plunger method and the last was using a balloon filled with confetti. Afterwards I created a feasibility chart and conducted some tests and figured out that both method one and two would require ample amount of force to create an explosion and the most viable option would be using a balloon filled with confetti. Now that how to achieve the end goal was decided, the next step was the design process. After watching multiple videos on YouTube, I finalised my design; it would consist of a ball going down the ramp, then hitting the dominoes, which would hit a car and the car would have a thumb pin attached to it, the car will be pushed into the confetti filled balloon, creating an explosion. I used the box which came with my vacuum cleaner, I made a path out of it for the ball to move down. For the ball to go down, I had to adjust the height at every turn in the path, so the ball moves down smoothly and doesn’t get stuck at any of the turns. Next thing was making the dominoes topple using the ball, since I did not have dominoes, I used my jenga blocks. The issue I faced here was that the ball was going into the blocks, so stop it, I used rubber bands at the end of the last ramp, so that the ball hits the wooden block causing it to topple, creating a domino effect and at the same time the rubber bands stops the ball from going into the blocks. The last step was that the ultimate block will fall on the back of a dinky car (with a thumb pin attached to its front) causing it move down the path (which was made out of my hot wheels set) into the confetti filled balloon creating an explosion. After making the segment, I tested it, the wooden blocks did not cause the car to go down, so I had to arrange them in a pattern where they hit the car in a manner, that it moves down the track. I conducted multiple tests and improvised each trial until the machine became fully working on each trial._

_
high.result:
_

_The meticulous designing and feasible approach proved to be successful, every component had a hundred percent reliability, which gave my segment a reliability of one-hundred percent._

high.situation:
I was put in a team of 5 and assigned to create a functional RGM machine within a certain timeframe and limited resources which included weekly goals and accountability. But along with that, I also had 5 other activities to do in the same unit. I have realised that this unit is fun but has quite a lot of activities to be done at the same time and that academic pressure was squeezing me and leading me to utter frustration. I consider ineffective aproach towards study and bad schedule to be blamed for that.

 

TASK: Each week, I and my team had to present our progress in a specific aspect e.g design criteria, reliability, preliminary design, etc. New agenda was assigned for the next week with a set of tasks to be completed the tasks for each week were interdependent, so I had to make sure that my team and I never fell behind.
