high.situation: The unit ENGG1050 is a fully online unit and it was my first time , I was studying a full unit online. So, it seemed difficult to me to communicate with my teammates online. Also, me and my teammates were confused in choosing software for presentations.
high.task_action: In our first session of class, I found that all of my teammates were friendly and we decided to make a whatsapp group for communication. Then one of my teammate shared a google document , this document purpose was to pick a common time for online team meetings , we all coloured timings red in which we were busy. At last for presentations, we consulted our tutor to decided which software is best.
high.result: As a result, we found a common time at which all were free for online meetings and our tutor referred us miro software and it's really best for making presentations and for communication.

high.situation: Our team was tasked with brainstorming the concept design for the sequence of events in our Rube Goldberg Machine operation.
high.task_action: To address this challenge, we organized a brainstorming session with the entire team outside of class time, which consisted of five members. The task was to come up with a creative and visually appealing sequence of events that would captivate our audience and execute the intended tasks. We began by clearly articulating the problem statement, which was to design a series of interconnected steps that would ultimately lead to a chain reaction, accomplishing a specific final goal. During the session, establish teamwork by encouraging everyone to share their ideas freely, no matter how unconventional. We used the gallery method of brainstorming, where each idea was written on a sticky note and posted on a Miro wall for all to see. This allowed us to build upon each other's concepts.
high.result: After finalizing the concept, we recognized the importance of breaking down the tasks further. We divided the sequence into individual components, each assigned to a specific team member based on their strengths and expertise. We maintained open communication throughout the design and building phases. This involved regular meetings, progress updates, and sharing of resources and knowledge. The collaborative brainstorming session resulted in a well-defined and creative sequence of events for our Rube Goldberg Machine project.

high.situation: We are dealing the challenge of developing a Rube Goldberg machine capable of performing a number of difficult tasks that required precision and uniqueness.
high.task_action: To take on this task, we selected a different team and precisely stated the goals of our Rube Goldberg machine. We began by breaking down the intended sequence of events into smaller, achievable segments, using critical thinking abilities. Each team member then brainstormed various methods and activities that may contribute to the overall chain reaction.  We used online resources to take inspiration and other tools which could potentially help us to collaborate easier with other teams.  We encouraged open debate and built on one other's ideas, ensuring that every proposed step corresponded with the project's objectives.
high.result: As a result of our critical thinking and collaborative effort, we successfully designed a Rube Goldberg machine that performed flawlessly.

high.situation: During a zoom meeting with the cohort, I needed to display my groups' progress by presenting to the zoom our ideas and concepts for our Rube Goldberg machine project.      Action:To make my oral presentation a success, I implemented the following tactic:I reviewed the main ideas and details of our Rube Goldberg machine and each member's contribution and prepared for the presentation. On my phone, I jotted reminders/palm card notes to retain focus and purpose throughout the presentation. These notes were short, and helped me in staying relevant without having to read off a script.
high.result: Thanks to my implementation notes, I was able to accomplish the task of delivering an informative oral presentation. I managed to cover all major aspects of our Rube Goldberg machine project whilst staying within the allotted time frame. Keeping reminders on my phone was extremely effective in achieving a polished delivery and staying focused. The input I received from my tutor suggested that our progress was effectively conveyed and my goal of delivering a presentation was complete.

high.situation: Our group was instructed to create a Rube Goldberg Machine in order to achieve a trivial output, such as making coffee, playing a song etc.             TASK:      We were tasked with organising our group, assigning roles to each member, deciding on what the machine would achieve, and coming up with a preliminary design for the machine.             ACTION:      Within our meetings over the past six weeks, we discussed and brainstormed what the machine would achieve, and how we would design it to achieve it. We allocated roles to each member of the team, based on our own individual strengths. Early in the design process, we chose to make the machine do something musical, and so we spent time deciding what kind of music it would play. We then spent time figuring out what sections we would each be responsible for and then brainstormed how we were each going to design the section we were responsible for to achieve the “micro-goal” of the section. We also took on feedback from tutors and other groups during the design process that arose during the weekly presentations.
high.result: We elected to have the machine play “Last Living Souls” by Gorillaz. I was allocated a section towards the start of the machine, responsible for starting the guitar part of the song. The others each have their own parts, involving different instruments, such as the drums, keys, and bass. At the time of writing, we haven’t yet figured out how to handle the vocal section. Most of us, seem to have some idea of how they will design our individual sections to achieve the task at hand.

high.situation: Members within our group had come up with different design ideas and end goals for our Rube Goldberg Machine project that is due at the end of this session.
high.task_action: In order to determine what idea is best suitable, I had asked the members within the group who disagreed with each other's ideas, to present arguments into why their design idea should be used. I had advised them to provide evidence and give their perspective on the ideas. I had also advised them to keep an open mind and really try to understand each other's ideas and ask questions as this could help avoid confusion or any other misunderstanding. I myself decided to be a neutral person in this situation and had loved all the ideas presented to me, however we could only choose one end goal.
high.result: Through the use of critical thinking, we as a group assessed each idea and studied the depths of it by understanding the positives and negatives that come with each design idea. We then later as a group came to a conclusion that our end goal for the rube goldberg machine will be to water a plant. However we decided to combine the ideas for the series of events that will take place within the design, such as “the ball falling into the cup” or “the balloon popping”, by incorporating different ideas that allows it to be inclusive and gives everyone the opportunity to share their thoughts.

high.situation: As a first year engineering student completing the ENGG1050 unit, I was placed in a group of people who I have never met, and tasked with completing a Rube Goldberg Machine project with this group.
high.task_action: Throughout the weeks of working together, I faced many challenges in areas such as communication and decision-making. As a first-year student joining a new group of students, I was quite nervous when introducing myself for the first time as I did not know anyone, and was unsure if someone would turn out to be unfriendly or judgemental. I would focus on finishing our given task which resulted in all of us effectively working together. However, this required a lot of initiative as I had to "break the ice" and remove the feeling of anxiety and nervousness that I had. Additionally, I also had to face many scenarios where me and my group were uncertain of what we should do. This forced me to take the initiative and provide possible solutions that we could use to finally make a decision in order to progress and move forward.
high.result: As a result, I was able to gain a lot of experience in dealing with situations where I feel anxious or uncertain of what to do, especially in a space where I do not know anyone. I was also able to take the initiative in many scenarios and try to make the best decision possible when facing uncertainty, effectively improving my communication, initiative, and decision-making skills.

high.situation: The situation for the chosen topic is the two weeks before my HSC (High School Certificate) final Year 12 exams in 2022, which would determine my final grade and the degree I would be eligible for. I had my 18th birthday and my Year 12 formal party during this two-week period, making it incredibly challenging to manage studying and having fun. I was unable to enjoy these 2 major events that one only gets to experience once because of the stress of the final exams, which had now begun to set in. As a result, even though I wanted to completely enjoy my 18th birthday party and my Year 12 formal, I couldn't help but feel a constant sense of urgency as I thought about the final exams, which were constantly lingering at the back of my mind. Hence, I had to find a way to manage my time, have the will to deliver and become flexible such that I didn’t regret not enjoying these events but also not regretting that I didn’t study enough for the exams.
high.task_action: To ensure that I enjoyed my time during these 2 once in a lifetime events and also did my best in the HSC, I had to implement certain time and resource management strategies. These tactics made sure I always had the perseverance and drive to succeed. I was able to maximise my productivity while studying using these techniques, which allowed me to have a greater amount of time to enjoy my 18th birthday party and my formal.       The very first step I took was to plan ahead, as time itself is also a very important resource. I utilised Google Calendar to break each day up into study sections. Each section was 1-2 hours, depending on how much work needed to be done. Each section would only focus on one subject at a time. During these study sections I would either go through my flashcards using an app called Anki (which utilises the study method called spaced repetition) or I would attempt practice exams and reattempt the ones that I got wrong. By reattempting questions I got wrong I was able to gain maximum benefits out of the practice papers which were limited. Moreover, Anki would give me daily notifications to remind me that I had a certain amount of flashcards to finish before the end of the day. This ensured that I was able to deliver and not become too overwhelmed by the sense of urgency.      The next action I took was setting clear priorities. As the dates of each exam was spread out, I would spend more time on the subject that had its test the earliest. Next, I would prioritise topics within each subject whereby I knew I had gaps in my knowledge. This enabled me to not only reinforce what I knew but also ensure that I wasn’t going into my exams without knowing everything that I needed to. By doing so, I was able to use the limited time resources I had and ensure I was doing the best I could. As I knew I was doing the best I could, I had moments where I felt calm and the lingering stress was decreased. This motivated me to persevere through the following weeks without drowning in an ocean of anxiety and self-doubt.      Another step that I took was to focus on one task at a time. Rather than thinking about each subject as a whole, I made a To Do list on Google Keep which enabled me to break the subject down into parts. This reduced the stress greatly as larger topics were broken down into more mentally digestible smaller topics. This also increased how flexible I could be when creating my study timetable.
high.result: The aim of my actions were to maximise the use of the little time I had to study for my exams so that I would be able to enjoy my 18th birthday party and my Year 12 formal party. The result of my actions was that I was able to fully enjoy the two events with only almost no negative effects on my exams. I had attempted each test to the best of my ability and I was confident that I was going to receive the mark that I wanted. I had not regretted enjoying the parties or wishing that I spent more time studying for my exams. Moreover I had very little stress during the exams. When I got my ATAR (final mark) back, I had accomplished the initial goal that I had set. However I was certain that I could’ve done better by not attending the parties, but this didn’t bother me as I knew it would’ve had no extra benefit as I achieved the goal I wanted to. In the end, I had no regrets and I achieved the initial task that I had set out to complete.

high.situation: At the very beginning of the unit, students were assigned to groups, commonly with people they did not know. I had no previous experiences with my 5 assigned team mates. As a team, we had to quickly establish a connection and communication lines amongst each other. A communications structure was needed to ensure we'd be able to convey new ideas and constructive criticism regarding any projects or classwork. A communications structure was also needed to ensure each group member was on top of their work and was informed about any upcoming classwork and/or team meetings.
high.task_action: In week 1, we established a preliminary communication line in the form of a Miro board. Subsequently, we then created a Discord server and on top of that a WhatsApp group for push notifications. We also created a Trello board so we could effectively document any tasks that were needed to be done and mark them as completed.
high.result: The result was being able to communicate effectively with each other and be able to contact one another at any point any day. Having the Trello board allowed us to stay up-to-date on work that needed to be done and be informed on what tasks had been finished.

high.situation: We had to schedule and distribute individual  time across the week to get ready for our weekly presentation and devise/create  concepts,  plans and materials  for our RGM project.
high.task_action: It was a responsibility that all team members allocated time each week to work on projects and presentations. Through team meetings, discussions, zoom meetings and phone calls we were able to commenced by evaluating the available week's timeframe, breaking down tasks for both the presentation and RGM project. A team meeting was orchestrated to define objectives and generate ideas collaboratively. A well-balanced schedule was crafted, accounting for presentation groundwork and project conceptualisation. We come up with the presentational design where each week the rotation of roles will be turned so that every week a different team member was presenting. How we kept the time management going is the we used online talking platforms like zoom, WhatsApp and phone calls to keep track of everyone availability and keep everyone updated on the upcoming meetings and allocated times. We even shared calendars so that we can know exactly we will be coming to the meeting on that certain weeks.      We also had to make time throughout the week to discuss how we going to come up with our certain RGM project and how we going to enable it to work and the team  encouraged a dynamic brainstorming environment where every team member felt empowered to contribute their ideas without reservation. As ideas flowed, the individual adeptly guided the discussions, helping the team to identify the most promising concepts. These concepts were then subjected to a rigorous evaluation process, assessing factors such as feasibility, innovation, and alignment with project goals. Through depth discussions and analysing how we are going to use materials to build certain aspects of our machine we managing resource management by writing down the cost of each product and also who is going o transport which product on the day of creation.
high.result: The combined efforts of these actions culminated in a cohesive and strategic approach to both the presentation and the RGM project. This approach not only facilitated the successful completion of immediate tasks but also laid the groundwork for enduring teamwork and effective project execution. Thanks to our well-structured approach and effective time allocation, we delivered a polished and engaging presentation that effectively conveyed our ideas. Our presentation was well-received by our peers and garnered valuable feedback. For the RGM project, our collaborative brainstorming led to a diverse range of innovative ideas. Through careful evaluation and refinement, we narrowed down our concepts to a few strong directions. The outcome was a set of well-defined plans and design materials that laid a solid foundation for the next phases of the project. By strategically managing time, promoting collaboration, and balancing tasks, we achieved a successful presentation and set our RGM project on a promising trajectory. This experience underscored the importance of effective planning and teamwork in achieving project goals.

high.situation: Since beginning this unit, I have needed to work alongside a team in order to build a Rube Goldberg Machine.
high.task_action: Many of the tasks that we have needed to get done have included working on our preliminary design, determining the materials for our Machine, and completing a presentation every week for our SGTA. A big aspect of this unit is having to complete all of our tasks online. This means that most of these tasks are worked on remotely, and we don't usually communicate face-to-face. One of the most important aspects of working on this project has been time and resource management. Since we only meet once every week, my group and I strive to complete as much as possible during our SGTAs/Workshops.       For the hour of time given to us, we make sure to communicate readily with one another so that we won't waste any time on miscommunication, and we allocate tasks for each member so that we know exactly what we need to do and get started much quicker. As for resource management, we have made sure to study all of the possible materials we will use for our project and tried as much as possible to make use of recyclable materials or things we already own.       This allows us to save both time and money, since we will no longer need to set aside a big budget for our project, and we won't need to meet up in order to buy materials together.
high.result: Through these, we were able to work on a huge portion of our RBM without wasting any time or resources. This also allows us to have extra time during every workshop, giving us the opportunity to work on creating better visuals for our presentations.

high.situation: We needed to find an alternative solution for one of our events within a group task. We were creating a Rube Goldberg Machine (RGM) in which we incorporated the transportation of water from one point to another.
high.task_action: Upon creation of our RGM we figured that the wooden channel that we had used for this task was not effective and that it was absorbing water and was not allowing it to reach its desired point. After having multiple discussions on different alternatives, we gathered together numerous materials that could be used for this task. high.result: Instead of only changing the materials we had also changed how the structure of the RGM would work. Instead of using water in the middle phase of the RGM, we had decided that the water part would only come at the end. So as each sequence had progressed within the task, the final outcome of the RGM was that there was a bottle that had water in it, and that was tipped over, which had created an efficient flow of water. This had successfully achieved its set purpose as the RGM was to water a plant.

high.situation: As I quickly progressed though my first weeks at uni I quickly realised the way I managed my time and resources was insufficient and would lead me to fail if i didnt evaluate my situation and adapt to the new environment I found myself in.
high.task_action: In highschool i greatly valued my family and social life, cometimes to the point where it would affect my school life. But it always ended the same way, putting off my approach to do’s until the very last minute, then pulling an all nighter and finishing them with an average mark. This worked well for me in highschool as i wasn't too hung up over grades and spent a lot of time with the people i love.        My first 7 weeks of session 1 really hit me like a ton of bricks. I had taken the same approach and before I knew it I had 3 assignments and a coding module exam due in the same week. I hadn't managed my time or my resources correctly and struggled immensely.       After that i knew i had to change my schedule if i wanted to do well in uni, so i did that. I spent all of my free time completing assignments early, studying or doing any extra reading I could with the goal of not ending up in the same position as last time.       After session one I finished with HD’s and D’s, and thought I had finally worked out how to correctly manage my time. Until the holiday where I realised I had not valued my family or social life in weeks. I had become distant with all my friends from highschool and haven't had a conversation longer than “how was your day”, “good” with my parents.       From there I had my mid semester break, which gave me two months to rekindle my connection with all my friends but also devise a schedule that meets all my needs equally, while still managing my time and resources to get the grades I so desire. I set my schedule up on google calendar and supplied time for ‘uni work’, ‘social life’, ‘family time’ and ‘alone time’. This allowed me to hit all my goals as a student, but also as a friend and a family member.
high.result: I'm now in week 12 of my second semester and I can proudly say it works perfectly. I'm averaging a D to HD for every unit I take. I've also been able to allow myself to attend social gatherings and reconnect with a lot of old friends from highschool.

high.task_action: We assigned a set amount of time to brainstorm together methods of a more efficient study process that would allow for us to solve our situation, we conceptualized various methods, however we concluded through debate and an anonymous voting system which idea we would implement, we also due to this debating system decided to run a one week trial of this idea to test is competence.

high.situation: My tenure as a senior product developer at Old Bones Chili Co., a family-owned and operated Chilli Sauce & Condiments producer based in Sydney, allowed me to hone and apply my co-manufacturing skills. Non-disclosure agreements bind the specifics of the situation. However, broadly, my role involved liaising with potential clients to discuss service packages, develop scopes of work, generate price quotes, produce comprehensive reports, and consult on product augmentations to ensure compliance with federal regulations.      TASK: In my role, I was responsible for all aspects directly related to the product. This included conducting pre-production debriefs to discuss Failure Modes and Effects Analysis (FMEA), overseeing the production process, and formulating reports detailing all aspects of production.      ACTION: I leveraged the principles of Net Present Value, NPV, analysis and multiple regression techniques in the conclusion/suggestion section of the post-production reports and de-brief meetings. I also utilised the principles of Six Sigma, a data-driven approach to process improvement, as the foundation of the co-manufacturing practice. Additionally, I applied the Theory of Constraints by Dr Eliyahu Goldratt, a management paradigm that focuses on improving system performance, as a guiding principle in directing the project. My consistent aim was to provide the client with profitable solutions and products.
high.result: My contributions played a pivotal role in the triumph of Old Bones’ co-manufacturing department triumph. They not only led to contented clients who were delighted with the results of the pilot projects but also frequently advanced to full-scale production, in line with recommendations from post-production pilot reports. Consequently, my involvement with the company escalated, with initial talks about leading the entire department locally and potentially assuming a leadership position in sister companies abroad.

high.situation: I was assigned a solo project to design and implement a simple Rock, Paper, Scissors game as part of a software engineering course. TASK: The primary task was to develop a fully functional Rock, Paper, Scissors game that could reliably handle user inputs and display game results accordingly. I needed to ensure that the game logic was error-free and that the user interface was intuitive. Additionally, I had to complete this project within a two-week timeframe to meet the submission deadline.      ACTION: I began by outlining the core components of the game, including the user input mechanism, the game logic, and the result display. I took the following steps to manage my time and resources effectively:      Scheduled specific blocks of time each day dedicated to the project, ensuring that I balanced my efforts between this and other responsibilities.      Used a simple to-do list on my phone to check my progress, such as planning, coding, testing, and debugging.      Implemented the game using a modular approach, which allowed me to isolate and resolve issues quickly without affecting other parts of the code.      Conducted iterative testing with peers to gather feedback and refine the user interface and experience.
high.result: By adhering to a structured schedule and using a project management approach, even in a solo setting, I was able to complete the project ahead of the deadline, with three days to spare. The project received positive feedback for its simplicity, contributing to a high grade in the course. high.situation: Taking the role as civil engineers in a 7-person group, we were tasked with designing student housing for Macquarie University students, focusing specifically on creating comfortable learning spaces doubling as accommodation. The challenge was to incorporate essential amenities while maximising space and ensuring the structure was sustainable and cost-effective. Additionally, we were required to create a realistic physical model using various materials replicating our design, incorporating detailed site analysis to reflect a realistic portrayal of geographical conditions.
high.task_action: Our primary goal was to design a practical and appealing accommodation to students but also environmentally sustainable and accessible. We needed to consider factors such as the average climate and terrain. The task involved extensive collaboration among team members to integrate architectural design ergonomics with modelling requirements. We initiated our project by researching to create a detailed site analysis, which included studying the topography, existing utilities, and accessibility of the site. To further this research we held multiple brainstorming sessions to discuss various designs, considering factors like natural light, ventilation, and communal spaces. Later accessibility issues popped up with considerations for the site location having to be connected to Talivera Road and efficiently utilising the nearby Mars Creek. Using SketchUp engineering software, we drafted early sketch models and refined them based on group discussions and research. After finishing our design, we constructed the physical model, ensuring every detail from our digital design was accurately represented using foam and cardboard.
high.result: The completed model was successful in demonstrating a layout optimised for student living, with designated study areas, green spaces, and communal facilities that encouraged social interaction while respecting privacy. Our design was well-received during our presentation, with specific praise for its optimal use of space and sustainability features such as rainwater catchers and solar panels. This project not only enhanced our practical skills in model building but also deepened our understanding of integrating various engineering roles into disciplines to meet real world needs.

high.situation: Over the past 6 years I have worked professionally as a Diagnostic Radiographer across many public and private healthcare facilities. To this day I can still recollect my very first nightshift, and the experiences that I had learnt from it. Working in the Townsville University Hospital this night, I was the sole radiographer providing all the Medical Imagining services across both Xray and CT for the entire hospital.      It was a busy night and at some point, I received two phone calls within seconds of each other, one was theatre stating they had an anesthetised patient on the table and needed x-rays, and the other was a MET (Medical emergency team) Call attending a patient with acute shortness of breath that needed me also. As I was leaving the workstation to change into my theatre attire, there was an announcement made through the  emergency PA system “Paediatric Rapid Response to RESUS”.
high.task_action: I immediately called the second on-call Radiographer, explained what was happening and asked them come in. I got changed into scrubs, informed ED and the MET call team that I had to attend theatre urgently and that another radiographer would be there shortly. Once I had finished screening (taking x-rays) for the theatre case, I returned to the medical imaging department, sent the on-call radiographer home and continued the rest of the shift.      Although I applied good critical thinking to able to overcome this sudden spike in the workload, I wanted to make a change, so that something like this would not happen again, to myself or my colleagues. So, I approached my team leader with my concerns about the increased workload and the pressure it has on the sole nightshift radiographer, especially the less experienced graduates (as I was). I also addressed the issue, with statistics I had gathered, in staff meetings and with the deputy director at the time advocating for increased support for the nightshift radiographer and especially for graduates undertaking nightshift for the first time.
high.result: At the Townsville University Hospital, I have since been the last radiographer to ever (since 2019) work their first nightshift alone. As part of my advocating for a change and the support of my  colleagues, the first initial nightshift for any new staff member is rostered with a ‘buddy’, another experienced radiographer to help guide them through the afterhours procedures and processes. Although I did also advocate for a second radiographer to be rostered to nightshift indefinitely due to the increased workload, I cannot claim that I was reason that they decided to do so. COVID-19  and the increased workload that came with it was,  but I supported this and undertook additional nightshifts to help the nightshift radiographer.

high.situation: WE NEEDED TO CREATE A BINARY CALCULATOR USING AN ARDUINO CIRCUIT ,LEDS,BUTTONS, AND A LCD DISPLAY. EACH BUTTON SHOULD TURN ON ITS RELATIVE LED TO DISPLAY A NUMBER IN BINARY NOTATION , AN EXTRA BUTTON IS PRESSED TO STORE THE FIRST NUMBER, THEN AFTER ANOTHER NUMBER IS ENTERED PRESSING THE BUTTON ADDS THE NUMBERS THEN DISPLAYS THE SUM ON THE LCD DISPLAY AND IN BINARY NOTATION ON THE LEDS
high.task_action: TO SOLVE THE CALCULATOR PROBLEM, I APPROACHED IT FROM MULTIPLE ANGLES, BUT THE BEST SOLUTION WAS TO BREAK IT DOWN INTO MULTIPLE PARTS. THIS INVOLVED CREATING MANY SEPARATE CIRCUITS. TO KNOW WHAT CIRCUITS TO BUILD A BRAINSTORM WAS DRAWN UP SHOWING ALL THE SEPARATE FUNCTIONS THAT THE CALCULATOR HAD TO ACHIEVE.THEN THESE FUNCTIONS WERE ASSIGNED TO SMALLER CIRCUITS THAT COULD BE INTEGRATED INTO A LARGER SYSTEM.  EXAMPLES OF THE SMALLER INDIVIDUAL CIRCUITS ARE AS FOLLOWS BUT LIMITED TO 4 BUTTONS WHICH TURN ON THEIR RESPECTIVE LEDS, BINARY TO DECIMAL CONVERTER AND SUM OF TWO NUMBER CIRCUITS. AT THE END OF THE CREATION OF THESE INDIVIDUAL CIRCUITS THEY ALL HAD TO BE COMBINED TO CREATE A FULLY FUNCTIONING BINARY CALCULATOR.
high.result: THE RESULT OF CREATING A CLEAR PLAN TO DESIGN THE CIRCUIT ALLOWED FOR AN EASY CREATION AND IMPLEMENTATION OF CIRCUITS WITH RELATIVE EASE , ALLOWING FOR THE CREATION OF THE FULLY CREATED END PRODUCT WHICH MEETS ALL OF ITS SPECIFICATIONS AND REQUIREMENTS.

high.situation: In order to complete this task and all of its extensions, I had to use critical thinking and problem-solving skills in order to construct a dynamic algorithm that could facilitate a seemingly infinite amount of gestures. In short, how could I use maths to find the relationship between numbered gestures, no matter the amount of gestures?
high.task_action: To complete this part of the task, I took my problem-solving skills to pen and paper. First, I drew out the relationships between each gesture in a game of RPS-11 _(Rock, Paper, Scissors with a total of 11 different hand gestures) _and then I numbered them from 0 to 10, moving clockwise around the circle. From observing the lines drawn to indicate which gestures would win and lose against others, I noticed that each gesture loses to half of the gestures that are immediately clockwise from itself and wins against the remaining half of the gestures that are counter-clockwise from itself. With that in mind, I began trying to express this relationship using maths. After a few attempts at nailing the formula, I began implementing the algorithm in code.
high.result: Although I made a few errors initially with the mathematical algorithm, I could test it using my code to work out what was going wrong. Eventually, I had built a dynamic algorithm for determining the winner and thus the result was a resounding success.

high.situation: I was tasked with assisting a user who presented a vague problem statement.  Their request was to "improve website traffic."  While website traffic is a metric, it's not a specific problem itself.      Task:      My primary function is to understand user intent and facilitate problem-solving.  In this situation, the task was to define a clear and actionable solution by helping the user pinpoint the underlying issue affecting their website traffic.      Action:      Clarification Questions: I employed open-ended questions to elicit more details about the user's situation. For example, I inquired about their website's current traffic levels, any specific goals they had for improvement, and if they had observed any recent changes in user behavior.      Problem Framing: Based on the user's responses, I helped them frame the problem in a more specific way. This could involve identifying areas like low search engine ranking, high bounce rate, or lack of user engagement.      Solution Exploration: Once the core problem was defined, I offered potential solutions tailored to the user's specific needs. These solutions could involve content optimization, keyword research, or user interface improvements.
high.result: Problem Definition: We were able to define a clear and actionable problem statement. Instead of a generic goal of "improving website traffic," the user now had a targeted issue to address, such as "increasing organic search traffic."      Focused Solutions: With a well-defined problem, I could provide more relevant and effective solutions. Instead of general website traffic suggestions, the user received actionable steps focused on improving organic search ranking.      Empowered User: By understanding the root cause of the problem, the user was empowered to make informed decisions and take appropriate actions to address their website traffic concerns.

high.situation: In a project to simulate a smart home automation system using Tinkercad circuits, I encountered a challenge where the simulated sensors were not providing accurate readings, hindering the functionality of the system. This discrepancy jeopardized the project's success as it relied heavily on precise sensor data for triggering automation actions within the simulation.      TASK: My task was to identify the underlying cause of the sensor inaccuracies within the Tinkercad simulation environment and devise a solution to ensure consistent and reliable sensor readings for the smart home automation system.      ACTION:      SIMULATION ANALYSIS: I began by thoroughly analyzing the Tinkercad simulation setup, examining the connections, configurations, and simulated environment parameters related to the sensors. I checked for any discrepancies or misconfigurations that could potentially impact the accuracy of the sensor readings.      CODE REVIEW AND DEBUGGING: I meticulously reviewed the Arduino code responsible for interfacing with the simulated sensors within the Tinkercad environment. I debugged the code to identify any logical errors, syntax issues, or inaccuracies in the data processing algorithms.      VIRTUAL TESTING SCENARIOS: To diagnose the sensor inaccuracies, I created a series of virtual testing scenarios within the Tinkercad simulation environment. These scenarios simulated various real-world conditions and sensor inputs to evaluate the system's response and identify potential sources of error.      DATA VISUALIZATION: Utilizing Tinkercad's data visualization tools, I monitored and analyzed the sensor readings in real-time during the virtual testing scenarios. This allowed me to visualize the data trends, anomalies, and inconsistencies, facilitating the identification of underlying issues.      PARAMETER ADJUSTMENT: Based on the analysis of simulated sensor data, I adjusted the simulation parameters such as sensor calibration values, noise levels, and environmental factors to better reflect real-world conditions and improve the accuracy of the sensor readings.      SIMULATION REFINEMENT: Through an iterative process of testing, analysis, and refinement, I fine-tuned the Tinkercad simulation settings and code implementations to mitigate sensor inaccuracies and enhance the reliability of the smart home automation system simulation.
high.result: * ENHANCED SIMULATION ACCURACY: By identifying and addressing the underlying causes of sensor inaccuracies within the Tinkercad simulation environment, I successfully improved the accuracy of the simulated sensor readings.   	* INCREASED RELIABILITY: The implemented solutions ensured consistent and reliable sensor data within the Tinkercad simulation, enabling the smart home automation system to function reliably and accurately in virtual scenarios.   	* PROJECT ADVANCEMENT: With the sensor accuracy issue resolved, the project progressed smoothly, meeting its objectives of simulating a functional smart home automation system within the Tinkercad environment.

high.situation: I NEEDED TO DESIGN AN ARDUINO CIRCUIT TO ADD BINARY NUMBERS AND OUTPUT THAT ON LEDS AND AN LCD. I WAS NOT SURE HOW TO LAY OUT THE PRIMARY COMPONENTS AND CODE BLOCKS AT A HIGH LEVEL
high.task_action: TIME WAS OF THE ESSENCE AND THE FINAL TOP LEVEL LAYOUT OF HOW THE CODE OR THE CIRCUIT WOULD LOOK WAS UNCLEAR. I HAD TWO OPTIONS: I COULD TAKE TIME OUT TO PLAN IT, OR FIGURE IT OUT ALONG THE WAY. BECAUSE TIME WAS SO IMPORTANT, I DECIDED TO FIGURE IT OUT ALONG THE WAY. SMALL PERFORMANCE AND READABILITY GAINS WERE SACRIFICED AT MANY TURNS IN FAVOUR OF MODULARITY, AS THINGS WERE PUT TOGETHER ON THE FLY.
high.result: THE RESULT WAS A SOLUTION THAT WAS PERFORMANT ENOUGH TO PASS THE NECESSARY TESTS, AND COMPLETED IN TIME FOR THE TASK.

high.situation: The task was to design a mechanism that could effectively unload cans from a robot onto a shelving unit. This was a complex problem requiring a solution that was not only functional but also efficient and reliable.
high.task_action: In response, a dedicated brainstorming session was organized. The first step involved forming a small group of engineers, where the problem statement was clearly defined to ensure everyone's understanding and alignment on the objectives. We used the gallery method of brainstorming, which allows individual team members to develop ideas independently and then share them on paper around the room for collective discussion and refinement. This method was chosen to encourage diverse contributions and prevent any single perspective from dominating the discussion. After generating several potential solutions, we systematically evaluated each one based on specific parameters such as weight, complexity, and manufacturability. This evaluation was crucial to maintain objectivity and ensure that the selected solution would meet all technical and practical requirements.
high.result: The outcome of this structured brainstorming and evaluation process was the identification of six feasible design solutions. These were ranked, and the most suitable design was selected for implementation. Following this decision, the tasks required to develop the selected design were clearly assigned to different team members, according to their expertise and roles within the project. This clear allocation of responsibilities was essential for efficient project execution and management.      This example illustrates the pivotal roles and responsibilities of engineers, involving not just technical expertise but also strategic planning, collaborative problem-solving, and effective project management. These components are vital for transforming theoretical ideas into practical, implementable engineering solutions.

high.situation: Due to differing levels of participation, it was acknowledged during our professional development seminar that the organisation needed to address attitude shifts and foster a culture of continual learning.
high.task_action: I led workshops to investigate learning behaviours, attitudes, and thoughts. We created cross-functional teams, performed surveys, and used a variety of strategies, including role-playing games and group discussions. To track insights, reflective journaling was created.      Enhanced collaboration, a greater understanding of the value of a growth mindset, and better participation in training initiatives were among the favourable results. In general, the effort promoted an organisational culture that was more positive and centred on growth and learning.

high.situation: While developing a software project using C programming for "Rock, Paper, Scissors" with different gestures, I faced the challenge of determining the winner for varying numbers of gestures (5, 7, 9, and 11) while ensuring adherence to the game's rules. In this game, three possible outcomes exist: a tie occurs when both players have the same outcome, and the game continues until one player wins at least two times. Victory or defeat is based on a player's gesture chosen against another player.
high.task_action: My task involved creating a program to play "Rock, Paper, Scissors" against a computer with varying numbers of gestures (5, 7, 9, and 11), demonstrating how the game determines the winner and functions until one player wins at least two times. To achieve this, I developed a program that handled player input, generated computer gestures, and compared gestures to determine the winner and manage the game flow. My perseverance was evident in my refusal to give up when my initial algorithm failed to handle games with 7, 9, or 11 gestures. By analysing the rules of the Rock Paper Scissors Lizard Spock game, I implemented a simple logic using even and odd to determine the winner for 5 gestures only and another using another mathematical algorithm for 7,9 and 11 based on the chosen gestures. This experience taught me the importance of being resourceful and flexible in problem-solving.
high.result: Overall, my program meets all the requirements for various numbers of gestures following the game's rules and can easily accommodate additional gestures if necessary. It runs efficiently, utilising only a small amount of the codebase, demonstrating my ability to flexibly understand and resolve complex problems.

high.situation: We need to create a simple machine using an Arduino that can perform 2-digit binary addition
high.task_action: The process involves breaking down the problem into essential components: variable input, storage, addition, and representation of the result. Each component is then developed sequentially, ensuring compatibility with previous components to minimize troubleshooting. After assembly, adjustments are made for unforeseen errors. Finally, the system is evaluated for inefficiencies and iteratively improved until optimised.
high.result: The result is an optimised design that was efficiently created by simplifying the problem into its base components, and then individually tackling several smaller problems.

high.situation: Based off of the schematics we were provided we were tasked with building a toolbox out of either aluminium or steel sheets.

high.result: This preparation process resulted in the allowance of various tools and machines to achieve a common outcome.

high.situation: There is a deadline for an assignment for technical drawing, where we have to draw specific shapes and 3D objects in different perspectives, and utilising specific tools to draw perfectly without any imperfections.
high.task_action: We created a group of four individuals and started managing our time by carefully planning each drawing, taking in consideration how long each drawing will take. We also collected specific drawing tools, such as a drawing board, 30cm ruler, compass, triangle rulers, pencils and rubbers. Once these tools were collected and our time was carefully planned out, we were able to start our drawings in ease and comfort and was able to conduct drawings efficiently, perfectly and effortlessly.
high.result: The result of our actions was beneficial as it allowed us to complete the assignment well before the deadline, exactly a week before. This result allowed us to understand the importance of time and resource management, informing not only us but other peers the amount of problems that can be resolved utilising these forms of management.

high.situation: In the mechanical workshop my task was to create a toolbox. This was my first workshop in my first engineering subject at university. The task was to create a toolbox and needed skills in using machinery and measuring accurately. When creating the side pieces the aluminium sheet I guillotined was not square. This meant that when I guillotined the sheet into its two side pieces the parallel sides of the rectangles cut out were different.      TASK: The task needed was to problem solve how to make the rectangle’s sides parallel so the Toolbox would be in proportion.      ACTION: The action I took was to remeasure and scribe on a centre line and mark out all cut lines, bend lines and holes from the technical drawing referenced from the centre line.
high.result: The result was square side pieces with the correct measurements; this enabled the toolbox to be built in accordance with the technical drawing.

high.situation: IN THE MECHANICAL WORKSHOP MY TASK WAS TO CREATE A TOOLBOX. THIS WAS MY FIRST WORKSHOP IN MY FIRST ENGINEERING SUBJECT AT UNIVERSITY. THIS MEANT THAT WHEN I GUILLOTINED THE SHEET INTO ITS TWO SIDE PIECES THE PARALLEL SIDES OF THE RECTANGLES CUT OUT WERE DIFFERENT.      TASK: THE TASK NEEDED WAS TO PROBLEM SOLVE HOW TO MAKE THE RECTANGLE’S SIDES PARALLEL SO THE TOOLBOX WOULD BE IN PROPORTION.      ACTION: THE ACTION I TOOK WAS TO REMEASURE AND SCRIBE ON A CENTRE LINE AND MARK OUT ALL CUT LINES, BEND LINES AND HOLES FROM THE TECHNICAL DRAWING REFERENCED FROM THE CENTRE LINE.
high.result: THE RESULT WAS SQUARE SIDE PIECES WITH THE CORRECT MEASUREMENTS; THIS ENABLED THE TOOLBOX TO BE BUILT IN ACCORDANCE WITH THE TECHNICAL DRAWING.

high.situation: During the initial stages of assembling my toolbox I unfortunately made an incorrect cut using the guillotine, resulting in me missing a bottom panel during the beginning stages of assembly.

high.result: This resulted in me prioritising the panels I correctly cut out and allowed me to focus on the positives, slowly rebuilding my confidence as the sessions went on. I at first started with my side panels, scribing the measurements, folding and cutting out corners, repeating these steps for the second panels; and during the morning session on the 19th of march I was able to scribe and cut out my bottom panel, allowing me to finish my project on time. I have learnt to be more careful and focused when using the guillotine, but I have also learnt in the face of one mistake I should remain calm and figure out a way to rectify it, as there is always another way.

high.situation: My team and I were tasked to make a bridge capable of holding the weight of a filled plastic water bottle.
high.task_action: we began brainstorming ideas on the bridge design and research for viable strong designs that are not too complex and require minimum materials as they are limited. All which should be completed in the short span of 2 hours. All members communicated their up to date findings and aimed to improve the bridge as we got new information.
high.result: The bridge style of truss was agreed upon but we still had to choose a specific design. We weighed up our options with material usage, time to build and aesthetics in mind and agreed upon a Truss Howe bridge. This way we ticked all the boxes for our outlines and rules we had to follow.

high.situation: Our group needed to use teamwork and communication to articulate a plan for our civil modeling workshop so that we would efficiently complete our project on time and up to a satisfactory standard.
high.task_action: We communicated between our group to find a time slot where our members would have free time to meet up and work on the project in our own time rather than in-class time. There were times when group members were not able to physically attend, so we would communicate to assign them work on the digital report rather than the physical model so that they could contribute to the final project. Regarding teamwork, our group worked most efficiently when we had all of our members build the physical model at once and would work inefficiently when split up into individual tasks (for example, the risk assessment task). Similarly, splitting up our final digital report into sections allowed our team to work efficiently to finish it with detail and on time, whereas an individual report would take more energy and time to do.
high.result: The result is that our group used teamwork and communication to finish our project by using communication to distribute workload per individual so that each group member had an equal array of work to complete, as well as teamwork to combine our strengths and work efficiently on the physical project and digital report.

high.situation: Needed to shorten a block of code to make it more readable to tutors.

high.situation: During a recent project, I was part of a team of engineers working on the development of a new medical device. Our goal was to design a product that would improve patient care and meet all regulatory and safety standards. However, as we progressed through the design and testing phases, we encountered several challenges related to material compatibility, electrical safety, and compliance with industry standards. These challenges threatened to delay the project and increase costs.      TASK: My role in the team was to oversee the electrical engineering aspects of the project. My task was to ensure that the device met all safety and performance requirements while maintaining cost efficiency. I was responsible for coordinating with other engineers, suppliers, and quality assurance teams to resolve the challenges and keep the project on track.      ACTION: To address the challenges, I took the following actions:      	* Conducted a thorough analysis of the design and identified potential risks and areas for improvement.   	* Collaborated closely with the materials engineer to identify suitable materials that would meet safety and compatibility requirements.   	* Worked with the quality assurance team to develop rigorous testing protocols to ensure the device met all regulatory standards.   	* Communicated regularly with suppliers to source high-quality components that met our specifications and budget constraints.   	* Organized team meetings to discuss progress, share updates, and resolve any emerging issues.
high.result: As a result of these actions:      	* The project was completed on schedule and within budget.   	* The medical device met all regulatory and safety standards, and it passed rigorous testing protocols.   	* The team achieved a high level of collaboration and communication, which contributed to the project's success.   	* The successful completion of the project enhanced our company's reputation in the industry and opened up new opportunities for future projects in the medical device field.      Overall, my actions as an engineer helped ensure the successful development of the medical device, demonstrating the importance of taking on responsibilities and working effectively within a team to achieve project goals.

high.situation: For my ENGG1000 machining project, I was tasked to make an aluminum toolbox in the workshop over an 8-9 long work period. high.task_action: I decided to ask one of my dominators/supervisors for assistance and to show me how to properly use to tool for riveting. He was happy to show me, assisting me with the first rivet. RESULTS:      After teaching me, I was able to rivet the pieces on my own. My first few rivets were okay, but weren't as good as I hoped it to be, but my later rivets became better each time.

high.situation: I was required to draw 3 different diagrams of a 3D-printed object, in order to communicate the size and properties of the object on paper.
high.task_action: First the object was drawn in 2 point perspective, utilising two vanishing points two effectively convey the 3 dimensional nature of the object and a separate, detachable piece in two separate drawings. This 3D nature was further expanded on in my isometric drawing, where the x and z axes of the object were each drawn 30 degrees from the horizontal. Finally, I drew a multi-view drawing of the 3D-printed object, using 2D perspectives from it's top, front and side views to portray it's shape from different angles in a detailed manner. In order to ensure the readers understanding of the object, a detachable piece of the object was drawn separately, using appropriate labelling to communicate it's cylindrical nature. The drawing was made in a 3:1 scale and included appropriately labelled dimensioning to accurately convey the size of the object.
high.result: 5 drawings were made, each with their appropriate features in order to communicate the shape, size and nature of the provided object.

high.result: Their assistance and guidance has helped me develop a better understand about code and how it can be utilised. They have helped me complete the tasks that we were assigned to do. high.situation: Our project team was frequently running behind schedule and over budget owing to inadequate time and resource management procedures.
high.task_action: Recognising the seriousness of the issue, I organised a meeting with the team to openly address our time and resource management difficulties. We detailed each project phase's precise tasks , as well as crucial milestones and dependencies. Next, we thoroughly examined our resource distribution. high.result: Several beneficial outcomes have resulted from our proactive approach. Finally, team morale and collaboration increased as everyone felt more empowered and responsible for their roles in the project's success. Overall, our efforts at time and resource management resulted in a more efficient and productive project execution process.

high.situation: When I was in high school, during my senior years I only presented by myself and digitally (i.e recording myself speaking and presenting), resulting in my presentation skills to be lacking when presenting physically.      TASK:       A few weeks into uni, one of my units required me to form a group to physically present a topic to the class a week after a topic was assigned. A new topic would be assigned the week after we presented, where the group members would be different people, and be presented the week after.       ACTION:      After allocating our roles in my first group and completing my part of the slide, where I wrote long key points, I initially wrote a transcript for myself to memorise when presenting. During my second group presentation, I wrote a lot more information in my slide than the previous presentation. When presenting, I didn’t write a transcript, but instead thought of what I’m going to say beforehand. When it was my third group presentation, the information on the slide was shortened by a lot unlike my previous presentations, and when presenting, I created a transcript for only my starting sentence and improvised the rest.
high.result: When presenting my first presentation, my performance was not great as I was stuttering and forgot what I memorised in my transcript due to nervousness, additionally I was not directly looking at the audience. In my second presentation, I improved significantly better when talking, however due to relying on the information from the slide, I frequently looked at the slide rather than at the audience when speaking. In my third presentation, while there was a bit of stuttering, overall my speaking skills were satisfactory and since there wasn’t much information on the slide, I looked at the audience when presenting.

high.situation: CULTURE SHOCK AND ACADEMIC STRUGGLE      TASK: Regain focus on my studies and adapt to the new academic environment while adjusting to a new culture in a foreign country.      ACTION:      	* IDENTIFYING THE CHALLENGES: I recognized my difficulties stemmed from a combination of factors. Initially, cultural differences made it hard to understand lecture styles and expectations. Additionally, homesickness and adjusting to a new social circle impacted my ability to concentrate on academics.   	* SEEKING SUPPORT: I reached out to the university's international student support center. They provided resources for cultural adjustment, offered workshops on academic writing specific to the university's style, and connected me with other international students.   	* CREATING A STUDY ROUTINE: I developed a dedicated study schedule, blocking out specific times for each subject. I also sought out a quiet study space on campus or in the library to minimize distractions.   	* FORMING A STUDY GROUP: I connected with classmates from my courses to form a study group. This helped with understanding complex topics through collaborative learning and discussions. Additionally, socializing with classmates offered a sense of community and helped combat homesickness.
high.result: * IMPROVED ACADEMIC PERFORMANCE: Implementing a study routine and seeking help from resources improved my understanding of the coursework. This resulted in better grades on assignments and exams.   	* ENHANCED CULTURAL ADJUSTMENT: Connecting with the university support center and other international students provided a network of support and helped me adapt to the new cultural environment.   	* INCREASED CONFIDENCE: Successfully navigating academic challenges and cultural differences boosted my confidence in my ability to succeed in this new environment.      This experience showcased the importance of proactive problem-solving and adaptability. By seeking support and establishing a study routine, I was able to overcome the initial hurdles of studying abroad and excel academically while adjusting to a new culture and now it does not seem as hard to me anymore.

high.situation: In my role as a project coordinator, I was responsible for managing a critical software update rollout across the company's main operational platforms. The update was intended to enhance data security and processing speed. However, after initial deployment, we encountered significant compatibility issues with existing hardware which threatened to delay the entire project.      TASK: My task was to quickly identify a solution to the compatibility problems without pushing back the project timeline. This required a detailed assessment of the issues and collaboration with both the software and hardware teams to ensure a feasible solution could be implemented swiftly.      ACTION: I organized an emergency meeting with the software developers, hardware technicians, and IT support staff to brainstorm possible fixes. We discussed several alternatives, including temporary hardware upgrades and software patches. After a thorough analysis, we decided to implement a phased rollout of the software update, which included immediate software patches to address the most critical compatibility issues while planning for minor hardware upgrades in the longer term.
high.result: The phased approach allowed us to proceed with the software update without significant delays. The immediate patches resolved 90% of the compatibility issues, and the hardware upgrades scheduled over the following three months addressed the remaining concerns. This strategy not only kept the project on schedule but also minimized downtime and operational disruption. The successful management of this issue led to a 15% improvement in data processing speeds and enhanced security measures across the company’s platforms.

high.task_action: I set daily quotas that I would need to meet and when I forgot to complete work, or the work is highly urgent/important, I would set alarms on my phone for times when I would be free so that I would not forget.      To do this, I first sorted each of my tasks for all of my units upon receiving them, by both importance and urgency. I then completed the tasks at the top of the list and worked my way downwards. At the end of each week's work, I would evaluate:       - How much work was left to complete       - How much work I had completed / How much effort I had put into my studies      - How well I had adhered to the planner      - Whether any part/s of the planner were inefficient/ineffective      - My mental health/stress regarding it
high.result: This method was very effective, however was also very time consuming and thus stressful. Although I kept ahead of most of my work, whenever I fell behind, my motivation to work would lower as the amount of work I needed to complete stacked up.       To improve this method, I would create a physical checklist where you can tick off each individual task (my checklist was a simple dot-point list). This would help with the stresses of work piling up, as it shows that work is still being completed.       More significantly, I would separate the sorting list into individual units, so that I can see how much work is left for each individual class. high.situation: In the robotics club, we signed up for the QUT robotics competition. This involves developing an autonomous robot capable of competing in a race against other bots from other universities (UNSW, Sydney, UTS, etc). Our team is composed of students from different engineering disciplines.      TASK:      As the lead programmer, my primary responsibility was to design a fundamental layout for how we would program it. My other responsibilities involved designing and decision-making. This task involved integrating computer vision, path-finding algorithms, and real-time data processing that could adjust the robot's movement accordingly.             ACTION:      To address these challenges, I took the following actions:      1.      TEAM COLLABORATION: Organising weekly meetings to ensure alignment between the software and hardware teams.      2.      SOFTWARE DESIGN: Develop software architecture that allows for easy actuator integration, making the system scalable and flexible.      3.      NEURAL NETWORK IMPLEMENTATION: We implemented a Neural Network that could detect obstacles, arrows, and other robots. We spent hours optimising the neural network to avoid overfitting, using pure training data and optimising multithreading with the main program.      4. Continue testing: Set up E2E testing on the main program and Blackbox and Whitebox testing to ensure everything is ready for production.      Throughout this process, I emphasised collaboration, leveraging the diverse skill sets within the team to tackle complex problems that were raised. I also ensured that the code quality was maintained to high standards by giving every developer their dev branch, e.g. (dev-Ewen), Which they needed to make a PR to the main branch to implement the changes, to merge the branch into the main branch their code required to be reviewed by two people and approved by both of them to merge, facilitating future modifications and improvements. I expanded my experience working with Neural Networks. I decided to use a CNN with VGG (Visual Geometry Group), with the following config: INIT_LR: 1e-4 (The desired learning rate at the end of the warmup phase), Epoch: 16 with a batch size of 32. Which yielded good results for the first round of training:      Epoch 1 Summary:      ----------------      Training Metrics:      Loss: 2.2142      Bounding box loss: 0.0961      Class label loss: 2.1181      Bounding box accuracy: 50.90%      Class label accuracy: 54.49%             Validation Metrics:      loss (Validation): 1.9421      bounding box loss (Validation): 0.0627      class label loss (Validation): 1.8794      bounding box accuracy (Validation): 90.48%      class label accuracy (Validation): 38.10%      ----------------      While testing the production branch of the code on the Jetson Nano, I encountered mainly compatibility errors. To mitigate the errors, we built OpenCV (With custom config) and TensorFlow from a source, which worked better than expected. We operated our system of Ubuntu 20.04 and made it controllable via systemd. One of the most challenging aspects was debugging and the testing phase, where the team brainstorms ideas, evaluates potential strategies and selects the most feasible design. As the lead programmer, I was responsible for translating these ideas into actionable plans and providing information on how achievable the hardware was with the system. On the software side, we originally wanted to do a linear system for data processing with OpenCV and TensorFlow (Running all the code under the same process without any threads. By doing this, we had to wait for TensorFlow to process its task whilst OpenCV had already completed it (took 1.3s – Massive delay). To solve this issue, we decided to run TensorFlow under a thread; this means whilst OpenCV is doing its task on the main process, it can continue to output its data without waiting for TensorFlow. When TensorFlow has finished processing its data, it returns the data to the main process through the thread, and the data is managed through there, and a new frame is sent into TensorFlow. Through this experience, I learned the importance of communication and leadership. Leading the team of programmers was crucial to maintaining clear and open communication channels, delegating tasks according to each member's strengths and motivating the team to the common goal.
high.result: The outcomes of these actions were significant:      1.      COMPLETIVE PERFORMANCE: Our robot must complete the race, demonstrating optimal navigation and obstacle avoidance capabilities. We needed to complete the course within a specific time frame to ensure that we would be in the upper echelon of the competition.      2.      PERSONAL GROWTH: The experience enhanced my problem-solving capabilities, technical knowledge, programming skills, and leadership skills. This project also enhanced my resilience skills when issues occurred.      3.      FUTURE OPPORTUNITIES: The project has provided an opportunity to network with other engineers and gain industry knowledge.

high.result: My efforts ultimately culminated in a functional, durable toolbox that received kudos from those around me. The process taught me valuable skills in regards to using tools in the workplace and allowed me to understand my abilities in relation to machining. high.situation: I am a cultural dance instructor and during the COVID-19 lockdown, I faced the challenge of transitioning my teaching and choreography sessions to online. This required adapting to a completely new way of instructing and engaging with my students, as well as navigating technical challenges inherent in remote teaching.      TASK: Despite the limitations of remote learning, my primary task was to ensure that I could maintain the quality of teaching and choreography for my students. Additionally, we had to prepare them for an upcoming competition that was scheduled shortly after the lockdown ended. This meant not only maintaining their skills but also ensuring that they were competition-ready despite the unconventional circumstances.      ACTION: I adopted several strategies to approach and tackle this challenge. Firstly, I focused on developing my patience and communication skills, realising that teaching over video calls requires clearer and more detailed explanations. I also dedicated extra time to individual feedback and support to compensate for the lack of in-person interaction. As the competition drew nearer, I intensified and became stricter on punctuality and performance, ensuring that despite the limitations of remote learning, my students were on track and competition-ready. I provided personalised feedback and guidance to each dancer, focusing on areas of improvement and refining our routines to perfection.
high.result: Despite the initial challenges and the abrupt transition to online teaching, all of our efforts paid off. When the lockdown ended and the competition took place, my students showcased a remarkable performance. Out of five competing teams, we secured second place, a testament to the hard work and resilience of both myself and my students. The transition into remote teaching not only allowed us to adapt to unforeseen circumstances but also enabled us to emerge stronger and more capable as a team, showing greater potential within them. We successfully navigated the challenges posed by the pandemic and proved that with determination and adaptability, we could overcome any obstacle.

high.situation: In Year 12 our engineering studies class had an assignment to create a rocket and reach high altitudes by modifying and innovating the design of the rocket.
high.task_action: The rocket design started with a 2-litre bottle without any modifications, resulting in a height of 15 meters. After receiving a baseline height, we attached a nose cone to the top of the rocket. We used a styrofoam dome, which reduced the height to 13.1 meters. So, we innovated the design to the conical top half of a Coke bottle, which enhanced the height to 17.7 meters.      Following this, we constructed our first set of rocket fins, consisting of four elliptical wooden fins, which achieved a height of 9.7 meters but proved excessively heavy. We then redesigned the fins innovating it by using corflute material and changing the amount of fins to three elliptical fins, resulting in an improved trajectory of 16.4 meters.      Further innovation led to a redesign of the fin shape into a trapezoidal fin, which allowed for rocket to reach a height of 20.3 meters, showing successful innovation. Our final innovation involved the addition of an epoxy resin cone to the top which replaced the Coke bottle conical top, this enhanced the tip and provided a counterweight, causing the best result of 27.5 meters.
high.result: The results showed how the innovation process is used to improve the rocket altitude. this was proven successful with the final rocket launch being 27.5m

high.situation: While developing the Rock Paper Scissors Lizard Spock game expansion, I encountered a significant challenge in scaling the traditional decision-making algorithm from the classic three gestures to include additional gestures like Lizard, Spock, and more, up to a total of 11 gestures. The complexity of the decision tree was growing exponentially, making the game logic unwieldy and error-prone.      TASK: My task was to redesign the decision-making algorithm to handle the increased complexity efficiently without sacrificing the game's performance and scalability. The goal was to ensure the algorithm could reliably determine the winner in a game with up to 11 different gestures.      ACTION: I initiated the redesign by researching various algorithmic strategies that could handle larger datasets and complex decision trees effectively. After consulting with my project advisor and reviewing academic papers, I decided to implement a modular arithmetic approach, which promised to simplify the winner determination process. I developed a mathematical model where each gesture was assigned a unique value, and the winner could be determined through simple arithmetic operations based on these values. I then coded the new algorithm, integrated it into the existing game framework, and conducted multiple rounds of testing to ensure its accuracy and efficiency.
high.result: The implementation of the modular arithmetic-based algorithm was a success. It not only reduced the complexity of the game logic significantly but also improved the execution speed. The new algorithm could effortlessly determine the winner among the 11 gestures, maintaining game fairness and enhancing user experience. The testing phase confirmed its reliability, as it accurately determined the winner in all scenarios without any errors. This approach also enhanced the game's maintainability, making it easier to add more gestures in the future if needed.

I then transferred these learnings into some simple flow chart diagrams which represented the required structure of the game and how it would function. Finally I also used my existing programming knowledge to implement the solution, which was by assigning different integer values to each action (e.g. paper was 1) and then developing a formula which could be used to determine if the computer or human won.
high.result: The result was a successful program which could add any amount of actions for all the variants and allow the human to play the computer using the flow chart game logic. This allowed for me to play and test the game with up to 11 variants, and theoretically more could be added too.

This tested areas related to my chosen topic on 'the impact of engineering on society and the environment', including, material management (impact on the environment) and quality of the build (impact on society).      Task: I individually built my toolbox and thus had the responsibility to manage time and materials. Although I had to be relatively quick to finish the project, I simultaneously had to take my time in order to not waste my materials. In addition I had to make sure that the project was of a high quality finish so that it could function the way it was intended and function well.       Action: In order to not have a negative impact on the environment, I had to make sure that I used my materials wisely. Firstly within the room, the students all cut up large pieces of metal in order to not waste what was there. However individually, I always made sure that I measured multiple times before cutting. This allowed me to understand exactly what I was to be left with, know that it would look correct, and know where to cut. This was important because I wanted to  minimise my waste material by cutting off as little as possible, and make sure I would not have to use more material if I had to start over due to the cut being wrong. In addition, the workshop minimised the impact on the environment by reusing wood as a base plate, having a scrap metal bin where metal could be reused and used a more wise material - that being metal and not plastic and so I participated in these workshop practices.       In order to have a good impact on society through engineering, I took my time with the project, making sure not to rush anything, to have a good quality product by the end of the workshop. This allowed me to have a high quality product in which it could last a long time and function just as it was intended. By doing this, the project can positively impact the society by having a durable, lasting, good product to use that can be trusted. This is especially true for workers as it can make their job easier by not only having the product in the first place but by also having it of a good quality and satisfies them. By having this product of a high quality, it sets me up to continue on this path of quality throughout my engineering journey and thus this will positively impact society by having a decent engineer work for the better of society.      Having a product that does not negatively impact the environment also in turn positively impacts society as the product made will make society live in a better environment but it also lets them live in peace of mind knowing the product they are using was made with the environment in mind - attempting to help heal it.
high.result: By putting these actions together throughout the workshop time, I was able to confidently learn engineering practices and make my aluminium toolbox while attempting to make it have a better impact on the environment rather than using way too much material and wasting lots of material. In turn by doing this I positively impacted society by not damaging the environment further and also gifting this to another member of society so they can use it and make their work easier, to which, brought them satisfaction.
