We prioritised the solutions based on how crucial they were to the project's outcome.
high.result: We were able to narrow down the list of potential design solutions created during the brainstorming session to the best one thanks to our teamwork. We took into account aspects like water saving and ease of maintenance while choosing a design that achieved a balance between utility and practicality. To ensure that the selected design continued to be developed, each team member was given particular tasks to complete.

high.situation: We all had to partake in creating a rube goldberg machine and each person had to contribute towards creating the machine.
high.task_action: Undergoing the task, we each brought in the products and materials that we agreed on bringing. We then began creating the machine that was discussed on the Miro board we have created using the materials that everyone has brought and everyone had a part of the machine they needed to make. After each section, we tested out whether the part of the section was successful or not, i.e., failure attempts. We had multiple failures and had a rework the machine a few times.      Results: Ultimately, after multiple attempts and reworks of the machine, we created a machine that worked very well. Everyone worked hard on each part and we were happy with the results.

high.situation: we needed to work on re-planing  our perliminary plan of RGM, becuase of the the inconveniance of making equipments for 5th and 6 th event
high.task_action: Undergoing a brainstorming meeting online on Sunday afternoon; listing the materials or things which that member obtain in his place; forming a draft of approximate plan; Adding the possible mechanism methods on each session or event.
high.result: we selected the 3 mechanism methods from the list of 6 possible method and generated in new perliminary plan for 5th and 6th event. Then we were down to continue the rest of the componet videos

high.situation: During our group tasks and weekly tutorials, I faced challenges related to teamwork and communication specifically regarding professional behavior. Over the past 13 weeks, one of our team members, Nibir Sandipan, consistently failed to engage and contribute effectively to our group tasks. This lack of involvement is evident in multiple instances, such as during our Week 7 presentation preparation and presentation itself, and the final presentation. Nibir's lack of professional behavior, such as keeping his camera off during video calls, not assisting with assigned tasks, not taking initiative to make suggestions on how to complete tasks, significantly impacted the team's dynamics and workflow.      TASK:       As a group (behrouz 6), our task was to collaborate week to week on various components of our group project 'The Rube Goldberg Machine', ensuring that each member contributed equally to meet our deadlines and maintain the quality of our work. This included preparing presentations, participating in video calls, and ensuring that each part of the project was coherent and well-coordinated. It was essential for all of us to demonstrate professional behavior, especially active participation, responsibility, and effective communication.      ACTION:       To address Nibir's lack of engagement and professional behavior we:      - Encouraged participation to  be present during our weekly video calls, even when Nibir kept his camera off and did not speak a word.      - Provided specific feedback to individuals as a group and assigned clear responsibilities to ensure everyone knew their tasks.      - Conveyed the importance of each member's contribution to the overall success of the project.      - Unfortunately took on additional work ourselves to cover for the tasks that Nibir did not complete or give a decent attempt, ensuring that the project would still be completed to the best of our abilities.      Despite these efforts, Nibir's participation and professional behavior did not improve.       For the Week 7 presentation, Nibir did not assist in creating the slides, leaving Ibrahim and myself to cover his share of the work.       In the final presentation, Nibir contributed to only one slide, which was differing with the rest of the presentation both in content authenticity and design. This then required additional effort from Ibrahim and myself to edit and try to integrate the slide effectively. Ultimately this affected the overall quality and flow of our presentation. Throughout this process, I maintained a professional demeanor by consistently communicating, taking responsibility, and ensuring the project's success despite the challenges we all faced.
high.result: The final result of all of this was a mixed outcome. Whilst we managed to complete the project on time and maintain a certain level of quality in both our video and presentation, the lack of contribution from Nibir affected the team's morale and overall productivity. It also highlighted the challenges of having to relying on team members who do not fully engage with the group's efforts or pull their weight.       Finally, we have raised this issue with our tutor and unit convenor, seeking a resolution to ensure a fair assessment of each team member's contribution. This experience highlighted the importance of professional behavior in teamwork out of respect for peers. It can be a tricky situation to be in, both sides could be struggling, the team has to pick up extra work due to the inability of a team member and the individual could be going through a traumatic experience that impedes them from completing their responsibilities.

high.situation: As a biomolecular science student, I've spent the last two and a half years working in small groups of 2-3 people. I decided to join an engineering unit and challenge myself by working remotely and with a team of more than three people. Starting in week two of the semester, I realised it was difficult to work in groups of six. First, because the class was offline, it was extremely difficult to understand or interact well with one another.
high.task_action: I enrolled one week after the semester began, and another student joined two weeks after that. It was becoming alittle difficult to collaborate online in large number of group. However, gradually through great teamwork and communication, we were able to improve and progress in our teamwork. Our primary objective was to create an RGM machine with 12 events while working online. As a team, we began to employ our team management skills; initially, we shared contact information in order to create a chat platform similar to Whatsapp, where we could easily contact one another.Then we all agreed on an online platform, such as Miro, that would allow us to collaborate and see who was executing which tasks. Then we started assigning jobs among the six of us, rotating them every week. For example, if I was allocated to do the minutes and agenda, others would be assigned to complete the weekly presentation, and two more would be assigned to present in class. We discovered that this was a crucial strategy for effectively managing the team; yet, there were instances when two or three members were missing.This caused some members to work more than others.       However, as a team, we all came together and addressed the problem, and we came up with a method in which even if they are absent, they can make time during the week, even half an hour, to go over the miro and be updated, as well as provide comments or editing. Alternatively, each week, a team member would participate in updating via WhatsApp, which proved to be highly efficient. We made sure that everyone had the opportunity to accomplish something different each week; yet, by the conclusion of week 6, because everyone had to do a variety of activities, we identified our group members' strengths and weaknesses.      We assessed it as a group and assigned a task beginning in week 9: if a person is particularly creative, they will work on creating the presentation, and if a member is really strong at presenting verbally with confidence, they will present in class. Using all of the methods we've learnt and perfected to work well in such a huge team. Everyone was ensured equal participation and involvement throughout the process of developing the RGM, which required everyone to include at least two activities of their choice. This allowed each team member to express their ingenuity and new ideas. By the end of each class, we ensured that everyone was satisfied with what we had planned/designed for our final RGM. However, if there was a disagreement among members, we ensured that it was resolved by a thorough assessment of the circumstances and negotiation that satisfied all members.       RESULTS:  With effective team management skills, our team ensured that the RGM's design and planning processes ran successfully. Each week, we took a step by step approach to designing the 12 events with the cooperation of each member. We all agreed to conduct two activities per member, each lasting 4-5 seconds.Throughout the implementation process, our team chose to evaluate the reliability of our RGM in real life. After everyone got an opportunity to test their events, we spoke about their reliability and success/failure.If an issue/failure arose during this process, we as a team devised robust setups, explored how to choose better materials for each event, and fine-tuned component alignment to improve reliability and consistency. Overall, working on this project as a group helped everyone to learn, grow, and progress in all facets of becoming a great engineer. Working as a team allowed us to learn about each other's shortcomings and talents, particularly mine. However, there was a challenge at first working in a team of six people, but along the process, we not only learned about ourselves, but also about our team members' effective skills.

high.situation: I was falling behind on my allocated parts of the group presentation and required assistance/more time
high.task_action: Everyone in the team was allocated the task of coming up with 2 actions for the Rube Gold-berg Machine (RGM). However, I was unsure how to start my process and spent quite a lot of time researching/brainstorming than other members. Consequently, I had reached a point where I would not be able to finish off my part of the presentation by the deadline in the workshop. Instead of delivering a half baked product, I reached out to my group and explained what had happened, apologized for the lack of content on my half, and requested assistance. After I had received help, I wanted to further take accountability for my shortcomings and offered to work more efficiently in the next meeting and also take on additional work in order to return the favors I had received from my team.
high.result: The team and I were able to finish off the entire presentation and I took on additional work the next meeting to compensate and take accountability for my lack of work done in the previous meeting.

high.situation: In my ENGG2050 unit, one of the very first tasks we had to accomplish was creating a conceptual solution for an Internet of Things (IoT) Tea Maker. When this was first explained to us, I felt very lost, as I hadn’t done any sort of design similar to this. I felt even more overwhelmed when we were told that this design was going to be required in order for us to complete another assessment later in the semester (A6 Video Presentation). I felt as though I was going to fail the unit before it had even started. However, this is where my chosen soft skill came into play. My skills in “creating new solution concepts” were still very limited as I had only completed two semesters so far and didn’t have too much experience in applying myself to create real world solutions. So, during the semester I had to figure out a way to use and further hone my skills in creating new solutions. Doing this was going to be vital, not only for my final grade but also in order for me not to feel too overwhelmed by what was expected of me.
high.task_action: The task presented to me at the start of my 3rd semester at university in my ENGG2050 unit was to create a conceptual solution for an Internet of Things (IoT) Tea Maker. I had to not only meet the different criterias given to us but also ensure that if my solution was to be implemented in the real world, it would work. Whilst I didn’t have to delve into the actual physical implementation of the Tea Maker (i.e. the electrical, mechanical and other components), I had to create a solution that if given to an actual manufacturer or company, they would be able to implement the solution. This meant that I had to consider every possibility of what could go wrong and describe the details of the IoT Tea Maker in as much detail as I could. I was able to complete this through the help of my soft skill and the weekly SGTAs.      The very first step that I took was to ask questions in the public discussion forum on iLearn. As this task was supposed to replicate a real world scenario as accurately as possible, we were tasked with asking questions on the discussion forum, where the unit convenor would reply from the perspective of a customer. From this we were expected to extract relevant information to identify the needs of the customer. I made sure to ask even the briefest questions I had. Additionally, not only were we given responses to our own questions, but also the questions that other people asked and their responses. From the discussion forum, I copied every question/answer into a document and underneath jotted down a couple of sentences on how I would be able to meet the customer’s requirement for that dot point. Here I was able to use my soft skill (creating new solution concepts) from my previous experiences of creating software solutions in other units. From experience, I knew that the very first step in creating a solution was to identify the requirements. I knew that these requirements were going to form the basis for the more detailed functions that I was going to have to describe. By calling on my soft skill, I was able to reduce the initial feeling of overwhelmingness I felt when the task was given to us.       The next action I took was discussing and brainstorming possible designs in a group. Despite having previous experience in working in groups, I was still a bit nervous when we had to discuss our ideas with people who we had just met. However, this discussion was greatly beneficial as the group discussion highlighted different ideas and solutions for the tea maker that I had not considered. As we all came from different disciplines, the group discussion was very effective and we were able to gain insight from different perspectives. This action helped me realise the important role that group discussions play when creating a new solution. I was able to improve my chosen soft skill through this.      Another action that I took was to consider the worst case scenario for certain aspects of the tea maker. For each individual function that I had described for my tea maker, I decided to question it and ask myself what would happen in the worst case (e.g. One of the functions was to store milk and my worst case scenario question was, what would happen if someone stored another liquid that could result in a possible accident?). This form of testing and evaluating my solution enabled me to see how I would be able to improve my solution. Moreover it taught me what scenarios to consider for the next time when I have to create a solution. This action helped me improve my “creating a solution” skill as I was able to learn how to reflect, question and improve my ideas.
high.result: My efforts were intended for me to use and improve my skills in creating a solution by creating a solution for my ENGG2050 IoT Tea Maker. Despite having limited experience with this soft skill and my discipline being Software Engineering (in which everything is very binary i.e. yes/no, 1/0) I was able to create a solution that could hypothetically be given to a manufacturer as an initial design. Furthermore, because of the actions that I implemented, I was able to improve my skills in creating a solution. Such a skill is going to be needed in future units and also my career. Knowing how to effectively create a solution concept is the basis of implementing successful engineering solutions. By improving my skills and completing my IoT Tea Maker solution to the best of my ability, I was content in my work and the initial daunting feeling I had when coming into this unit was gone.

high.situation: Throughout the first few weeks of the ENGG3050 unit, we have been presented with a wide range of leadership, management, and entrepreneurial skills and outlooks. In Engineering I’ve learnt the importance of how a team works together and how to produce a quality and efficient team for any task. The role and importance of leadership can involve anyone within a team and allows for problem solving and idea generation. Both the Week 1 SGTA and Case Study 1 allowed me to reflect on my own and others leadership styles and how different styles can benefit teams in specific ways, which allowed me to understand how to effectively lead engineering endeavours.      Task: There have been many tasks across my engineering degree where I’ve had to consider the implications of teamwork and how to lead a team, whether I was merely part of that team or was attempting to lead it. The ability to lead a team is a difficult and relentless task, attempting to organise who in the team needs to achieve specific tasks in order to produce the final product. In week 1 of this semester we looked at the specific types of leadership styles and each of their advantages and disadvantages which allowed me to reflect on what I should be aiming for as a leader. I believe that a democratic and transformational style of leadership works the best in ensuring a team works well together. It allows everyone to input their ideas and thoughts whilst pushing for improved team efforts and performance. To further this I was tasked with looking at the different leadership styles in General Electric (GE) to investigate the different ways the CEO’s pushed their companies and pushed the endeavours of their engineers and how each of their leading styles impacting the company as a whole showing a general idea of what is crucial for a business to perform well.      Action: Over the course of my degree I have found myself in positions where I am part of a team trying to produce a result. This includes ENGG1050, ENGG2000 and a few others to name a few, where working with a group of fellow engineers we are to design and produce some result. Although I wasn’t necessarily appointed the leader in these cases I felt the need to often step in and attempt to solve conflict, improve team morale and ensure everyone in the team gets a fair say during the creative processes and of course the actual building of whatever we had planned such as the Rube Goldberg Machine or the Marble Machine. Others also stepped in at times to lead and ensure everyone was on the right path to getting the project done. I found it was beneficial and very important to involve a wide variety of leadership styles at each stage of the process to enable different ideas flows, ensuring work was done and submitted on time and that everyone was enjoying themselves to boost the overall productivity and mental health as such across the projects. It has been very interesting now going into depth on these leadership styles in ENGG3050, looking at real life cases of where they have been used and the overall results of each. I’m excited to push forward to ENGG3000 to again be part of an awesome team and produce an amazing result whilst trialling different leadership options and how to push the engineering endeavours to even greater heights.
high.result: Pushing myself to be a leader in a range of engineering endeavours has really helped open my eyes as to how a team functions and how to really push for the best quality outcome in any given case. Being able to put the team back on track when getting side tracked during class time and pushing brainstorming sessions to narrow down what is a viable idea like in the marble machine. Without this leadership no one was willing to push the team forward and to come up with a conclusive idea. Specifically during the ENGG2000 project, at some point as a team we didn’t know how to proceed with our design. I sat down with the team and asked them what parts of the box they wanted to contribute and suggested what each person could do. We discussed as a team which allowed us to proceed with our engineering endeavour. I decided to lead by example and proceed with my part. I would like to see how others perceive this leadership role and how they would implement their own ideas to help achieve that leadership and pushing those engineering endeavours. I will look forward to taking the ideas learnt in the ENGG3050 class and using those leadership skills that we have been learning and applying them during the ENGG3000 workshop, next years thesis writing and my future career in engineering.

high.situation: Utilising the transferable and employability skill, the importance of accountability and applying it to university work and adapting it to improve everyday life.
high.task_action: In ENGG3050 I’m being taught about the importance of having and improving leadership and entrepreneurial skills to becoming a successful and productive engineer. I’m a big procrastinator. If there is something else I can do to distract myself from university work or anything important, I will do it to push back the hard work to a later time. This ultimately ends with me wasting my day and having to cram all my work in at towards the end of the day and night. This significantly impacts my next day as I’m tired and not as productive causing myself to fall even further behind as I’m pushing back my important tasks again. However, in the first seven weeks of ENGG3050 I have slowly learnt and implemented the transferable and employability skill, the importance of accountability and successfully applied it to my university work and adapted it to everyday life. I have now learnt to tackle the hard work first so I can enjoy myself later guilt free later. By putting my phone on do not disturb and sometimes even putting it in another room, I was able to keep myself accountable, focused and ultimately finish my important work so I can have fun later. I write down a check list to keep myself on track and once I complete that checklist I’m free to do what I want. Another strategy I implement to keep myself more accountable is keeping my parents involved with what I have on. By doing this I am constantly asked how I’m going with an assignment or test study, which is constantly reminding me I can’t escape the work and I should just do it so I dont have to feel guilty and lie about doing it.
high.result: The transferable and employability skill, the importance of accountability has resulted in the significant improvement of my wellbeing as a whole. I’m able to get a full 8hrs of sleep and wake up early enough to do my work and still have fun and do other things I want to do in the afternoon. My mental health has improved as a I feel less stress and I am able to enjoy myself without feeling guilty of having work to complete. My grades have also improved as my overall quality of work as improved. Thus, the transferable and employability skill, accountability has successfully improved me across all aspects of my life.

high.situation: *   Recently, I went Scuba Diving as a type 1 diabetic. This was not my first time diving with diabetes, and at the beginning of the dive I gave a small presentation about this. I talked about what it meant to dive as a diabetic, including the potential risks and what I would need from people in case a situation arises. However, during the dive a complication arose due to my diabetes. The group I was diving with felt confused and unprepared, they were unsure of what they should do. This made me realise something, that I didn’t do an adequate job informing people of their role to play in case of an emergency. I needed to prepare and script a better presentation, to explain my diabetes to my dive partners.
high.task_action: *   A close friend of mine is one of the best cave divers in the world. He put me in touch with one of his students, another type 1 diabetic. We sat on a phone call for about 4 hours, brainstorming all of the potential problems I could have underwater due to my diabetes and what those problems would mean for me, for the people I’m diving with, and how those problems would be managed or mitigated. Once we came up with that list, we worked out which of those problems I could manage on my own, and which of those problems required help from other people. From there, we created clear and concise explanations, including props and images, to explain only the problems that would require help from other people.
high.result: *   We came up with a new presentation, one which did a much better job of preparing people. We did this by explaining the condition, what it means for me as a diver, the important hand symbol for diving with diabetes and what it means for a dive, the potential things I need from people, and stressing that certain problems just need time for me to work through them on my own, and that if I require assistance I will signal people for it. Using this method, I was able to create a much more effective presentation. I tried this out recently and did a mock simulation of a diabetes problem underwater, and no one from the diving group was confused or felt unequipped to handle this situation. This presentation was a success.

high.situation: In the third week of this semester, I was given a substantial assignment that was due in week 7. Initially, I did not prioritise it because the due date was so far away. By week 5 I had only completed about 15% of the work. I became overwhelmed with other pressing assignments and placed this one on the backburner, even as the due date rapidly approached.
high.task_action: As week 6 arrived and the assignment was due the next week, I realised I needed to make this my top priority. I began chipping away at the work, determined not to submit it late and incur a 5% penalty. The assignment was much larger than I had anticipated, requiring extensive research and writing for each of the 10 questions, ultimately taking around 2hr per question. To manage the workload, I made significant sacrifices, staying up late and working through the Easter long weekend to make progress. I had to cancel some of my mid-week plans in week 7 to focus solely on completing this large assignment. On the day before the due date, I was on campus and found myself preemptively completing class work to allow me to go home early work on the remaining sections at home.
high.result: Through sheer perseverance, urgency, and a strong will to deliver, I was able to complete the entire 6000-word assignment by 12:55am, submitting it with just 4 minutes to spare. This required a high degree of resourcefulness and flexibility, as I had to adapt my schedule and priorities to ensure I could devote the necessary time and effort to this assignment.      Whilst the experience was extremely stressful, the outcome was successful. I was able to submit the assignment on time without incurring any late penalties. This experience has taught me valuable lessons about planning, organisation, and the importance of not becoming complacent when faced with a daunting task. In the future, I will aim to better assess the scope of an assignment and allocate my time accordingly, rather than leaving it until the last minute.

high.situation: At home, my space is in the garage. I have my computer, my tools/materials, musical instruments and have carved out my own little space because our house is quite small and everyone is living on top of eachother. Because our house is quite small, the garage is also the primary storage location for my mum, her partner and my sister, and as people accumulate random items my space in the house is filled with other peoples mess, this is a source of conflict in our household.
high.task_action: A potential solution didn’t jump out at me naturally to resolve this storage issue/conflict, I was working on welding together a flatbar in the garage (an obstacle for skateboarding) and my back was hurting because I didn’t have a workbench for these types of activities and I had to bend over to work on the ground. When I stood up, back aching, it occurred to me that if I used the materials in the garage to build a workbench there would be more room to store peoples clutter on the shelves, I would be utilising the offcuts taking up room in the garage and I wouldn’t be hurting my back every time I wanted to undertake a personal project.             So I put my half finished flatbar aside and spoke to my family to see what they would think of my idea, and they liked it! I finished the workbench in the following days and then went on to build a few shelves in the garage with leftover materials.
high.result: Upon completion I was so excited. My space, the garage, was clean and I had much more room to move around, I also have a sense of pride everytime I use the workbench to build something, knowing that my work has helped me with undertaking future projects is very satisfying. Unfortunately, others in the house have seen all the new space I’ve carved out and it seems the garage is slowly starting to fill up with junk again (although its still currently far better than it was).

high.situation: Leading up to the end of the ENGG3050 unit, it has come to my attention that ethics, integrity, and social responsibility are incredibly important in the engineering field. Both from my own personal experiences, as well as research done for my presentations done in class, it is clear that ethics and integrity have grown in the field and is changing vastly. This comes along with new technologies such as Artificial Intelligence models like ChatGPT as well as looking at renewable energies and technologies related to the company I presented in week 10, Wallbox. Ensuring as an engineer we understand our social responsibilities and how we take technology to the next level will ensure a positive impact is made on the society around us and improve the lives of future generations.             Task: In terms of artificial intelligence, it can be applied to so many tasks which have occurred throughout my engineering degree. Especially in tasks which require writing like many lab reports or even these reflections, I have seen people using ChatGPT to copy and paste answers. I dislike this, and it does not reflect my true intentions as an engineer. Of course it is easy to do and would reduce the time it would take to do assessments, but it does not deliver the same gratitude and understanding that actually being involved in those assignments brings. This issue has been raised by many of the higher ups and academics at Macquarie University where they have addressed the issue of plagiarised and artificially created content, which I agree with. It should be used as a learning tool and to provide background information as needed, like google that gives summarised information. In extreme cases, it is important to hold moral ethics and integrity as an engineer as anything we create and present to the public will be used by people maybe across the world, so ensuring we take some level of social responsibility during our careers is important, and artificial intelligence does not have the capacity to think and consider the consequences and the many challenges that come with engineering.             Action: I have used ChatGPT, I am not afraid to admit it, but I don’t believe that discredits my integrity or ethics as an engineer. I try my very best to use it as a tool to aid my learning Often during the ENGG3050 SGTA’s I find myself stuck with topics, and such I use ChatGPT to summarise the main idea for that week, like in week 11 I wasn’t sure what the IRR is and how it is calculated. Using ChatGPT it summarised what the IRR is, and the formula associated, combined with other research I was then able to answer the question in my own words and it overall made it easier to understand the concepts. I think as we progress into the future, AI models will be integrated into every part of our lives and as such is why I’m reflecting on its use in the engineering world, it will help to do things quicker and push technology further, but it is critical to use it for the benefit of ourselves and socially. I’ve noticed a lot of people using it just to pass though and that concerns me for the future. Sure, whilst university might not reflect the real working environment as an engineer, the moral implications and ethics associated with completing this work with honesty and integrity is crucial as an engineer. Moving away from artificial intelligence, and taking integrity and ethics as a whole, in my career as an engineer it is daunting to be held responsible for real world applications of engineering. To build up integrity today means providing honesty and transparency to customers in the future, to ensure safety to the people whose lives I may change or impact as an engineer. One major field I am interested in, sustainability and development in the future requires a great amount of ethics, moving away from simply making money and ensuring I am socially responsible, it is a great skill I am learning every day throughout these units.
high.result: It’s hard to judge my own ethics and integrity. I think that’s up to the people around me, my tutors, convenors and hopefully one day the general public. I can say with full confidence that throughout the past few years, especially in ENGG3050 I’ve really learnt what it means to build up my confidence and consider my own actions as an engineer and how that impacts say a business, the general engineering team in my future career, and to build up on my transferable skills. I’ve also learnt that you cannot please everyone, sometimes you have to make sacrifices and make large amounts of change in order to create long term sustainability in a company and push past short term profiting, this was especially clear in the first case study with GM. This was also highlighted in case study 2, where the implications of global warming and future sustainability was presented, showing how although coal power was cheap and easy, the integrity and ethics of engineers knew that it was not sustainable and sacrificed time and research into providing solar power solutions. Integrity isn’t something that can be taught or learnt easily, it takes time to build up what it means to have ethics, and to be able to see the social responsibility that is essential in this field, and for many fields in that matter. I am not perfect, and engineers make mistakes, but it is important that we learn from those and build up from there. In terms of artificial intelligence, again I must reiterate that it is going to be a critical tool, today and in the future, so ensuring the future generations of engineers can use it to push themselves and develop their ethics and integrity is critical for long term success.

high.situation: This semester, I overloaded myself by taking five units instead of the usual four. This resulted in periods where I was overwhelmed by an immense amount of work and assignments. For one of my units, we were assigned a significant Continuous Professional Development (CPD) project worth 60% of the final grade. Around weeks 5 or 6, I finalised my CPD project idea, choosing to work on a facial expression recognition program. Initially, I underestimated the project's complexity, thinking it wouldn't be too challenging since I had some prior exposure to machine learning. However, I made the mistake of prioritising other assignments and exam preparation over the holidays, completely neglecting my CPD project until week 9.
high.task_action: When I realised I needed to start working on the CPD immediately, panic set in. As I began, I quickly understood that learning everything and coding a complete model or program alone within the limited time frame was unrealistic. I used my better judgement and made the strategic decision to prioritise learning and comprehending the topics over focusing solely on delivering a final product. I slowed my pace, created a plan, and concentrated on grasping the concepts and expanding my knowledge rather than stressing over producing a fully functional program. I also made sacrifices in my personal life, cancelling social plans and non-essential activities like going to the gym to dedicate time to this task.
high.result: These trade-offs and my re-prioritisation proved to be a wise decision. By week 11, I had gained significantly more knowledge and understanding than I would have if I had solely focused on creating a working program. I became well-versed in machine learning topics and developed an understanding of how to approach problems, even if I didn't have a final product to showcase. This approach reduced my stress and equipped me with the theoretical knowledge needed to eventually start coding a functional product to present to the class.      This experience taught me valuable lessons about acknowledging the importance of tasks, prioritising them accordingly, and managing my time more effectively. I realised that I should have recognised the significance of the CPD project earlier and prioritised it over other assignments. Furthermore, I became aware of my tendency to work on one assignment at a time, hoping to complete it quickly, which often led to a backlog of assignments due simultaneously. I recognised the need to change this approach to avoid overwhelming stress when facing multiple deadlines.

high.situation: Attitudes, thought and learning       After completing the previous reflection on accountability and work efficiency this was a natural progression for a reflective topic. The stresses of a university student are vast and maximising time efficiency is imperative to ensure a good work, study and life balance. Although this unit has no ‘group component’ per say, there are optimizations that can occur to help aid in learning of content in a succinct manner. All projections for tasks are just that and the main hurdle here is determining the projected time for tasks that have been assigned. Time management must accommodate work balance, study commitments and life.       Committing enough time to study while working full time is tough, especially when taking appropriate down time to recoup and recover from many busy days. I found myself doing work tasks over study commitments as they are easy and an odd form of procrastination. The somewhat trivial work tasks took much needed time away from study time which impaired efficient learning.       It would seem that having some preconceived understanding of the concepts that are being taught heavily impacts your understanding of the topic and is additionally reinforced when there is a face to face component. This semester it was quite difficult to keep on top of the material prior to the face to face engagement and therefore at some intersections I felt lost throughout the practical and lecture sessions.        Managing social commitments was also difficult throughout the semester and being able to say no was required at critical points throughout the semester. Although I have yet to be late for any assessments due to social commitments, there have been times where the quality of work was less than what I would have liked as these social commitments drained me of energy and time. Ensuring that the time allocated for social commitments align with the time required for work and study commitments. A number of unavoidable social commitments surfaced throughout the semester, some of these could have been planned for, some were sporadic. It is difficult to log the times for the sporadic events but I should try for a rolling average over the different months to ensure I accommodate for the future.       Looking back a number of different scheduling techniques could have been implemented to get better use of my time, however further research is required for implementation. Balancing a prioritisation matrix would vastly improve the outcomes for time management. I am looking for a platform that can manage this. Perhaps a project for the future?       Managing a project based team was interesting throughout the semester. Often I found myself being distracted by group members asking for advice on a topic that was required for a group project. This lead to some required inefficiency but could have potentially been better handled by scheduling specific times and objectives, such that the feedback for given tasks could be more efficiently managed. Unfortunately many useful tools that could further improve efficiency were not available to us during this semester. We did try to use trello to manage the workloads for a while but it became cumbersome and ineffective.      Task:       The task that I will be reviewing today is that of attitudes, thought and learning, where efficiency and project outcomes are a large part of the metric I rate myself against. This is a particularly important review as it will lead me in the future to create the right environment for best practices that work for me with managing time.       In the beginning of semester as always I populate my calendar(perhaps too much) with the schedule I would be adhering to throughout the semester. Work, study and (less so)social aspects. At the beginning I found myself somewhat overwhelmed with the workload I had committed to and there was rarely a moment that I was able to decompress. At the beginning of week 5 I had completed an average of 50 hours of work, 15 hours of study and roughly 15 hours of social commitments each week. This was unsustainable, but did give me some strong work ethic to begin the semester. This followed on with a slight reduction in work and social hours per week. It was a breath of fresh air after the difficult month I had. There was a momentum shift which unfortunately did not equate to extra study for the units I was enrolled in. I think this was a particularly difficult semester to shift gears here as the units structures were not overly clear and getting ahead was particularly difficult.        The group assessment I was involved in was particularly difficult to manage my time, as I was not particularly enthusiastic about it and it was quite difficult to summon the motivation to get ahead without my peers' involvement. I pushed weekly objectives and expectations of my peers but it inevitably took more time than I set aside and often took away from my mental acuity for other endeavours. This was primarily due to the fact that I needed to switch my attention from one task to another while guiding my peers. This alongside the barrage of questions and clarification required by some members I felt this took away from my time efficiency.      Time management of my own time and that of the group is something I need to further work on. I could have been more proactive ensuring all members have the resources and knowledge to effectively complete their tasks. Further discussions with each team member will allow me to address any concerns and misconceptions that may arise based on the tasks they have been assigned. Further reducing the amount of time they and by extension I need to spend on a task.       In my position I should try to remain more adaptable but also more solid in the time allocations I put down to the variety of tasks required of me from the different units. It is always quite clear what I needed to do as a teaching assistant, but not always so clear what was required of me as a student. I should learn how to bridge that gap. The time allocations I put forwards as merely guidelines rather than hard and fast times. This I could have been more rigid with.      I also found myself scrolling on a variety of streaming platforms when I was tired, hungry or unsure how to proceed with a task. This took away from my time management in a number of ways. One was, hours could pass as I am scrolling which directly leads to reduced productivity, it would also often cut into my sleeping time which made me less effective the next day. This reduced my overall ability as an effective educator and student, often increasing the average time many tasks took to complete.      Action:       The attitudes, thought and learning come heavily dependant on time management, and this has not agreed upon best practices, it seems to be heavily dependent on the individual. Finding what works best for you is a continuous cycle of iterative improvements. This means that an accumulation of my understanding is what currently works for me and I can talk and discuss with others but it may not work for everyone. I find that I can handle a large workload but not for an extended period of time. I consider this like a muscle that I am exercising, the more I exercise the stronger it gets, until it becomes a point of time management excellence and efficiency.       This does take away from balance, which seems to be vitally important to consistency over longer duration. This balance leads to longer term time efficiency as many things are not scrambled and can be properly accounted for over longer periods. This includes stress, stress is definitely one that I have somewhat in balance, maybe even teetering on the not stressed enough category. An adequate amount of stress ensures time management is effectively followed. When I have only one or two tasks I procrastinate them, where if I have many tasks I know that I want to produce good work and so I effectively manage my time to work on them incrementally until they are finished.      Incremental iterations of any task is extremely important to time management, well for me at least. I was able to chip away at tasks until they were complete as I had designated times to do the necessary work. This iterative process further assisted my time management as the smaller and smaller pieces of work I needed to complete could be done in smaller time frames. This meant that I could put them into smaller chunks of time further increasing my resourcefulness.      The group assessment I was involved with made me realise that I need to be more selective with the distractions (even if productive) that I have around me. Having all these notifications and interruptions reduced my overall efficiency and led to poorer time management. Mental acuity is very important, remaining cognitive and functioning at higher levels allowed for more efficient learning strategies and work practices. This reduced overheads of workload allowing for more work to get done.      I am of the belief that tasks should have no time associated with them, they should be done until the scope of the work is completed. This is unfortunately not practical, with only 24 hrs a day and deadlines encroaching, many tasks are required to be completed in a certain timeframe, or else they impact other aspects of life. This leads me to allocate as much time as possible to the different tasks and hopefully having time left over for items that are not in the top layer of my prioritisation matrix, urgent and important, then followed by important. This is an extremely important gauge of how my time management strategies are being utilised. If urgent and important items stay on this matrix then I need to reevaluate how I am spending my time.      The streaming platforms lie in the not important and not urgent category. Although I commenced quite well without using these platforms, as the tasks become more difficult and I don’t see a clear path to the answer they became utilised more and more as a procrastination mechanism. I would find myself less motivated to do any work after even just 10 mins of social media before I get out of bed. This was terrible for my time management. Preventing this by setting a routine which I could do asleep (figuratively). This prevented some of these behaviours that reduced my ability to best manage my time.
high.result: The things that I discovered whilst working through this, in regards to time management, allowed some iterations to make my time management and efficiency better. The continuous iterations allowed me to accumulate an understanding and try to implement them. This was potentially not the best session to try some new time management practices as I had a larger than usual workload. Using the methods I had learnt and had been practising was quite literally thrown into the deep end.        This allowed me to further exercise effective time management like a muscle that I am exercising, the more I exercise the stronger it gets, until it becomes a point of time management excellence and efficiency.       This does take away from balance, which seems to be vitally important to consistency over longer duration. Having all these notifications and interruptions reduced my overall efficiency and led to poorer time management. Mental acuity is very important, remaining cognitive and functioning at higher levels allowed for more efficient learning strategies and work practices. This is unfortunately not practical, with only 24 hrs a day and deadlines encroaching, many tasks are required to be completed in a certain timeframe, or else they impact other aspects of life. This is an extremely important gauge of how my time management strategies are being utilised. I would find myself less motivated to do any work after even just 10 mins of social media before I get out of bed. This was terrible for my time management. high.situation: During weeks 4 and 5, my team and I were brainstorming conceptual ideas for the Rube Goldberg Machine amongst other activities that were needed like presenting to the tutor and deciding on our project definition/aim.
high.task_action: To brainstorm and develop individual ideas that would help to achieve our final goal for our Rube Goldberg Machine. Each individual idea had to be unique and stand out while also being a viable and reliable option to use as one of our components. We each developed our ideas which then lead to the communications of our ideas to our team by either explanation or diagrams that we used in our weekly presentations.
high.result: The result was being able to openly ask questions about how other team members' designs would work and also connect their individual events with the events before and after theirs. This involved looking at the different ways to join each section and also discussions based on if building the entire project on campus was better for everyone.

high.situation: Our group needed to create an RGM machine that was both visually creative and integrated various actions and events that effectively seamed together to finish a task. We decided early on to go with a common theme in RGM machines which was completing a simple task with a relatively complicated machine. We also needed to decide how to film each part and how to structure the RGM.
high.task_action: After we decided on the theme, we decided what simple task should accomplished. While attempting to create something creative, we first had the idea to segment each group member's two actions into different areas of the house (e.g. one task completed in the kitchen, another in the bathroom). By tackling the issue of filming first we were able to easily create more creative ideas. The creative and critical process helped inspire each other's ideas.
high.result: This resulted in being able to use our decided structure to inspire our creative aesthetic and task accomplished, which ultimately was someone asking for a roll of toilet paper, triggering the RGM throughout the house.

high.situation:
I was the presenter for Week 5, and I did give a presentation, the content was good enough and Our group mentor asked me to deliver it to the posse room A, however he left me a Constructive feedback on how to make the content display better 10 mins before my presentation in front of the Posse room A.

ACTION: I took a decision of bringing the change in the moment of urgency and found interactive images of the content to bring visual display of the Idea I was about to deliver to the big room among 80 people. I changed the images and reformatted the content, moved the content within the entire presentation. I was confident and I gave the presentation with the new slides.

high.situation: We need to identify issues, problems, or paradox's that come about designing our project.
high.task_action: Organising the best suitable time for everyone in the group to get together and analyse our current position, analyse what situation we are facing. Communicate across the identified issue and ultimately find new solution concepts, in this case the order of events our project will run in. We defined issues which came with what idea would suit what order of event.
high.result: The result was being able to communicate the problems and compile solutions that enhanced the flow of our project design. Later allocating what needed to be done by each individual group member to ensure an efficient and effective improvement to the current design.

high.situation: So basically our goal for RGM is to turn on a kettle. We needed to present a total of 12 actions to achieve this goal. We have chosen our theme to be a kitchen theme since we are using a kettle and we are using kitchen themed objects in our actions. With coming up with this idea as a team we brainstormed a lot on what to do so we chose to do something that is achievable and realistic so we have a higher chance of success.
high.task_action: To figure out the solution and the series of actions to achieve our goal to turning on the kettle. We had lots of brainstorming sessions during our tutorials and we have a weekly team meeting to discuss our series of plans. So what we did was when we had our goal and our theme, we had each person brainstorm as much actions as they can and when next team meeting arrived we had presented all of our actions that we had brainstormed. Once we presented all of our ideas for what our 12 actions should be, we eliminated the ones we thought that were unrealistic and that it would not work at all. Then we eliminated the ones that could not be done in a kitchen themed way. So after the whole process of elimination, we had 12 realistic and achievable kitchen themed actions that simultaneously increased our success rate for our RGM.
high.result: Once we had our 12 actions, we then worked on what order we should do them in and how these 12 actions will work along with each other which we had thought out in our weekly team irl meetings. We then assigned two actions for each person to further work on those actions and improve on them.

high.situation: During weeks one through six of ENGG1050 we were instructed to work together in a team to construct a preliminary design for a Rube Goldberg machine.
high.task_action: We underwent many brainstorming sessions: researching RGM’s, watching different previous example videos, and discussing potential materials and themes. By using decision matrices where we stated our constraints, assessed our strengths and weaknesses, and utilized the advice of our tutor we have three preliminary designs.
high.result: We have three options to select from for the final design we are going to begin construction of for our RGM project.

high.situation: During week four we as a group had to have the initiative and willingness to make decisions in the face of uncertainty, for the exact function of our RGM project.
high.task_action: Broke down the key sections by taking the initiative which we had to complete to fulfill the desired outcome of our project which once decided would be for watering a plant, we designed key sections personally to ensure that our individual parts would be unique. We had to be held accountable to ensure that we achieve what is needed for our given section of the task.
high.result: The result was a great visual diagram that outlined the key sections of our RGM which showcase our willingness to make decisions in the face of uncertainty.

high.situation: Our main goal is to turn on a kettle. At this stage we are almost complete with our RGM video and our also working on our final presentation.
high.task_action: Each member is making sure that we have recorded our actions with a good angle and that with each action we make sure its at least 3 seconds per action, also making sure with each action flows nicely from clip by clip. For the presentation, every member is working on it and we are following the marking rubric to ensure we can get the maximum marks possible.
high.result: We have most of our actions recorded and matching up with the criteria, with only a little bit of work left which is just to gather up all the clips and put them in 1 video and do a bit of editing then it is completed. For the final presentation thats going swimmingly since with the help of the marking criteria we know what areas to focus more on.

high.situation: We needed to organize the structure of the RGM component videos and make sure that there was a smooth transition over from each event in order to make it seem that they were filmed in the same space where in reality it wasn't.
high.task_action: Undergoing a brainstorming session where we identified all possible factors that could lead to an RGM that would not have a smooth transition. Each person put in their input and we concluded that the background, lighting and speed of the moving components would be the things that would change and need to be made similar in order for the RGM video to be seamless as possible. We then decided to film in the same time of day that being the afternoon, which would have easier to control lighting, to have a completely white background, so having white cardboard around, and to use video editing to carry the speeds through.
high.result: The result was having RGM component scenes seem very similar in environment ultimately achieving a seamless RGM.

high.situation: My team was tasked to design, build and operate an RGM which includes a series of actions that hands toilet paper to someone in the bathroom.
high.task_action: Building an RGM with a team required leadership skills in order to finish the project by the due date, so I was put in charge as the main leader for the entire project. Team managing included the usage of leadership tactics and strategies such as delegation, setting objectives, etc. A team strategy I implemented to combat fairness, and conflicts during online projects such as unreliable schedules or inequality in workload were delegating tasks to each member who gave an objective for themselves to complete as well as using team collaboration tools such as MIRO and Discord to ensure communication. This was done as meeting up in person was not practical and required too much of a time investment if done and there were other priorities members had to deal with.
high.result: The main team strategy I used affected the scope and result of the project and proved to be ineffective and detrimental to some members, as although members were able to set their own objectives such as getting a specific part of the RGM complete, most members did not see the priority in their own objectives and did not complete most of the work online and with the behavior encouraged due to other members managing to fill the gap. Furthermore due to leading online members ignored and did not learn from each meeting further worsening team effectiveness. However, the use of collaborative tools increased communication between members and improved morale and productivity. Collaborative tools such as MIRO helped members create and share valuable ideas and assist others.

high.situation: We needed to brainstorm a design mechanism/creative process through weeks 1-11 for developing a Rube Goldberg machine. Our idea for this design was to develop a machine for dropping a Berocca tablet into the water via various complex processes, by using pendulums, the domino effect, shovels, pulleys, and legos, to name a few.
high.task_action: We went through various brainstorming sessions throughout the weeks and came up with our design for the RGM project. We would dispose of a Berocca tablet into the water through a series of complex processes. Our idea behind this experiment/ problem statement was to try to think outside the box and also showcase that engineering can be a hard course that might give us a headache, therefore popping a Berocca every once in a while isn't a bad idea. We had several offline/online meeting sessions to brainstorm and put together our ideas. We used various methods of brainstorming like starbursting, reverse brainstorming, rapid ideation, etc to come up with the best, feasible working project. Upon discussion, and trying our individual, and group parts, multiple times, we decided that the 'weight' of the marble should be in accordance with all 12 of our actions taking place, that is, it should not be too heavy or too light. We didn't want to make the project too 'complex' but also wanted it to be complex enough to showcase what we learned through the weeks. We also chose components that were easier to make/'manufacture' like household items that weren't impossible to achieve, which would help our project execute well. We also spoke about other parameters such as the distance between objects, the difference in height, the duration of the video, and the percentage credibility of the overall machine.
high.result: The result was brainstorming and being able to come up with the perfect solution which included 12 actions, 2 actions each, executed by the 6 of us in a particular sequence.

high.situation: The task at hand was to create a flowchart describing how the game scissors, paper, rock. Would be programmed
high.task_action: We formed a group of three people to work on the task and by brainstorming possible outcomes using critical thinking, considering factors for scissors, paper, rock. We then evaluated each of the suggested options and iterated on them until we made our final decision
high.result: Our team successfully created a flowchart describing the game logic with only four steps instead of the original nine. By collaborating and using a iterative approach, we were able to simplify the task and arrive at a more optimal solution.

high.situation: Our group was required to design and present a Rube Goldberg Machine this semester which required 2 functions from each team member.
high.task_action: Every week our team was required to attend the weekly workshop for ENGG1050 where we did a mini presentation to our tutors/peers. Additionally, our team had mini weekly meetings outside the workshop class to clarify with each other what our roles, to talk about what we will be discussing in our presentation and to share our ideas and thoughts about the RGM design. We are due to present what we have designed so far in our workshop next week.
high.result: Through effective communcation/teamwork our team was able to smoothly present our design for the RGM to our peers/tutors each week. We able to clearly communicate with each other our ideas which we were able to integrate into our RGM design and because we were able to work so weel we feel that we are ready to showcase our design to our peers next week.

high.situation: Reflection upon myself and other team members initiative and decision making in the face of uncertainty.
high.task_action: During each team meeting all attending team members have put for award their opinions and many times we have come to a decision collectively. Something we made clear from the beginning is that we would all be very inclusive and stay open to each others ideas and make formulated decisions in a rational and fair manner, using a voting system for our decisions. Subsequently, our output as a team has been for the most part seamless, with room for growth. Certain challenges arise when team members miss meetings, when we are unable to understand the exact task at hand, as well as miscommunication amongst team members. During the times of uncertainty amongst certain tasks, all team members at various points have taken it upon themselves to either begin, add to, or complete a task and kept the momentum going.
high.result: The results of our decision making collectively as a team has worked extremely well because we are open to each others ideas and our voting system usually ends in a unanimous vote. When bigger challenges arose for the team and for the individuals, our work flow has kept rolling because someone has shown initiative and made decisions without necessarily waiting for someone’s approval, for example, I missed a meeting due to unforeseen circumstances and a team member began working on our presentation and once I had the time I could seamlessly move onto working on the presentation they had already begun.

high.situation: The group faced a challenge when only two out of the five members completed their individual events on time due to poor communication and commitment. This led to internal conflict and a delay in the group's progress.
high.task_action: To overcome the setback, the remaining members who hadn't completed their events had to promptly create their sections of the RGM. We collectively agreed on a specific deadline and sent out reminders to catch up on the overdue work. In case any member still couldn't finish their RGM event on time, we devised a contingency plan where other members would assist or, if necessary, create a new event.
high.result: Consequently, the three delayed members successfully constructed and recorded their two RGM events within the agreed-upon timeframe. This allowed the group to proceed with the production of the combined RGM video and resume work on our final presentation.

high.situation: We needed to come up with innovations for a mechanism where a ball hits a button and turns a fan on.
high.task_action: After class our group met in a meeting and our first point on the agenda was to brainstorm ideas to make the mechanism more efficient; each member had 5 minutes of research and 5 minutes of rough planning to come up with idea/s. We evaluated each option from a logistical and viable standpoint based on the parameters (materials, manufacturability, complexity, cost etc).
high.result: The result was being able to pick the best of the 6 ideas from the brainstorming session, that being a microswitch connected to a relay that when activated, completes the circuit break in the extension cable that turns on the fan. This eliminated the need for a large amount of instantaneous force.

high.situation: I needed to choose a topic for the reflection writing. I chose critical thinking as my topic for the reflection writing.
high.task_action: Throughout the unit, we have been encouraged to go about every process using critical thinking. When we first started planning the RGM, we used critical thinking to have the best possible design for the RGM. We carefully planned each stage of the RGM and worked on the final design. After the final design was completed, we again used critical thinking to improve the reliability of the RGM. We could see some of the stages were less reliable than others and was harder to implement. By thinking critically, we were able to make the RGM more reliable.
high.result: By practicing critical thinking, we were able to make better decisions throughout the unit and make a reliable RGM machine.

high.situation: We needed to create floor plans and designs for a student accommodation building.

high.result: The result was being able to create a design which would include the collective ideas of the group, as opposed to one person's design, minimising the possibility of flaws or weaknesses.

high.situation: The group project required a report due in less than 24 hours with no one knowing what to do.

high.result: a report finished to a high degree within the time and any sections left untouched were a team decision.

high.situation: I accidentally measured the holes of my toolbox wrong and they didn't line up with each other.

high.result: This allowed me to finish the toolbox on time and get a passing grade on my toolbox.

high.situation: We needed to draw an object to scale and make sure its measured correctly.

high.situation: As part of my engineering course, I was given a limited amount of time to construct an aluminum toolbox in the machining workshop.
high.task_action: taking time management into account, I made sure not to waste any time and started working on the toolbox immediately.
