TASK: My goal was clear: to excel in my engineering studies while also fulfilling my professional duties. This involved attending lectures, completing academic assignments, and preparing for exams, all while meeting workplace deadlines and making valuable contributions to my job. The workload was sometimes overwhelming, but I approached each day with a sense of purpose and determination, aware that success required unwavering commitment and careful effort.      ACTION: To succeed in this dual endeavor, I adopted a proactive attitude, using determination, quick action, creativity, and flexibility to overcome upcoming hurdles. I devoted extra hours to studying after work, sacrificing leisure time to keep up with my academic progress. When faced with concurrent deadlines, I strategically prioritized tasks based on urgency and importance, wisely allocating my time and resources to maximize productivity. Additionally, I utilized my creativity, seeking out supplementary educational resources, collaborating with classmates, and seeking advice from mentors to enhance my understanding of intricate engineering topics. I also maintained a flexible approach, prepared to adjust my strategies in response to unexpected challenges, ensuring consistent progress towards my goals.
high.result: Through determination, swift action, creativity, and adaptability, I navigated the challenges of balancing work and study in the field of engineering. Despite the heavy workload, I maintained outstanding academic performance, consistently achieving high grades and earning recognition for my contributions at work. This journey also spurred my personal growth, instilling in me a greater sense of resilience, adaptability, and determination. Looking back on my achievements, I take pride in knowing that my dual path has not only enriched my educational and professional experiences but also highlighted the crucial importance of perseverance and adaptability in achieving success.

high.situation: Throughout this semester, I have been working with the Australian Glass & Window Association in a technical administration role where I am responsible for auditing glass & window thermal performance simulations submitted to the Window Energy and Rating Scheme (WERS).  I am responsible for ensuring that simulations made in LBNL software: Therm, Window and Optics, are simulated in accordance to rules and regulations set by the AFRC (Australian Fenestration Registration Council) and the NFRC (National Fenestration Registration Council) through the analysis of cross-sectional models of window frame members and their relevant thermal behaviour.  This year has brought a significant spike in the submission count for simulations, and has created a backlog of pending submissions that must be audited.  This has created some urgency to work through submissions as quickly as possible to maintain a low submission count without sacrificing thoroughness and consistency.
high.task_action: My primary task was to increase my auditing efficiency for these thermal simulations without sacrificing the thoroughness that ensures all simulations are consistent.        I began by optimising my auditing processes through the use of more adequate file management methods to make keeping track of my completed audits and quickly switching between different submissions much faster.  I adjusted visual settings that made keeping track of my cursor and identifying errors easier, further increasing audit speed.       I followed this with increasing the amount of communication between the Engineers I work with and myself, clarifying issues that I am uncertain on with them rather than taking the time to read through the large list of rules and standards.  This allowed for me to reduce time wasted finding specific rules through making use of the resources around me to quickly refer to points in my relevant documents and their advice.  This also assisted with increasing my understanding of the rules, reducing the amount I need to refer to any documents.      I also began tracking my progress more thoroughly which went hand-in-hand with my file management processes, allowing myself and the Engineers I work with to update association members on wait times and create plans to manage progress through the backlog.
high.result: Through implementation of the above actions, I aimed to address the increased workload that came with the increased submissions effectively, ensuring that my auditing processes remained robust, efficient and in full compliance with all relevant standards.

high.situation: Throughout my engineering degree, I've cultivated leadership skills through various endeavours. Three notable examples stand out: Firstly, our Engg2000 project tasked us with designing and fabricating a marble machine. Secondly, I spearheaded the completion of a white paper for the Astra program, exploring Australia's role in the global space industry. Lastly, my executive position within Macquarie Orbital and my current initiative, MQ Rover, demanded leadership to navigate complexities in project ideation, design, and execution.      In the Engg2000 project, I frequently assumed leadership roles, delegating tasks, facilitating brainstorming sessions, and ensuring project adherence to deadlines. Similarly, as lead editor for the Astra program's white paper, I coordinated team efforts, provided guidance, and ensured the final paper was of high quality. My involvement with Macquarie Orbital and MQ Rover has involved extensive event coordination, team leadership, and interdisciplinary skill development.
high.task_action: In each instance, my leadership roles involved key tasks and actions. For Engg2000, I initiated regular team meetings to address progress, challenges, and collaborative problem solving. Effective communication ensured clarity on responsibilities and fostered a sense of accountability and teamwork.      Regarding the Astra program's white paper, I led the coordination of a diverse team, developing structured timelines, assigning tasks based on expertise, and guiding discussions to produce a cohesive and impactful final paper.      Within Macquarie Orbital and MQ Rover, leadership tasks extended beyond project management to encompass team building, outreach, and strategic planning. This included organising recruitment events, conducting training sessions, and fostering an inclusive environment.
high.result: The outcomes of these leadership roles were significant. In Engg2000, our team successfully delivered a functioning marble machine, showcasing collaborative leadership and timely project completion. For the white paper, our efforts resulted in a well-researched and compelling document that garnered positive feedback from program organisers and industry professionals. I also received the Emerging Space Pioneers award as recognition of my leadership role within the team.      In my roles with Macquarie Orbital and MQ Rover, the impact of leadership extended beyond specific projects to the broader student community. Through engaging events, innovative projects and promoting the importance of networking we attracted and empowered a diverse group of students to pursue their interests in engineering and space exploration. The establishment of MQ Rover represents a continuation of these goals.      Overall, my experiences leading engineering endeavours has reinforced the importance of effective communication, collaboration, and strategic thinking in achieving success. By leveraging these skills, I have been able to drive innovation, foster teamwork, and make meaningful contributions to both academic projects and extracurricular initiatives.

high.situation: I recently had to collaborate on a team project with people that had different backgrounds and skill levels. We had a short window of time to plan, assemble, and present a presentation on a complicated cube (the Marvellous Marble Machine).             Task :       To assure a morally sound and functional marble machine, the endeavour required not only in-depth subject matter understanding but also successful teamwork. Our success was mostly dependent on participation and clear communication. Increasing collaboration, streamlining channels of communication, and making sure that everyone felt heard and respected were my main objectives.             Action:       Understanding the value of strong teamwork, I proactively worked to promote open lines of communication among the group. I started holding regular meetings so that everyone could exchange ideas, worries, and updates. I valued each team member's distinct viewpoint and made sure everyone knew exactly what their duties and responsibilities were. I also encouraged contributions from all team members. Furthermore, I paid close attention to criticism and recommendations, adding them as necessary to our project strategy. I promoted teamwork by creating a welcoming environment where people felt at ease disclosing ideas and thoughts. I led candid conversations to identify the underlying reasons of the disagreements and collaborated with the group to identify compromises.
high.result: Our team managed to produce a well-researched, well-coordinated presentation that surpassed expectations by placing a high priority on cooperation and communication. Our teamwork bolstered our relationships as a unit and improved the calibre of our output. In addition, I learned a lot about leadership, empathy, and flexibility from the experience, all of which I can apply to ENGG3000 assignments going forward.

high.situation: So far this semester, I have been required to complete multiple group projects worth significant weighting in terms of the total assessment percentage. They are critical to achieving a good result within the subject and test on a wide variety of skills including not only technical problem solving but also presentational and articulation-based skills.
high.task_action: Within the various groups I've worked in, it is crucial to split the workload effectively and balance the tasks based around each students' strengths and weaknesses. However, my groups have had students who are less motivated than others and they have found themselves failing to meet deadlines which causes the entire group to suffer. In such cases, the concept of accountability is extremely important. If this behavior is not called out it becomes increasingly difficult to 'play catchup' and it is therefore critical to have these difficult conversations about why there has been a lack of effort as soon as possible. In my case, our group needed to reach out to the slow-moving teammates and hold them accountable so we could submit the group-task before the due-date. If these group members had not been held accountable, it is likely the project would have failed to meet the deadline. Similarly, we held ourselves accountable for the outcome of the project as we made significant effort to ensure the project would be delivered on time - even if this included chasing others for their portion of the work.
high.result: The outcome of this accountability is that the projects have all been submitted on time with an equal contribution from each member. This has resulted in my teams all submitting project we are proud of and we have felt like overall they have been a success.

high.situation: Balancing school, work, and fun has been tough. As my semester went on, last year, things got even harder. My grades started slipping, I was always tired, and I felt like I couldn't keep up with everything I had to do. Trying to manage homework and job shifts left me with no time to relax or see friends.      TASK: I needed to find a way to handle my schoolwork and job, and still have some fun without feeling overwhelmed. It was about making sure I could do well in school, keep my job, and also enjoy some free time.      ACTION: Here’s what I did to change things:      1. Organizing My Time: I started using my phone to plan my week. I set specific times for studying, working, and hanging out. This helped me see when I could take breaks and have fun.      2. Getting Help:  I talked to university counselors who gave me great advice on how to manage my time better and not stress too much.      3. Talking to My Boss: I explained my situation at work, and we agreed to reduce my hours a bit and make my schedule more flexible around exam times.      4. Leaning on Friends: I joined study groups and spent more time with friends who were going through the same struggles. We supported each other a lot.
high.result: The changes had a tangible impact. My grades improved from D's to HD's in most of my classes by the end of the semester, a clear sign that dedicating specific time to study paid off. Work became less of a burden; with fewer hours, I wasn't as exhausted all the time, which made me more productive when I was there. But perhaps the most significant change was in my personal life. I started having regular movie nights and coffee meet-ups with friends again, which boosted my mood and overall well-being. This experience showed me that with the right approach, it's possible to find a balance that allows for success in school, at work, and in having a fulfilling personal life. It was a lesson in the power of planning, communicating needs, and the importance of support from those around me.

high.situation: At the beginning of the semester, I was overwhelmed by academic and personal responsibilities. My academic pressure is usually high since I must do four units per semester. Coupled with increased personal commitments, I was struggling to prioritize tasks effectively. This situation highlighted the need for a structured approach to goal-setting and understanding my needs to manage my time efficiently and achieve desired outcomes.      TASK: Recognizing the need for improvement, I started researching effective goal-setting practices; I did some self-reflection to identify my needs and set clear and achievable goals. I also sought guidance from tutors and friends who may have been in similar situations.      ACTION: I began by conducting a thorough self-assessment to identify my strengths, weaknesses, values, and aspirations. This involved reflecting on past experiences, seeking feedback from peers and instructors, and utilizing tools such as SWOT analysis and goal-setting frameworks. I also sought resources on time management and effective goal-setting practices to enhance my skills.
high.result: Through diligent effort and perseverance, I witnessed a transformation in my approach to understanding needs and setting goals. I developed a deeper awareness of my personal and academic priorities, enabling me to align my actions with my long-term objectives. By setting SMART goals, I successfully navigated through the challenges and pursued opportunities. I also cultivated resilience and adaptability, learning from setbacks and adjusting strategies accordingly.

high.situation: during a recent group project for the design of concrete, the challenge of creating a new concept that would solve our issues with loading for a span of a concrete beam arose. The beam would undergo too great of a load for the span we had designed to fit seamlessly into our carparking structure.      Task : my task was to explore alternative approaches and devise innovative solutions to address this challenge while also managing our group to solve the problem more efficiently. To solve this issue, we had to understand the fundamental principles of concrete beams and their tendencies to fail under certain loads when reinforced incorrectly. As a group we would need to consider the loading paths above the beam, the self-weight of the concrete and reinforcements, the design moment and design shear for our scenario, and ultimately design whether or not we need to completely redesign our structure to comply better with a shorter beam. Our goal as a team is not only to design an appropriate beam, but to understand the concept as a whole and implement our understanding in an innovative way.      Action : To address the challenge of creating a new concept to solve the loading issues for the concrete beam span, our group took a proactive and collaborative approach. Firstly, we conducted thorough research into various design principles and methodologies related to concrete beams, focusing on factors such as loading paths, structural analysis, and reinforcement techniques. This enabled us to gain a comprehensive understanding of the problem at hand and identify potential areas for improvement. Drawing upon our collective expertise and leveraging the diverse skill sets within the group, we engaged in brainstorming sessions to generate innovative ideas and alternative approaches to tackle the issue. We encouraged open dialogue and creative thinking, fostering an environment where every team member felt empowered to contribute their insights and suggestions. Furthermore, we actively collaborated with our project advisor and sought feedback from the tutors to validate our design concepts and ensure their feasibility and practicality. Their expertise and guidance provided valuable insights that helped refine our ideas and enhance the overall quality of our solution. By taking a systematic and collaborative approach, we were able to devise an innovative solution that addressed the loading issues for the concrete beam span effectively. Our design not only met the technical requirements but also showcased our ability to think critically and creatively in solving complex engineering problems.
high.result: As a result of our efforts, we successfully developed a novel concept for the concrete beam span that significantly improved its load-bearing capacity while maintaining structural integrity. Our innovative approach garnered praise from both our peers and project advisor, highlighting the effectiveness of our solution. Furthermore, the experience of working collaboratively as a team to overcome a challenging engineering problem strengthened our communication, problem-solving, and project management skills. It also fostered a deeper understanding of concrete beam design principles and reinforced the importance of innovation in engineering practice. Taking leadership in an endeavour such as this has provided a deeper understanding of both how to lead a team in creating new solution concepts and how to lead future projects that may have similar issues later in life in the field of civil engineering.

high.situation: During the week 6 workshop for the Engineering Leadership and Entreprenership unit, I was asked to select a pain point from a list collated through our prior field research conducted as part of the customer discovery process, aimed at achieving product-market fit. I approached this activity with enthusiasm, eager to delve deeper into the research and fact-finding aspects of my engineering journey. After carefully reviewing the list, I chose to address the "inadequate support for using AI in learning". This selection resonated with me due to the prevalent industry focus on AI and its implications in society.       Initially, this situation appeared as a significant challenge within the education landscape, where traditional teaching methods struggle to adapt to the rapidly evolving technological advancements. Many universities globally encounter difficulties in addressing AI plagiarism, leading some to shy away from AI integration altogether, thus exacerbating the lack of adequate support for AI in education. Recognizing this gap, I identified the task of developing a solution to effectively integrate AI into the learning process, aiming to enhance educational outcomes and address the shortcomings of traditional education methods.
high.task_action: After identifying the pain point of inadequate support for using AI in learning, my task was to develop a solution that effectively addressed this challenge within a university setting. To begin, I conducted extensive research to gain a deep understanding of AI integration in education, exploring its potential benefits, challenges, and the technological requirements involved. With this knowledge as my foundation, I proceeded to brainstorm potential solutions, considering various factors such as software, hardware, administration, humanistic approaches, and innovative hybrid models, as discussed in lectures.      Navigating this task proved to be challenging and time consuming, particularly as it was my first experience undertaking such an endeavor. I felt uncertain at times about the suitability of my solutions across the different categories. However, I leveraged my background in data science and engineering and applied technical skills, understanding of engineering principles to articulate solutions to the problem at hand.      Delving deeper, I explored how the integration of AI could address related pain points such as research, marking, and feedback processes. From an administrative standpoint, I considered how educators could reallocate time and resources towards educational advancements for students, with AI handling repetitive tasks to ensure consistency. Seeking validation and feedback, I interviewed Nicholas from SPINE Engineering, to gather perspectives on my proposed solutions and subsequently, evaluated each solution and made necessary revisions based on the feedback received. I found such feedback was quite insightful as it provided real suggestions from the workplace this product is aimed at and brought forth concuring and refuting opinions to from one of the stakeholders.
high.result: Through the iterative process of solution development and stakeholder feedback, I refined my understanding of pain points, and ultimately shaped a final Key Value Proposition (KVP) for the product. This approach not only enhanced my problem-solving and critical thinking skills around ethics but also allowed me to articulate a compelling value proposition that addresses the needs of users and stakeholders in a University environment.

high.situation: I am a student at Macquarie University and for the unit ENGG3050 Engineering Leadership and Entrepreneurship, we had a quiz (Quiz 2) that was due on the Saturday 6th of April. The Quiz is worth 6% of my overall grade and it is crucial for me that i complete the quiz to the best of my ability and aim for the highest possible marks.
high.task_action: The task at hand involves taking full responsibility and accountability of this Quiz making sure the Quiz is completed before the due date hence managing time is crucial. It is also important i utilise all available resources and make sure the quiz is completed to the best of my ability. The quiz itself will include various questions that are based on various different articles/reading materials that we were given for our SGTA classes. There is no time limit but i only have one attempt at doing the quiz so i have to make sure it is my best attempt. I have various other assignments due the same weekend so i have to take full accountability and make sure i do this quiz. i decided to do the quiz on Thursday, a few days before the quiz closes. This was the best option for me as i had free time and it will give me plenty of time to make sure i do the quiz efficiently. I began the quiz and attempted all questions to the best of my ability, reading through the various articles that the quiz was based, using critical thinking skills and analysing the sources i answered the questions. I spent an hour and a half on the quiz and when i was confident in my answers i submitted the quiz. i made sure i adhered to ethical principles of academic integrity by properly doing the quiz without cheating.
high.result: Through the importance of accountability, i was able to complete the quiz to the best of my ability and within the set due date. i finished the quiz a few days before the due date maximising time management, i utilised all available resources avoiding plagiarism and upholding honesty and integrity. This benefited me as i received 7.5 out of 10 for the quiz which is a success in my eyes. By taking full ownership of my learning i was able to complete the task at hand and now i will be able to transfer this skill of accountability to further my professional and academic develoment.

high.situation: I was tasked with conducting a series of interviews to understand customer pain points related to a specific topic. This involved gathering information to develop an understanding of various issues and using this data to formulate personas that represented different customer demographics, backgrounds, buying habits, and motivations.
high.task_action: My main task was to identify and analyse these pain points through the interviews and then use the insights gained to propose solutions. The goal was to provide 10 solutions that could address these pain points effectively, which could be in the form of software, physical artefacts, administrative changes, humanistic approaches, or innovative hybrid solutions. I began by designing a structured interview process that would allow me to gather comprehensive and relevant information from participants. I ensured that the questions were open-ended to encourage detailed responses and covered a wide range of topics related to the chosen subject. After conducting the interviews, I analysed the responses to identify common pain points and patterns. Based on this analysis, I created personas that represented the different segments of the target audience, highlighting their specific needs and challenges. Finally, I brainstormed and developed 10 solutions that addressed the identified pain points, ensuring that they were feasible and relevant to the personas.
high.result: The result was a well-defined set of solutions that were tailored to the needs of the target audience. By understanding the relationship between pain points and customer demographics, I was able to propose solutions that were not only innovative but also practical and likely to be well-received by the intended users. This approach helped in providing a clear direction for addressing the pain points effectively and enhancing the overall customer experience. Civil engineering often involves identifying and solving complex problems related to infrastructure, construction, and urban planning. The ability to understand and address pain points, as demonstrated in this project, is directly applicable to tackling engineering challenges. Overall, the skills and results from this project demonstrate a holistic approach to problem-solving, which is invaluable in the field of civil engineering for developing infrastructure that meets societal needs and enhances the quality of life.

high.situation: As a university student trying to balance part-time work, a full study load, and a passion for fitness, I often find myself in situations that test my perseverance, resourcefulness, and flexibility. With a younger sister just starting her degree, I feel an added responsibility to demonstrate these qualities and guide her through the challenges of academic life trying to give her the best experience as well as prevent her from repeating my mistakes. Balancing multiple commitments while maintaining a sense of urgency and a will to deliver required a great deal of determination and adaptability.
high.task_action: One particular instance that highlighted these qualities was when I had to complete my minor project for an elec unit. I had food poisoning, migraines and was recovering from an accident. To be able to manage that as well as continue to complete my uni work does require perseverance and a will to deliver. To tackle this challenge, I really had to remind myself of why I am here to study and push through to be able to sit down, find the mental capacity to learn and apply what I had left to get it done and give it my best to ensure I had to regrets after.       I approached the task with a sense of urgency, breaking it down into smaller, manageable sub-tasks and setting strict deadlines for each aspect of the project. This not only made the project feel less overwhelming but also allowed me to track my progress and ensure I was on schedule to deliver and try and reduce any time it took to go through and de-bug any issues I was having.      When I encountered obstacles, such as my arduino not working, and software glitches, I had to be resourceful and adapt. I met up with some friends and while they were working on their code or other projects, I was able to borrow those to test what I had done to try and understand what the next step could be.      Throughout this period, I made a point to share my experiences with my sister, emphasizing the importance of perseverance and resourcefulness in the face of academic challenges. I encouraged her to stay focused on her goals and to be willing to adapt her strategies when necessary. Although her and I approach things very differently, sharing those pathways can still help provide an alternative perspective.
high.result: By demonstrating perseverance, maintaining a sense of urgency, and being resourceful and flexible, I was able to complete the minor assignment on time, whilst trying to balance everything else. The experience not only boosted my confidence in my ability to handle pressure.

high.situation: I was assigned with an embedded systems project in ELEC3042 (due Week 7) that required using C language to code on MPLab X ide (a complete alien software to me). I got sick in Week 5.
high.task_action: The project, namely, "Minor Project", required us to code a countdown timer on a HW262 Arduino shield. We were given 6 weeks to complete the project. In the first 5 weeks, I completed the practical sessions that were a form of implementation of the knowledge gained in the lectures. Following that, I sketched a rough state machine diagram to help me conceptualize my code structure and determine my approach to the final result. Unfortunately, I got sick during that week, lacking behind on the plan. I believed that I would be able to pull it off, but I wasn't seeing it through; I didn't see myself fixing the bugs in my code for a long time. I divided the burden of other assignments and the Minor project for the last week into hours and days. I prioritized the assignments based in their due dates. Therefore, completing all other assignments, I resumed coding the countdown timer. I sat goals and measurable achievements to help track my progress, but I was falling behind. We were allowed to use generative AI, so I used it to help speed my progress. Even after using AI, I had to manually fix the inaccuracies of the code.
high.result: After long hours, I was able to complete 8/9 tests and submitted the code half hour before the due time. The code worked perfectly other than the integration of a potentiometer to change the brightness of the 7-segment display.

high.situation: In the midst of my third year of engineering studies, I was stretched thin between part-time work, multiple assignments, and battling severe migraines. This period tested my limits, with health issues significantly affecting my ability to manage an already demanding workload.      TASK: Despite these challenges, my goal remained clear: to successfully complete my assignments and fulfill my work responsibilities. Getting extensions would mean more work for my future self, so I just applied for special consideration for one of my units. I just wanted to push through and get the work done. The task required exceptional time management, a high degree of perseverance, and finding effective ways to manage my migraines.      ACTION: I took a strategic approach to my situation, breaking down assignments into manageable parts and utilizing any available time efficiently, including the slight extension. To cope with the migraines, I adopted various strategies such as prescription medication, sleeping early, and creating a quiet, dark space for relief. Importantly, I discovered that ambient, low-volume sounds helped mitigate the intensity of the migraines, allowing me to get some work done. After consulting a doctor and getting some tests done, I was diagnosed with chronic migraines. There were times that I felt like giving up, but I tried my best to push through.
high.result: The careful planning and adjustments I made enabled me to meet nearly all my deadlines and maintain my work commitments without compromise. The small extension on one assignment provided a crucial buffer that, while not significantly easing my overall burden, emphasised on the importance of communication and asking for help when I need it. I came out of this period with an improved sense of resilience, having maintained my academic grades and work performance despite the unfavorable situation.

high.situation: During the testing of a work order of valves that were to be sent out to be used in a launch vehicle for Gilmore Space there were performance concerns about the valves. Although they passed the test, based on the customer’s performance requirements, they did not match up with family data of previous work orders of valves. Several of the performance criteria were approximately at a 50% increase in values when compared to other valves that have been sent out.
high.task_action: There was a decision that needed to be made to work out whether it was our responsibility to disassemble the valves and go into a full analysis of components due to family data or to send the valves out to Gilmour with the current performance numbers. There were significant ethical and financial concerns with this - on one hand the valves met customer requirements and would most likely function adequately, on the other hand, a tear down of the valves would stop production and could take up to a month to find the root cause of the issue which would cost the company a lot of time and money.      As a result of this there was an internal meeting held with myself and other managers to determine what would be the best steps forward. Due to the nature of the performance metric that was affected and my understanding of the valves due to my role as the Manufacturing Engineer, QC Inspector, and Assembly and Test Engineer I felt strongly that the most likely cause of this was due to a particular set of seals within the valve. At the conclusion of the meeting, it was determined that we should take a proactive approach to resolving the issue to better understand our valves as well as showing our customers that we can be trusted to provide the best product to them.
high.result: As a result of this meeting a single valve from the work ordered was disassembled to check on the suspected set of seals, After a week of detailed analysis it was found that the seals were purchased from a new provider who provided seals with a slightly lower Duro rating then advertised - this was the root cause of the performance concerns. As a result of this investigation molds were designed in-house and going forward the seals are made in-house. Our failure investigation report was presented to Gilmore Space to help with establishing better customer relations moving forward. All valves were disassembled and reassembled and then re-tested to ensure they passed customer requirements as well as meeting family data. As a conclusion our valves will be better going forward with improved reliability.

high.situation: In my place of work, autonomous sailing vessel and the systems aboard it (navigation, communication, datum-collection, etc.) had to be prepared for launch with the minimum chance of failure or "hiccoughs" during its scheduled operation.
high.task_action: A digital list of checks that had been compiled earlier and made into an online form was gone-through by pairs of workers and items ticked off one at a time. Several changes had been made to the form so that tasks specific to one boat or another could be marked as "not applicable" on forms related to boats that did not have the related equipment. Unfortunately, some undocumented changes had been made to the form by members of the operations or software departments without the knowledge of the shop workers or shop management. These forms are initially stored and filled-out client-side.and are only updated on the server when the form is submitted. Software or coding limitations meant that forms could not be submitted until completely filled out.
high.result: Some of the undocumented changes, along with the limitations of the form software, IT infrastructure and form design meant that forms could not be completed and filled out for some boats, and checks had to be documented manually in the old-fashioned way after being transcribed from a half-completed form. This resulted in significant confusion and wasted some of everyone's time, including workers and management from both the workshop and other departments. Luckily the launch of the boats was not delayed, but it added potential risk of a no-go to an already fraught situation.

high.situation: Accountability and Workload efficiency      At the beginning of the semester the ENGG3050 cohort had a number of tasks required to complete the learning outcomes of the unit. Each student was self allocated into teams to work together towards completing the weekly tasks and a presentation. The formulation of these groups seemed to be based on who knew who and who, and we sat at the same table initially. The specialisations of the group seemed to begin with collecting together initially, however a few stragglers around the room were also invited. Although the weekly tasks were individual work, to optimise each individual's time we decided to divvy up researching given weekly questions and report back when we found some that were useful.      In previous sessions I had been designated team lead in a number of projects. In this unit there is little in the way of group work other than a group presentation, even that seems separated into separate sections and really primarily individual work, with only collaboration of ideas and format, rather than output and presentation skills. The slides would need to be consistent and our messages need to align, but the content we would be presenting was our own based on the five different questions related to the company we chose.      In saying that, the weekly SGTAs would pose a greater challenge for any individual compared with sharing the workload. From early in the semester our table of 8-10 people decided to segment the required readings and summarise each reading's findings. This allowed us to maximise output and increase efficiency of the group compared with working solo, an essential skill for any engineering discipline. There were some hurdles when some students did not provide a sufficient summary as we will discuss below.      Task:      The task that I will be reviewing today is that of creating an environment where collaboration, communication and quality is a standard. In this instance we have ten people that have varying relationships, values and motivations.       At the outset I was using my comfortable management style, which is typically led by suggestion. I don’t like confrontation too much and I don’t like being the leader, as there are additional responsibilities involved taking care of both. In order to streamline the work process I was unable to avoid this commitment with my peers as maximising efficiency was the goal. From the onset I needed to quickly adjust, as the variety of values throughout the group responded to different leadership styles. As a group I led the prompt of discussion to set up some clear weekly expectations and responsibilities for each member. To implement accountability for the tasks that were agreed upon by the group I pushed for a group discussion on weekly responsibility. I didn’t dictate terms of the meeting, rather kept it on track and prompted the group to discuss the possible strategies to be more effective during the SGTAs. This I did by holding a meeting where each team member gave input and finally agreed to the responsibilities of each member. In a nutshell, being more effective and efficient as a group. This was easier said than done.      The first iteration of this for the weekly tasks was a mini disaster. The summaries provided by each team member that we collectively agreed to was not enforced, the feedback from the group was not followed up and work of sufficient quality was completed by just four members. Although others had a contribution it was not sufficient in detail or quality. Not only this, a majority of poor summaries were fixed by just myself. This behaviour needed to be corrected for myself and the other members of the team for future SGTAs. We spent some time collaborating on expectations of the summaries of the readings. We then spent the first 30 mins working through our individual readings and summaries.      In my position I needed to be more adaptable, iterating corrections each week to be more efficient to get to the outcomes the group seeked. Being dynamic in this situation seemed crucial, in both my communication style and increasing the strengths of each member of the team. Understanding and aligning members with the vision of the group giving them perspective was the idea in subsequent weeks. Managing the accountability of the group is still an opportunity for me, but being aware is half the battle and each time it happens it gives me an opportunity to grow.      Conflict resolution is an important leadership skill, these opportunities I am given when interacting with difficult team members allows me to exercise that skill and further develop. Staying on point for the concerns of the team mate has allowed me to understand what the values of the team mates are and help them see the vision of the group. Conflict is not a bad thing as it promotes diverse ideas, it is how we manage it that is crucially important.      Time management of my own time and that of the group is something I need to further work on. I could have been more proactive ensuring all members have the resources and knowledge to effectively complete their tasks. Further discussions with each team member will allow me to address any concerns and misconceptions that may arise based on the tasks they have been assigned. Further reducing the amount of time they and by extension I need to spend on a task.      Action:      Adjusting for this at the next group meeting, I put forward some important questions to the team such that each member was aligned and expectations were clear. I didn’t want the imbalance of time or energy across the group. Things like ‘when should we have the first draft ready?’, ‘What is a sufficient amount of time this should be worked on to achieve a HD?’ and ‘what sections play to the strengths of each team member?’. This was to set clear expectations, deadlines and give the group a sense of involvement and responsibility to the task. I further put in place that each team member would take a second responsibility of checking and proofreading another group member's summaries.      Although I knew each member would have to read all the summaries and make necessary changes, this would hopefully do two things, alleviate the amount of feedback I had to provide personally, and increase the awareness and ability of the other students. This allowed the accountability to come from a diverse range of perspectives and didn’t feel like it was just coming from me. I am a huge advocate for training and development, passing these skills on to my teammates I feel I have an obligation for. One day we may actually work together and I want to maximise success for those that want it.      This addressed some of my leadership inadequacies which I need to take action on. One being managing difficult conversations. A number of times there were disagreements across different members of the group, which moved the group in a negative direction compared to the outcomes we all seek. In this structure I think it is important to hold more regular meetings to address and air out the potential speed bumps that everyone has. This will also allow for a more democratic selection of the direction of the group, allowing all members to be heard and voting on contentious issues. Spreading the responsibility across all group members, increasing the accountability and decreasing workload      This flat management structure makes accountability tricky and allowing contentious issues to be aired reduces friction between group members and makes them feel heard and a part of the team. Having a way to with no judgement air these concerns is something I will need to figure out, such that all parties' concerns are addressed and the desired outcomes of each member are sufficiently satisfied. I am further seeking guidance from Nic when we return from break to exercise diplomacy within the group.      Having a better understanding of the tasks that are required of the group will allow me to more effectively direct the members of the team to individual outcomes. When we receive a new task, next being the testing document I plan to be both more specific and broader on each team member's responsibilities.
high.result: Designating responsibility is not something I typically gravitate to. However in this environment spreading responsibilities seemed worth the payout in this environment. The peers I work with each week are quite like minded and took well to the idea of being more effective and efficient. They posed many great ideas to increase efficiency. This was different in this flatter management structure, working with suggestions rather than giving direction as the requirement of managing fellow students. I got the agreement for work outputs to be dictated by each individual, who have different values, expectations and outside commitments.      I was not perfect at this and there is definitely an opportunity to grow, learning to be done. The payoff seems to be better than I expected, not only exercising the persuasion muscle for team mates but also reducing overall workload for my peers. This was an excellent decision and I would only have small changes if I was to start this semester again, perhaps documenting more so. Accountability of each team member comes from a strong understanding of each team member's motivations, values and ability to commit to the unit. Clearing up misconceptions early was an excellent decision as peers and tutors start off all on the same page.      Setting a standard of work, clear expectations and deadlines at the start of the semester worked nicely. This got better week on week. With all peers contributing their ideas and perspectives, I believe gave each team member more ownership of the tasks they were completing each week and therefore higher quality work was produced. Since each member read every summary to complete their SGTA tasks feedback was coming from every member, increasing individual accountability.      The group sessions focusing on training and development for all peers went well, with each piece of feedback being actioned in a timely manner and the quality of work increasing each time. I perhaps should have taken a more hands off approach here getting the person that was meant to proof read the section to do this work.       There were positive and negative outcomes from the different contentious issues between me and another team member. In some instances I addressed these and we came to an agreement in other instances I let petty comments slide as they did not impact the outcomes of the project. Perhaps they did, as they did cause damage to the cohesiveness of the group. Currently this is intangible but I will monitor this in future weeks. Although there are still some issues that exist between team members, the group dynamic is getting better.       The time management piece was considerably better. Still a slight imbalance from the amount all team members contributed but overall no complaints. No drastic agenda items for workload balance have been required up to this point. With responsibilities being set early, there seems to be a system of self accountability on each individual of the group each week.

high.situation: During week one we needed to understand everyone needs and set our goals in a charter together in order to make an RGM machine.
high.task_action: During the team charter process we had to set a common goal but before we agreed on that we first needed to share our needs, strengths and weaknesses. We set SMART (specific, measurable, attainable, relevant and time-bound) goals that motivated us to allow the team to function at the peak performance. Afterwards, we assessed each of the team members answers and picked the best fitting goal that we all agreed on.
high.result: The outcome of this allowed us to fully understand each others wants and needs then using this to set a team goal that we can all work towards together.

high.situation: We needed to find a solution for one of the components as it faced issues regarding moving the ping pong ball down the stairs.
high.task_action: During our session, we discussed the issue since the ping pong balls did not ricochet off the object. We created several strategies, such as changing the distance between each thing, changing the angle of reflection and increasing the size of the ricochet object. We also tried using different balls for the movement. We measured the reliability of each idea to ensure the best success.
high.result: Through this brainstorming and innovative ideas, we could find a reliable solution for the issue.

high.situation: During the semester we needed to understand everyone situations like availabilities and communicate in order to make meetings and get things set up together as a group in informal meetings.

high.situation: We as a team needed to start working on our individual Rube Goldberg Machine components.
high.task_action: We set a hard deadline for when we wanted all the machines and individual component videos to be completed (21st of October) and expected each team member to hold themselves accountable and deliver what was expected of them in a timely manner. We outlined a few expectations beforehand, such as that we wanted the videos to be filmed solely in landscape mode to make the final video more cohesive and that the footage should be relatively high quality. Members of the group also discussed with each other how they were planning on conducting the transitions between each element. For example, two members were using water bottles as a transition device, and had a conversation about what brand they were planning on using to make it as cohesive as possible. Throughout the week, team members sent videos of their machines into our group chat and uploaded them in the shared Google Drive for easier access. We also held team members who seemed to be lagging behind accountable by asking them where they were at with their machine and assessing to see if they needed any help by encouraging collaboration. Furthermore, we were all transparent with any issues we were having or any problems that could implicate the timely delivery of our components. For example, some team members had assessment tasks from other subjects that they were prioritizing which we all understood would take more leeway.
high.result: The result were 5 videos of every team member's two components that could be combined into a final video achieved through consistently holding people accountable.

high.situation: I needed to test, verify and validate my aspect of a Rube Goldberg Machine for a major client

high.result: As a result of the testing, verification and validation, the machine is hence more reliable and still suits the constraints set out by the client, creating a more effective machine.

high.result: We are currently in week 6 and have resulted in nearly finishing our detailed design. high.situation: Working on our preliminary design - that is, brainstorming and creating the series of events and the formulation of the Rube Goldberg Machine.
high.task_action: We utilised tools including a zoom whiteboard and Miro to effectively communicate and visualise our ideas. high.situation:
You and your team of engineers will undergo the planning, designing, construction and presentation of a Rube Goldberg Machine (AKA: RGM), over a 13-week period. One of the challenges given to our new team, was meeting each other for the first time and working together to accomplish the RGM. 

 

Task:

During week 1, we engaged with introductions and tasteful banter to establish a more relaxed and comfortable working environment, from here the team briefly did research on the task at hand, brief brainstorming happened as the ‘miro’ team charter took shape.

 

During week 2, we were somewhat acquainted and beginning to fall into our roles, each member was assigned their role through volunteering, this way each member can focus on their strongest attributes toward the team’s completion of the project. Communication was established through the use of ‘WhatsApp’s group chat function, which allowed for communication outside of class with the entire team.

 

Action:

During week 1, the 6-man group established relations for a productive workplace environment and begun work on the idea generation phase of the design process, through the use of specialized software that allows for simultaneous contributions from each team member.

 

During week 2, a communications structure was formed through the use of portable software (WhatsApp) to communicate with the entire team outside of designated times. The formation of effective teams was the primary goal of the first 2 weeks, as teamwork is essential to our completion of this task.
high.result: The outcome of the groups interactions effectively established a proper team, with the designation of roles, a system of democracy to decide how we progress through the task and the introduction of additional communication methods. The result from our action portrays the transition from shy strangers to confident engineers.

high.situation: Reflecting upon my time and resource management through this past semester, I notice that this had become a lacking skill of mine during the start of my study at Macquarie. As since, beginning I’ve had to face many problems of varying types. Eventually through trial and error and help from my peers I have been able to solve these problems by, beginning to adjust my attitude and habits to further aid my future life as a student and eventually an engineer.
high.task_action: Undergoing attempts to improve my time and resource management, I have attempted to use several methods such as, to-do listing suggested by my ENG1050 tutor, prioritising tasks, improving my organisation, removing unnecessary distractions and improving my overall health. Thusly I have explored all of these options and created a subsequent approach to my time and resource management.
high.result: The approach I have now devised is to use to-do listing, prioritising tasks, removing unnecessary distractions and improving my overall health. By creating to-do listings, I am now able to layout all future tasks for each and the subsequent order in which I must complete each task, indirectly creating a prioritised order of tasks. Hence this is aided by removing unnecessary distractions from my workstation, by moving my workstation to a quieter room in the house and placing my mobile phone in a separate room whilst I am studying. Another addition is improving my overall health, lately I have been falling ill a lot due to unnecessary stress and not keeping a regular sleep schedule and/or not eating properly. I have now begun to prioritize improving these items, as pushing myself to the breaking point not healthy.

high.situation:
In the ENGG1050 unit, I have worked in a team consisting of four other undergraduate engineering students on a bi-weekly basis with the goal of ultimately constructing a Rube Goldberg Machine. To produce this machine, our group has worked over the course of 12 weeks to brainstorm, conceptualise, design, construct and analyse our RGM. Throughout this process, we have needed to make minor (and sometimes major) changes to our design due to the poor execution of ideas, constraints arising from severe weather, inefficient components, low reliability and other factors. This need to alter the initial conceptual design is sometimes a point of contention between group members, as we often have differences in opinion on creative matters. Additionally, minor changes need to be made accordingly to the events within each persons section resultant of inconsistencies in performance. Each team member has now constructed all elements of their RGM, however some of these events are not consistent enough to warrant their inclusion in the final video. 

TASK: As a result of this particular concern, we have been tasked with effectively deciding a means of quantitatively assessing components of our RGM. This needs to be done so that the individual events within each team members section is reliable and consistent enough to record a successful take of the operation of these events with ease.

ACTION: In order to complete the the quantitative analysis of RGM components, our group has designed a means of testing the reliability of each event. Over the break, each team member constructed the components that make up their section of the RGM, and videos of these components in operation were sent to the group chat (WhatsApp messaging). It was decided that each team member was also to test each RGM component a minimum of 10 times, and to note the number of successes and failures associated with each event. Each team member has conducted repeat testing of their RGM components on a weekly basis since the break, and this data has continued to be published to the group using Excel. Using this data, I have constructed vertical column graphs comparing the percentage reliability of each individuals' events, in order to discern the success of these components. The Excel graphs update every time new data is published by a team member.
high.result: Each team member now has a quantitative understanding of the reliability of each of their events, and a clear visual depiction of how this reliability has changed over the last 6 weeks. This data is also available to all group members, allowing our team as a whole to make informed decisions on aspects of our RGM that need to be improved upon.

high.situation: We needed to prepare and give an oral presentation weekly for our tutors so they could see what are Rube Goldberg Machine is and how we plan to make it.
high.task_action: Preparing the oral presentation is the easy part as it only needed to be a few minutes long and had to show a small portion of the work we had done earlier on in the week, and as a team making the presentation was quite simple and easy. Giving the oral presentation however was difficult as it meant I had to talk to an audience of my peers and public speaking is one of my self-identified weaknesses. To overcome this problem and to make sure I do not screw up the presentation I made sure to practice giving the presentation multiple times to ensure I do not screw it up in the Posse meetings.
high.result: Practicing the presentation proved to be helpful as the presentation I had given was not too bad, I stuck to the script and I had not stuttered or blanked midway through the presentation.

high.situation:
Following up on the first 5 weeks of ENG1050, from week 6 to week 11 as part of team C15 we have had the massive task of innovating and validating our RGM design. As the next step in our design process, we had to finalise our preliminary designs and began constructing our RGM. We had to overcome reliability issues across all of our events implementing the “test, verification, validation, and certification” processes to finalise these events.
 
Task: For the past few weeks we have been tasked with the small-scale testing, full scale testing and overall reliability improvements of each event. As one of the few teams doing the whole RGM as one machine, we have run into a lot of problems requiring a lot of testing, innovation and problem solving to improve and finalise our designs.
 
Action: As a group we had to meticulously plan and organise the materials we needed, the times we were meeting and how to set up the machine. Initially when setting up the RGM we ran into a lot of problems. We began testing the first and second events and realised that our small-scale reliabilities did not translate well to the full-scale testing. The led to a lot of innovation and changing of designs within the first few events and the rest of the RGM. With continuous changes made to every event it was crucial that we set up the events right as we only had one shot to get it right. Unlike the other teams who could take days/weeks to perfect their events we ran into time constrains which meant our events were not as perfect as I would have liked however I am pleased with what we got done and happy that the construction is finished.
high.result: For the first 2 events we had to improve the reliability of the golf ball rolling down a table and knocking into a pendulum. Our reliability was basically non-existent with the ball rolling off the table, bouncing unpredictably and not going where we needed it. We had to test just the first event about 20 or so times before we got it right. To verify and validate the reliability of the event we had to make some major improvements with the limited resources we had. We found using tape as a sort of springy barrier worked well to dampen the bouncing of the golf ball. The innovative thinking that we as a group had to come up with was rather difficult yet rewarding when it worked. We also decided to split up the events to make sure they would work seamlessly whilst not taking us hours to get that one perfect event. We also realised that it was taking us a very long time to set up each event, with our first meeting together, we successfully created and filmed 7 events over 6 hours in week 10. We then planned for another meeting the following week, in which we finalised our RGM filming.

high.situation: In week 7 of this semester, within my ENGG1050 project group, Damien, Keval and I had to perform our detailed design presentation. high.task_action: By this point in the semester, we were all well known to each other and were very comfortable in exchanging ideas. high.situation: MISUNDERSTANDINGS AND MISSED DEADLINES RESULTED FROM A BREAKDOWN IN TEAM MEMBER COMMUNICATION DURING A COLLABORATIVE PROJECT AT WORK.   My job was to identify the team's communication problems and come up with a plan to make the conversations more efficient.      Action:       To candidly address the communication difficulties we were experiencing, I called a team meeting. I urged everyone to express their opinions without holding back. I paid close attention to the worries and recommendations made by every team member throughout the meeting. Together, we determined that a lack of regular updates, varying communication preferences, and unclear expectations were the main contributors to the breakdown in communication. In light of our conversation, I suggested putting in place a communication plan that would take into account everyone's preferences and include frequent status updates, roles and responsibilities that are clearly defined, and a variety of communication channels. Additionally, I recommended scheduling weekly check-in meetings to make sure everyone was on track and to quickly address any problems.
high.result: We saw a notable improvement in team communication following the implementation of the communication plan and the holding of frequent check-in meetings. There were fewer misunderstandings, more deadlines were consistently met, and team morale increased. Furthermore, team members expressed a greater sense of worth and involvement in the project, which fostered a more cooperative work atmosphere.

Likewise, managing our material resources is extremely crucial for the designs so that we can decide a) which series are difficult and simple to construct and b) which group member's are best suited to the completion of each series with their given home resources.

high.situation: This semester in ENGG3050, we were asked to research a startup and create a 4 minute presentation on a specific aspect of the company. This work was to be completed in groups of 5, and team members would need to collaborate in order to form cohesive presentations that flow on from each other. An extensive list of startups was provided to choose from, and group members could be chosen from students present in class. Specific research topics would be on a particular aspect of the startup's business operations and entrepreneurial journey, adhering to the Business Model Canvas components.       The 5 research topics included: management structure and people/company culture, funding process and general business case, growth strategy and scaling journey, market opportunity and technical edge, and customer segment and product placement.       TASK: After forming groups, research would need to be undertaken to learn more about the startup and create a presentation relevant to the chosen company. We were encouraged to select a startup in an industry or market sector that interested us, to make researching the company more engaging and generate more enthusiasm during the presentation. After choosing a startup within the group, specific aspects of the startup would be selected by each member to conduct detailed research on.       Prior to week 9, when the first member of our group would be presenting, my group planned to meet in-person and conduct research on the startup together, to allow for better collaboration and to encourage each other in our work.       General research on the company would first be undertaken, to gain an understanding of who the startup is and what they do on the whole. In conducting this research, insight into the company's global expansion and inter-continental reach would be completed to develop an understanding of the importance of global perspectives and interactions. For some, companies, their geographic location or language may prevent them from expanding into new markets or making connections with overseas suppliers, manufacturers and customers. It is important to consider the global presence and the social, political and environmental landscape of a company, particularly when the company is from an area the researcher is unfamiliar with, considering standards and practices may be drastically different to that of Australia.      ACTION: In my group, we selected to research and present on the EV charging and energy solutions startup Wallbox, which was founded in 2015 by two ex-tesla employees in Barcelona, Spain. After group deliberation, we decided that I would present on Topic D - Market Opportunity and Technological Edge.       In researching Wallbox, I learned about how they progressed from presenting their prototype at startup pitch competitions to gain exposure and funds to making partnerships with international manufacturing chains to aid in their supply chains on a global scale. In the span of a decade, Wallbox progressed from a startup of two engineers, to a company with over 500 employees, 1 million customers worldwide and a valuation of $320 million.       After gaining funding through winning a startup competition in 2016, angel investors in the late 2010s and 5 rounds of Series A VC funding in 2020, Wallbox was able to produce their first product for distribution within Spain. I found it interesting to learn about the large extent of the funding required to support the creation of an MVP and production, even though it was still limited to within the company's founding country at this stage.       Wallbox initially had difficulty expanding their products into global markets, as company employees had little experience with overseas markets and foreign supply chains. Furthermore, the language barrier prevented CEO's Enric and Eduard from comfortably contacting potential business partners and investors. The somewhat turbulent political landscape in Spain combined with the lack of local manufacturers meant Wallbox had to look for alternative ways of designing and manufacturing their product. To overcome the language barrier and challenges imposed by the region the startup was founded in, Wallbox made the decision to hire employees overseas with better knowledge of the local area, to help assist them in global manufacturing and distribution. Knowing China's importance in global manufacturing given its ability to produce products on a large scale at low prices, Wallbox entered into a partnership with Changchun FAWSN, to become a key player in EV charger manufacturing in the region. The challenges faced by Wallbox in expanding into international markets reflects their inexperience and lack of knowledge of the global landscape.
high.result: In researching Wallbox and their experiences with global manufacturing and trade, I gained a greater insight into the global considerations and experiences that need to be taken into account when making business deals or considering international partnerships. While it's easy to assume that all humans, or in this instance CEOs and business owners, have similar views and experiences to that of yourself, however this simply isn't true. Each individual is uniquely shaped by the environment they live in and their life experiences. How people choose to respond to these often negative life experiences is what shapes them as a person.       Similarly, companies are moulded by the industry they operate in, their country of origin and the business decisions made by those in charge of the company. Particularly when considering countries other than Australia, the living conditions, political stability, civil status and environmental landscape can influence company experiences and culture.       In researching Spanish startup Wallbox, I have learned about the factors that come into play when making decisions on a global scale and how differences in opinion and experience can be resultant of unique environments within a particular country. This experience has helped me develop a global perspective and understanding of the unique circumstances that shape individuals and businesses on an international scale.

high.situation: During my Second year at university, I participated in a group project for our engineering course. Our assignment was to design a 200mm cube that allowed marbles to travel through a series of interconnected events. A conflict arose between our team and the Structures team regarding a critical component needed to achieve a high distinction. This disagreement risked delaying the project and creating lasting tension between the teams, potentially jeopardizing our collaborative efforts and project success.      TASK: My responsibility was to mediate between the two teams to find a compromise that would enable us to stay on schedule and meet the expectations of both the Motions and Structures teams. I needed to facilitate a solution that would keep the project moving forward without sacrificing the integrity of the final product.      ACTION: To address the conflict, I took the following steps:      	* INITIAL MEETINGS: I arranged individual meetings with both teams to thoroughly understand their perspectives and concerns. This helped me gain a detailed understanding of the issues at hand.   	* JOINT DISCUSSION: I facilitated a joint meeting where both teams could express their needs and limitations. During this discussion, I stressed the importance of teamwork and mutual respect.   	* BRAINSTORMING SOLUTIONS: Together, we brainstormed potential compromises. One suggestion was to incorporate a rolling staircase mechanism powered by a DC motor, allowing the marbles to exit through the top of the cube.   	* DEVELOPING A REVISED PLAN: Based on our brainstorming session, we created a revised project plan that included a clear timeline for the initial launch and subsequent updates, along with checkpoints to monitor progress and quality.   	* ONGOING COMMUNICATION: I set up regular follow-up meetings to track the project's progress and ensure both teams remained aligned, promptly addressing any new issues that arose.
high.result: The conflict was successfully resolved, and we launched a prototype that satisfied both teams. The initial cube launch was completed on schedule, enabling the Motions team to take advantage of the delivery and presentation. Subsequent updates were released as planned, maintaining the quality standards of the product. This outcome not only achieved the project’s objectives but also enhanced the working relationship between the teams. The positive result underscored the importance of structured communication and collaborative problem-solving.

high.situation: I was developing a new engineering solution to address the parking problem at Macquarie University in the 5-7 workshop weeks. Given the innovative nature of my approach, which aimed to create a system that could efficiently determine the availability of parking spots and alert users when they were full, I was keen on protecting my intellectual property (IP) to secure a competitive edge in the market. However, considering my limited resources and expertise in IP law, I faced challenges in determining the most effective pathway to protect and commercialise my technology.
high.task_action: My task was to identify and implement a suitable IP protection strategy and patent law that would not only safeguard my invention but also facilitate its successful commercialisation. I needed to evaluate various options, such as licensing, joint ventures, spin-offs, acquisitions, and in-house development, and select the most feasible approach for my situation.      To address this, I formed a dedicated working group comprising engineers, legal advisors, and business development experts. I began by conducting a thorough analysis of my technology to determine which aspects could be protected by patents, trademarks, copyrights, or trade secrets. I then evaluated the commercial pathways available to me:      1. Licensing: I considered licensing my technology to established companies with the resources to bring it to market quickly.      2. Joint Ventures: I explored potential partnerships with other firms to share the development risks and costs.      3. Spin-offs: I discussed the possibility of creating a subsidiary focused solely on the new technology.      4. Acquisition: I looked into acquiring or merging with a company that had complementary technology and market presence.      5. In-house Development: I assessed my capability to develop and commercialise the technology independently.      Using the gallery method of brainstorming, I generated and assessed these options, considering factors such as resource availability, expertise, time-to-market, and potential return on investment. Each option was evaluated based on predefined parameters, including cost, complexity, and strategic fit with my long-term goals.
high.result: After a detailed evaluation, I decided to pursue a combination of licensing and in-house development. Licensing the core technology allowed me to generate immediate revenue and leverage the commercialization capabilities of my partners. Simultaneously, I retained in-house development of specific components to maintain control over key innovations and ensure continued improvement of my technology.      As a result:      	* I secured multiple licensing agreements with leading companies in my industry, generating a steady stream of revenue.   	* I successfully patented several critical aspects of my technology, providing strong IP protection and enhancing my market position.   	* My in-house development team continued to innovate, leading to the creation of additional patentable features and improvements.   	* The dual approach enabled me to enter the market faster while maintaining strategic control over my most valuable IP assets.      Overall, the chosen IP protection strategy and patent law not only safeguarded my invention but also facilitated its successful commercialization, contributing to the company's growth and market competitiveness.

Copyrights: Protect original creative works, such as books, music, software, or other forms of expression.      4. Trade Secrets: Protect confidential information that provides a competitive advantage, such as customer lists, manufacturing processes, or secret formulas. high.situation: I was assigned the task of delivering a 4-minute presentation on the growth strategy and scaling journey of Dronehub. This task required not only in-depth research but also the ability to present complex information in an engaging and clear manner. Adding to the challenge, I had not presented in a while due to interruptions caused by the COVID-19 pandemic, which made me feel quite nervous about my ability to deliver an effective speech.
high.task_action: To address this challenge, I undertook a multi-step approach. First, I conducted thorough research to gather comprehensive data on Dronehub’s strategies, successes, and challenges. This involved reviewing company reports and industry articles. With a wealth of information at hand, the next step was to sift through and identify the most relevant and compelling points for my audience. I then organised these points into a coherent structure, beginning with an overview of Dronehub, followed by its growth strategies, scaling methods, and future projections. Recognising the importance of engagement, I incorporated visual aids such as charts and graphs to illustrate key data points and trends. I also added anecdotes and case studies to bring the story to life. Practicing the speech multiple times was crucial. This helped me refine my delivery, ensuring clarity and confidence. I timed each segment to adhere strictly to the 4-minute limit, making sure that every second was utilised effectively. I also sought feedback from peers and adjusted my presentation based on their input to enhance its overall impact. To overcome my nervousness, I applied critical thinking to develop a detailed and engaging speech. I reminded myself of my past successes and broke the presentation down into manageable sections, focusing on delivering each part effectively.
high.result: The presentation was a success. It was well-received by both my classmates and the instructor, who praised the depth of research and the engaging delivery. The use of visual aids and anecdotes was highlighted as particularly effective in maintaining interest and conveying complex information clearly. This experience significantly enhanced my creative thinking skills.
