high.situation: We needed to develop creative ways to develop our rube Goldberg machine so it maintains a practical function of switching off an alarm clock, but also make it creative enough so it is interesting to watch from the viewers perspective.
high.task_action: After discussion with the team members, using the zoom breakout rooms, phone conversations and text conversations, it was understood that rube Goldberg machines function on the 6 simple machines; pulleys, levers, inclined planes, wheel and axle, wedges and screws. This information was then included onto a Miro board so we could brainstorm creative solutions involving these machines. For example, wheel and axles can be accomplished via use of a toy car, which uses the wheel and axles to roll down another simple machine, an inclined plane. Once we had brainstormed possible solutions, we then determined which ones would be possible to create with the equipment limitations of each member. In this way we would be able to dedicate time and resources to the creative solutions we can produce, instead of having to apply effort or purchasing resources to solutions we can't accomplish.
high.result: The result is the ability for each team member to successfully be able to accomplish a certain solution towards the project using the materials they have, without the need to purchase any items. These solutions will involve the application of the 6 simple machines and therefore be both entertaining to view, while also being true to the essence of what a rube Goldberg machine is.

high.situation: Good teamwork and communication skills are required in this unit due to working in groups to make a RGM.

high.situation:
As a part of 5 members team, I along with my other team members were tasked with building a Rube Goldberg Machine which equally serves the purpose of sustainability, user friendliness, and economical. We had a very limited time to design, build and solve any constraints of this unique project. On top of that, I had to enhance my team working, formal procedures and online based working skills by regularly submitting minutes, agenda and updating our clients. We had limited time of 12 weeks to come up with a functional Rube Goldberg Machine

TASK: Our main task was to build a Rube Goldberg Machine by the end of the semester while ensuring various project requirements. These are but not limited to sustainable. economical, environment friendly and unique user experience. In addition to that, I had to submit minutes, agenda, progress update presentations to our clients every week.

ACTION: To achieve our goal, we had to take several actions. On the very first week, we had a team meeting to get to know each other and discuss our availability. Each member was equally rostered for the weekly minute and agenda submission and to do the client presentations. By the next couple of weeks, we brainstormed and set our project goals and objectives. We had our detailed deadline and time frame as the following:

	* Design Phase: Week 6
	* Initial Testing : Week 7-9
	* Reliability Constraints and Problem Solving : Week 9-10
	* Video of RGM : Week 11-12
	* Final Presentation: Week 13

These time frame was designed in a way that we have at least one week of buffer time in case if anything doesn't go according to our plan. We had necessary backup members on standby if one of the team member failed to deliver their assigned part of the project. We regularly had team meetings outside of designated workshop to keep up with the project deadline and requirements. 

The next big challenge I faced was resource management. I had to come up with resources that is regularly used in our day to day activities and serves the purpose of sustainability. 

For my initial components, I had my reliability figures ready by the end of week 7 and shot my final video by week 9. This gave me enough time to make further improvements and help my team mates whenever it was necessary. When the question came to finding suitable resources, I took the inspiration from my passion of cooking. Most of the parts of my RGM components were sourced from food packaging boxes, containers which I planned to recycle later. 

To improve my team working, formal procedures, and online-based working skills, I attended regular meetings to discuss our progress and challenges. I also submitted minutes and agendas to keep track of project development and updated our clients regularly.
high.result:
Despite the challenges we faced, we were hoping to complete our project on time and within the given constraints. We will present a functional Rube Goldberg Machine that meets the project requirements of sustainability, user-friendliness, and economic viability. By using recycled food packaging boxes and containers, I was able to contribute to the sustainability aspect of the project.

Our team working skills also significantly improved throughout the project. We regularly communicated and coordinated with each other to make sure everyone was on track with their assigned tasks. The formal procedures we implemented, such as regular meetings, minute and agenda submissions, and progress update presentations to clients, helped us keep track of our project development and ensured that we were all working towards the same goal.

In the end, our hard work and dedication are paying off as we received positive feedback from both our clients and our peers. Overall, this experience taught me the importance of time and resource management, effective communication, and teamwork in achieving a common go

high.situation:

high.situation:
My team and I hosted a team meeting on 12/05/23 to record footage of one of our RGM’s components, and to also create a presentation around its design and features. on the following Monday, I gave this presentation to my tutor’s posse.__

TASK:

Prior to the 12th, my team and I drafted a set of tasks in an agenda, which were to be completed throughout the meeting. These tasks included:

-        Record and edit footage of component Four for presentation purposes.

-        Discuss various properties of component Four, such as its physical implementation and design features.

-        Outline its reliability, and key strengths with respect to our team charter.

-        Create a presentation that displays our video for component Four and summarises the properties which we discussed.

ACTION:

We began our team meeting by first consolidating our footage of component four, where Hamim (who was responsible for the module) had recorded its features and forwarded them to us. As a group, we decided what segments we wanted to include in our final video, which Hamim edited together. In doing this, we created a continuous demonstration of component four in action, which stresses both its features and processes.

Following this, my group and I discussed which of our component’s properties (e.g., used materials, module goals, etc.) were the most important to justify in our presentation. Due to the breadth of possible talking points, combined with time constraints, we decided to generally focus on properties that were both relevant to the component’s functionality and design. As such, we agreed to talk about the overall purpose of the component, the functionality, reliability, and physical limitations of each module, and the themes from our team charter which the component reflects.

After we established what content was to be covered in our presentation, we finalised our meeting by creating our presentation collaboratively on Google Slides. We decided to separate our slides structurally with respect to our talking points, such that it would be easy for our audience to ‘encapsulate’ a given idea, theme, or photo.
high.result: From this meeting, we were able to create footage for our RGM video which can be used in future presentations. Furthermore, we also outlined a general strategy for creating our footage by emphasising the features of Hamim’s video which we collectively appreciated. Finally, we found that our approach to creating this presentation was positively received by our audience. By reusing and refining these strategies for future presentations, we hope to maintain this positive reception.

high.situation:
In our team Head on hat, all the team members are working full time along with full time university, which makes it quite difficult for us to manage time for the weekly meeting. This was one of the major problems for us when we first started as a group to separate the time for the weekly meeting. Similarly, our RGM revolves around being efficient and recycling the items that are available to us at home, which means we need to build a lot of the complex machine using the everyday items that are in our home.

TASK: the major issue for us was making time for the meeting as we are required to have meeting weekly for the project with all the team members having different time schedules availabilities. It made it difficult for us to find a proper time for the meeting as 3 of the team members could do in the morning and would not be able to make it on the evening and the other 2 would not be able to do it on the morning as they would have their classes in the morning. Along with this the resources for the RGM were also a issues for us as we only planned to use the things are available for us at home but we also wanted to include fun element on our project which would require us to buy some materials which can be expensive and would also go against the main objective and theme of the project for us.

ACTION: during a team meeting through the Zoom, we decided to have a common calendar (Google calendar) for each week where each team member would put their availabilities and we could check it each week. We also concluded that if some days if some team members were not able to attend the meeting, we would put everything we did and discussed in a google docs which would allow us to have a look at it later. For the resources, we decided that we would try to stick to a 20-dollar budget if we were required to buy anything. Along with that we decided on sharing some materials with the team members. This could allow us to stay on the theme of our project as we would be staying in the recycling the materials.
high.result: Making the google calendar helped us tremendously as we were able to find a common ay where al of us would be in university and would be able to have a meeting, as all the team members preferred the in-person meetings. Along with that the google docs allowed to put our work in the in the document which allowed us to track the progress of each member. Similarly, the idea of sharing the materials allowed us to make our RGM more interesting. Along with that the budgeting our expenses helped us to stay with in the theme of our RGM.

high.situation:
Through the early theme creation and brainstorming of the conceptual design, challenges arose in the form of team members struggling to contribute due to a lack of structure within the initial stages of idea generation. This came about as a result of the vastness of choice provided by the single criterion of ‘coming up with an idea’ and the general consensus was a formal direction needed to be created to reach a decision.

Task: Produce a decision/concept from a collaborative brainstorming activity. Generate several concepts and options for the RGM and incorporate them into a portion of the design solution.

Action: Initially, my approach to begin the process of conceiving ideas was proposing general concepts each person could develop to be assessed at the end of the session. However, the general experience of this process was prescribing ideas only solved part of our problem, with no determined way of optimization or assessment, ideas could be created but not a decision. The next plan for organization was the construction of a rigid framework with conceptual criteria set out for an idea to be reviewed and a numerical goal for total outcomes of brainstorming to be met within the session. Thereby, every part of the process was predetermined with opportunities for individual creativity.

Results: Every brainstorming session since the implementation of this structure approach has yielded actionable and unanimously accepted results contributed to the RGM. For example, the formation of events for the Conceptual design were laid out in this manner, each group member formed and included their own creative and technical analysis throughout the RGM which met the goals of flexibility and feasibility.

Learning: The best method of conducting groupwork and delivering an actionable outcome is by a predetermined scaffolding which can give group members definite direction as to what should be achieved within collaborative sessions. The collective nature of this approach also adds to the inclusivity of the group meetings by ensuring all contributions are a part of the analysis stage of brainstorming, delivering a quantifiable outcome as a team unit.

 

Accountability in a Team Operation

Situation: The individual burden of developing an agenda and meeting minutes each week was discovered to be overwhelming for group members attempting to contribute to design work whilst taking notes and organizing a reflection for each workshop. This placed stress on the group as one person would not be involved in mainline work for the next presentation, limiting the ideas received in that session and the subsequent agenda deadline added to the already high workload of that respective group member.

Task: Produce a document containing the information of attendees, agenda, meeting minutes, and reflection based on the minutes, for each weekly ENGG1050 workshop.

Action: I suggested the agenda be a collaborative submission for the minute taker. Every group member would contribute a portion of work in note taking, workshop planning and reflection writing in order to finish the work in a timely manner. This also gives everyone in the group an opportunity to work on the design of the RGM instead of being inundated with the agenda. Just like the idea generation process, the next phase of this teamwork process was to organize the submission with a generalized, preconceived scaffolding to populate with information. Doing so reduces the collation time of notes and allows the document to be simply populated and submitted.
high.result:
Further efficiency and less stress are now evident within the team. High quality minutes are submitted on time every week with positive feedback from the tutors. Because more time is now spent on the next week’s presentation, the quality of work being presented to the course stream is exceeding the goals the group set and increasing the speed at which said components of work can be produced.

Learning: Complete group involvement in every stage of the workshop process allows me to balance my knowledge, skills and workload with the rest of my team members to efficiently conduct group work. This accountability for all submissions also has strengthen my trust in the team as each person can assist one another throughout the week and submissions are held to a common quality standard, ensuring me and every other person is satisfied with the work produced.

high.result: During the Week 3 meeting the Team member then addressed what had happened in Week 2. I resulted to humbly apologize for pushing the matter in Week 2 with the Team member and admitted to strongarming a decision that was technically invalid as no one said anything during the vote. I took my ego out of the equation in this situation and put forth a better demeanor to move on with what we needed to do.

high.situation: For my ENG1050 workshop, my group was tasked with choosing a theme for the Rube Goldberg Machine we will be building this semester. high.result: Each suggested theme was evaluated based on a selection criterion of complexity, ease of implementation, time, cost, and whether the theme is exciting to watch. high.situation: In order to create an RGM machine, on week-by-week basis, we are to form an effective team that has the perseverance, will to deliver and flexibility to progress the project and develop individually as engineers.
high.task_action: Every week we are required to go through a session, where we plan out our RGM in stages starting with identifying the problem at hand and then going through design steps as part of the engineering process. Using open discussion and brainstorming on Miro and Discord, we would evaluate our ideas, choosing the ones with most viability and validity to our project. To be as productive as possible, we aimed to do all our tasks within the session with quality and checking our checklist.
high.result:
The result was being able to progress with our RGM effectively by checking out the design ideas of each member and breaking down the methodology and tools required to accomplish those ideas. This consolidated our position as an effective team.

Reflection: Initially doing this unit online seemed challenging and unfamiliar since for practicals so far, I have always done them face-to-face in session. Especially in a unit that requires effective and mostly stable collaboration between a team. Thus, if any one of us were away or experienced connectivity issues, the rest would need a flexible mindset to make up for anyone missing and create quality solutions easy to show to the missing members. As well as the will to deliver despite being in an unfamiliar situation. Furthermore, making sure we went through everything on the checklist, in limited time required perseverance. However, as we started to work together using Miro and visualising each other’s ideas as well as discussions through zoom was reliable enough to support this project. We took a systematic approach by breaking down each members’ part into the material required, how it flows and consolidating it into diagrams.

Learning: By approaching the tasks with open discussion and collaborative mindset, we were able to create an effective team to breakdown complex problems that we encountered weekly. Viable designs were based on if they were possible to do for the group member and if they had most of the materials ready and under our constraints of buying little to no materials and prioritize used and recycled parts from around the house. Thus, the freedom of this task has allowed me to realise that projects require constraints such as themes or budget to better accomplish the goals. The need for an effective and collaborative team was thus only emboldened in order to polish ideas to make decisions and solutions.

high.situation: In my Rube Goldberg machines team, I was tasked with designing an element that not only performed its function reliably but also ensured consistent results when replicated by others. To achieve this, I meticulously considered calculations and the risks and reliability factors of my machine's components.      To provide a clearer picture of my machine's design, I began with a sequence involving a marble. Firstly, the marble would roll down a sloped zigzag path, ensuring a controlled and predictable motion. Next, it would collide with a second marble attached to a string. This collision would set off a pendulum-like motion, swinging the second marble into contact with a platform, also attached to a string. The impact would release a third marble positioned on top of the platform, initiating the next stage of the sequence.
high.task_action: Having completed the 3D design of my build, the next crucial step was ensuring its reproducibility. While initially tempted to rely on trial and error, I recognized the importance of a more systematic approach. To achieve this, I sat down with a paper, pen, and ruler and made a series of calculated actions. I meticulously calculated the mass and diameter of the marbles, the angles of the slopes, and the lengths of the strings. This comprehensive approach allowed me to anticipate and account for potential variables, ensuring consistency in the machine's performance. Following these calculations, I conducted multiple test runs to validate their efficacy in real-world conditions. Through rigorous experimentation, I confirmed that my calculations translated seamlessly into practical applications. This iterative process not only affirmed the reliability of my design but also enhanced my confidence in its reproducibility.
high.result: The result of my approach was a machine built on factual numbers, ensuring its reproducibility by others wishing to replicate the design. By basing the design on precise calculations rather than trial and error, I provided a clear blueprint for anyone seeking to construct a similar machine.

high.situation: The challenge and situation for this reflection is to reflect with a specific focus on exploring the theme of "The design process phasing and approaches." Leading up to weeks 11 my team challenged ourselves to design, construct and partially film our Rube Goldberg machine. This theme posed a challenge, as it required me to delve deeply into our methodologies and strategies that we have employed over the last 11 weeks, during the design and construction phases of our project. I Recognised the importance of effective design methodologies in ensuring the success of our machine. I understood that the success of our project would hinge on our ability to implement sound design principles and have consistent approaches and strong communication and roles throughout the entire process.
high.task_action: In response to the theme and RGM design challenge, we decided to adopt an iterative design process as our primary approach. This involved breaking down the construction of our Rube Goldberg machine into smaller, manageable segments, or events. 3 events each then got split between our team members and our goal was to ensure we each build our sections and they ensure the smooth transition and reliable execution of each event. To achieve this, we began by meticulously analysing and identifying potential weak points or areas prone to failure within each event. Through a process of constant iteration and refinement, we sought to address these issues systematically, with the aim of achieving a reliability rate of over 90% for each event. Furthermore, we recognised the importance of modular construction in facilitating our design process. We divided the machine into modular sections, with each section assigned to individual team members. This modular approach allowed us to isolate track issues, troubleshoot problems, and fix individual components without disrupting the entire setup. By compartmentalising our design in this way, we maximised efficiency and minimised the impact of potential failures, thereby enhancing the overall robustness of our machine. We also consistently gave each individual their roles in the project each week. By making sure everyone in the team had clear responsibilities, we were able to have a strong consistent approach and give
high.result: As a result of our strategic approach to design phasing and methodologies, we successfully constructed a Rube Goldberg machine that exhibited high reliability and performance during the start of filming. Our iterative design process enabled us to systematically improve the performance and reliability of each event, ensuring smooth transitions and flawless execution throughout the sequence. Additionally, our modular construction approach allowed efficient troubleshooting, further enhancing the overall reliability of our machine. Moreover, by prioritising the use of sturdy materials and components, we minimised the risk of unexpected failures during filming. The attention to detail and emphasis on reliability not only ensured the success of the project but also underscored the importance of adopting structured design methodologies and approaches in complex projects. Looking back on this experience, i have gained valuable insights into the significance of effective design phasing and approaches. We have learned that by breaking down complex projects into smaller, manageable segments and employing iterative design processes, i can systematically address issues and improve performance over time. Moving forward, we will continue to apply these lessons, recognising that sound design principles are essential for the successful execution of any project.

With so many options we have to choose between designs for the tea brewing mechanism. Finally we shortlisted only two ideas with the features where one design suggested a simpler method as immersing the leaves in the water whereas the other one is highly sophisticated  but comes with high price. Both has benefit and drawback, but the first one might take longer time but we go with the first one as per the customer needs and the budget on hand. Before making any final decision, we have analysed designs strength risks and threats where we compared lots of issues and factors contributing the success as user reliability, friendliness and preferences. I was responsible for making a list for generating and finalising list.             Action:      As a team we initiated the discussion, where we need to accomplish our solution. We first defined the problem where we have outlined our specific requirement on how we perform the brewing, how the mechanism will be carried out. There is design flow chart where we mentioned the shape and size of the product such as factors considering for the final. We had possessed detailed session where our goal was to finalize what kind of materials to use for product manufacture as iron, steel or aluminium. Some consideration and concepts developed as to use the glass materials which was the positive outcome for the brewing mechanism, such as reliability, efficiency, and user-friendliness. In addition, with the generated ideas we search some models for better performance, usability, cost price and reliability. We have the open platform where everyone had to be heard and listen, providing equal opportunity, where everyone presents their ideas, brainstorming, concerns, by facilitating the open discussion ensuring everyone had a chance to contribute. Lastly, we have a vison with the potential outcomes and risks with design. With team effort we have identified the strengths and weaknesses of most of the design that was presented by the individual team taking the considering factors such as the complexity of implementation, maintenance requirements, and overall system reliability.
high.result: As a result, we had our final design on hand which the team agreed upon, with the balance between reliability, performance, ease of implementation, user friendly, and most of the features that can be integrated for the maximum benefit. This decision making as a group together not only helped in building the functionality of the tea maker but also strengthened our team's ability to effectively communicate and collaborate with increase team bond in Project-The Tea Maker.        In addition, with the learning, team work, effective communication skills and effort from our individual experience along with the text book reference for better understandings of engineering principles and practices helped the team for better result. We are encouraged and impressed how the team work showed a positive outcome with better understanding of designing phases and the obstacles that comes with it. It is one of the greatest assets that contributed to the professional development as teamwork, effective communication, decision making, leadership and initiatives.      Deal framework:      DESCRIBE:      The soft skill that I want to reflect on is working on the design process phasing and approaches.  It is essential in the context of the situation as it plays a vital role throughout the whole process of making the project successful from start to the end.      When we had the project- The tea maker as a team we had to accomplish the goal, finish the task as a team with the given period of timeframe. But the main thing is how, where and when. This is most crucial to the whole team. Some of the specific requirements are there to implement as features and functionality with the budget provided but who is going to start, when to start and how to start the project as an individual or as a team. The point is team is formed and its there but working individually which is random and backlogging the whole set of work.      EXAMINE:      Lots of challenges to face on. I had to work from the scratch on how to accomplish the task. I was thrilled and energized to do the work but the main questions still remain on how to perform. I was very good on brainstorming, conflict management, team work and effective communication which was very helpful during this project. These skills helped to escalate the project to the next level. The very first thing was the design process phasing and how to approach it. So, we have created a group session where we will be drafting the brainstorming will all the ideas, then we tally our ideas and shortlisted with all the possible generated ideas. Such as while working on the size and dimension, team were discussing on what would be the size such as square, rectangle, oval, cup shape, irregular polygon shape. Secondly, we were working on dimension, how big it is going to be as length wise, height wise and width wise. Third, what material we are going to use such as steel, aluminium, cupper. Then after the phase is the approaching method, we are going through lots of referencing ideas which would be beneficial as to keep the water and the liquid hot at what temperature. We look for the reliability, user-friendly process where the risk is less involved. At some point of time there were places where there is conflict between ideas as not accepting the ideas as they are not agreeing on it. It was challenging at that time, but my effective communication skillset helps me out. Being good communicator, I managed the team be come to a common ground where I describe the situation, the benefit and drawback and how the project will be affected. I have created the project work flow and drat the diagram of the design process phase in the project flow chart which help to navigate and understand very easily.      ACT:      Based on my examination I came to conclude that we should always take an initiative and put our ideas and expression forward. Do not hold back and start working individually being afraid to put the      Ideas forefront. Communicate, engage and act and work together as a team. In order to strengthen my proficiency and effectively apply it in future situation, I would be working very proactively with the groups when we are working in any projects as a team. I would suggest to create a common platform where we can share some ideas on discord, whatsapp and viber so that we are in constant communication and sharing the ideas. Not only that we will share our time table for meeting and minutes so that we can work on proactively and everyone knows who is working on what and how the project is carried forward.      LEARN:      I have learnt a lot whilst doing this project. I was thinking that study and work life needs to balanced and they need to put in separate place, which is right. But what we have learnt in life with experience such as in work I have learn many skills, such as time management, work in pressurized environment, team management, effective communication and team player and accomplish work in timeframe. This skillset can be used in study and we can be beneficial from both sides for personal growth, self-confidence and personal integrity.      I would definitely say that with the project on hand working together as a team and completing the design phase with much of the intensity and research with working in tight timeframe is same as in workplace we the task need to be accomplished in time in an pressurized environment. So, the learning is always there in every corner, we need to utilize to most aspects.

high.situation:
During ENGG1050 Workshop we were assigned to work on a project on a Rube Goldberg Machine as a team of 6 people. The Miro platform was offered as a collaborative working space to work on a project. Since this platform was new to everyone, we started to explore tools and pre-built features while simultaneously getting to know each other. Working in a team is usually not my strength but this time my first impression of a team was good, starting from the beginning it felt like we are like-minded, and our traits and skills complement each other. All contacts were exchanged, a group chat was created, and we were ready to start. The only problem at this stage was the absence of one member.

TASK:  The main goal of working on this project as a team is developing and mastering a broad range of teamwork skills and soft skills which are transferrable lifelong assets that have become more important in fast-changing industries. Working on a project we first completed DISK and OCEAN tests to find out which roles in the team would suit us better based on our strengths and weaknesses.

ACTION: Starting from our first workshop there was a positive atmosphere established so everyone could feel confident and encouraged to propose any ideas, improvements, or constructive criticism. About two weeks after the start minor problems came up affecting the group performance. The issue was an imbalance in the efforts team members contributed to the process. One reason was neglecting given responsibilities and overdue tasks by one of the members which as a result led to delays at some project stages. Another reason is due to absence for the first two weeks of another team member. Since the first weeks were very important in terms of establishing a routine, I assume it was hard to merge with a group in the middle of the process and catch up. Despite all efforts of the team to introduce that person to the project as a group we still experience a lack of communication, contribution to the project, and accountability. I personally also experience some challenges doing the project. Communication skill is one of the most important soft skills and not my strongest side. As a major part of our project is presenting and writing reflections, I have to put some additional effort into it as those tasks are the hardest for me to perform.
high.result: Good thing is despite the process not being as smooth as planned we still managed to accomplish every assignment on time so far, and I still think that my team performs at a high level overall. Most of the group is responsible and dedicated which helped to balance out minor problems and overcome difficulties. To make everyone work on our goals, it was necessary to find an effective tool of persuasion to eliminate the lack of responsibility and involvement issues. The only effective method I found useful to get someone working on an assigned task is chasing a person and gently reminding them many times what needs to be done offering any assistance if needed. Using peer pressure gives a positive result. But I found it harder to get someone involved and participate actively in the project, one of the reasons might be online learning, being apart from each other doesn’t give an immersive experience of the process and make it tempting to just hide behind the screen and stay quiet. This is still something to keep working on.

high.situation: We needed to increase our Rube Goldberg Machine reliability as a whole. In doing so we need to identify the issues and problems we were having with each individual component to devise a way to resolve it.
high.task_action:
To determine the issues we used brainstorming sessions and root cause analysis as a group to determine the possible issues that we were having. In doing so we looked at each person's chain of events and their individual components as well as the task they were attempting to complete . This allowed us to understand what issues and problems were arising as we all discussed what they might be and also possible solutions to resolve them. To do this we asked these questions; what happened, why did it happen, what can we do to fix it. Which formed the bases of our root cause analysis. 

Results:  The results of this root cause analysis allowed us to increase the reliability of our Rube Goldberg Machine greatly. This can be seen with our reliability increasing from 4% to 52% reliable. This process also allowed us to as a group understand each component in greater detail and share ideas on how to resolve the issue of each machine as often the issue we determined to be most affecting the components was a simple one that was overlooked.

high.situation:
We had to begin constructing the action events of our RGM’s.

 

TASKS/ACTION:

In preparation for week 8’s workshop we were asked to create a presentation on our RGM components individual reliabilities. Consequently, we were required to begin construction of the RGM events. We aimed to distribute the events equally amongst group members in our pre-workshop meeting. In this meeting, we created a Miro board with each unique action event and the materials required in each component. Action events were subsequently split between members based on who possessed necessary materials for a particular component. For example, I had string required for the zipline event and I had the cloth for our tablecloth event, and therefore I was assigned these two events. Following this meeting, each member was given the responsibility to construct and test their event, approximately 10-20 times, to determine reliability and then fill in their events subsection in our presentation for week 8. By the time of this workshop, we had tested a total of 6 events. I had tested the zipline event, however not extensively due to time restraints, and I could not acquire the slinky required in the tablecloth event, hence failing to test it. We presented these 6 events and their individual reliabilities in the week 8 workshop.

 

The following week 9’s presentation required us to improve upon the individual reliability of our events, subsequently requiring us to construct what we failed to construct in the previous week. A pre-meeting was conducted where we discussed our failed attempt to get all action events in the previous week’s presentation, with a prevailing issue being time management, and thus decided on how to move forward. Specifically, we agreed each team member should allocate time to construct and test the remaining action events at least 2 days prior to our week 9 workshop. This deadline was put in place so that if someone had still not completed their event, they could ask for assistance in completing their event. Furthermore, we discussed each component we had already completed and how to improve reliability, with members suggesting changes in materials and alternative approaches to increase successful attempts. In order to improve time management each member decided and set aside 2 hours on a certain day to complete their assignments.
high.result: By the week 9 workshop, we were able to present 10 out of the 12 action events and their reliabilities, with us improving the 6 previously constructed events and their reliabilities considerably. We organised a time on the weekend to meet up and work on the final two events together.

high.situation:
We had began to construct our individual sections of our team RGM project and had come to the realisation that not all parts were working as intended and were overall very inconsistent.

Task/ Action: In order to improve the design of our project, we began to run numerous test runs for each individual component to identify the reliability of each part and which sections needed redesigning to improve the consistency of the project.
high.result: Using the data collected from the test runs to determine the overall reliability, we were able to identify which sections needed to be analysed based off what sections had the lowest reliability percentage . Improvements were made to theses areas in order to increase the reliability of the overall project and increase the consistency of successful runs.

high.situation: Throughout the conceptual, preliminary and testing design phases of our RGM project, our team constantly needed to communicate changes and modifications in our disciplinary design and needed to ensure our RGM transitioned together as one cohesive multi-disciplinary final product. Our team needed a procedure and solution in ensuring the accurate sharing of information during the transition of events as well as reliably connecting each event to make the RGM seamless in production.
high.task_action: As a team, we utilised effective communication tools to gather data and information about each of our individual RGM designs. During each weekly meeting, we individually updated the rest of our team on our progression and modifications to each of our individual events, and connecting events between each team member had to discuss their methods of transition. We paired our group members in smaller teams of 2-3, where each team member collaborated with adjacent events to develop a creative and efficient transition; and a connecting clip of individual disciplinary events that had to be made. In addition, our team had several group discussions on the technical aspects of each disciplinary design where each member had an opportunity to lead and think about each individual event as one system.
high.result: The resulting end product of our RGM produced a seamless transition between each disciplinary design. We have also developed our communication skills to effectively filter out non-essential information and build rapport with each of our team members. Throughout this process, whilst we worked on our background of disciplines, we had to understand as a team, our collective disciplinary design as one connected interdisciplinary system.

high.situation: As an electrical engineering student, I was assigned to lead a team of ~20 engineers for a marble machine project. This project involved integrating various subsystems, including mechanical, electrical, and software components, to create a complex, automated marble run. Midway through the project, we encountered a significant conflict between the mechanical and electrical teams. The mechanical team proposed a design that required specific motor specifications, while the electrical team argued that these specifications would exceed our power budget and lead to potential failures in the control system.      TASK:   My task was to mediate between the two teams, negotiate a feasible solution, and ensure that the project stayed on track without compromising the integrity of either subsystem. The goal was to reach a consensus that would allow the project to proceed smoothly and meet our performance and safety standards.      ACTION:   I initiated a series of meetings with representatives from both teams to understand their concerns and requirements thoroughly. During these meetings, I emphasized the importance of open communication and active listening. I encouraged both sides to present their technical justifications and explore alternative solutions collaboratively. To facilitate the negotiation process, I:      GATHERED DATA: Collected detailed specifications, performance data, and potential failure points from both teams.      PROPOSED COMPROMISES: Suggested possible adjustments to both the mechanical design and the electrical specifications, aiming for a balance that would satisfy both teams.      SET PRIORITIES: Helped the teams prioritize critical project requirements, such as power efficiency, performance, and safety, to guide the decision-making process.      MEDIATED DISCUSSIONS: Acted as a neutral mediator to ensure that discussions remained focused and productive, steering the conversation towards finding a mutually acceptable solution.
high.result: Through persistent negotiation and willingness to compromise, we arrived at a solution that involved modifying the motor specifications slightly and optimizing the control algorithms to stay within our power budget. Both teams felt their concerns were addressed, and the final design met all project requirements. This resolution not only maintained the project's timeline but also fostered a collaborative spirit among the team members. The successful integration of the subsystems led to the completion of a fully functional and impressive marble machine, which was well-received during our engineering expo.      This experience taught me valuable lessons in conflict resolution, the importance of compromise, and the effectiveness of clear communication in a technical team setting.

high.situation: Balancing full-time university studies and work commitments has been a significant challenge. As a third-year engineering student, managing time and energy between these responsibilities is critical. Specifically, in the context of my electrical classes, as those are the ones I have struggled with the most. This semester for one of my electrical units, we had a project focused on system engineering, modelling, and interfacing, where we had to create an automated traffic light system using LEDs, buttons, and integrate everything with coding, which is still a skill I am still developing. With another electrical unit happening at the same time, being able to invest as much as I can, whilst balancing other commitments has been a big struggle this semester, but, it has developed my ability for critical thinking to help me maximise the time I have to not only complete all my tasks and assignments, but complete them well.
high.task_action: The task was to design, code, and implement a functional traffic light system. This involved several steps: planning the project, designing the system, writing the code, and testing the hardware and software integration. Each step required not only technical skills but also time management and perseverance. It also required me to be critical from the beginning; how do I plan out my time, my project, to prevent significant issues later on and have to spend hours floating through the endless space of de-bugging 400 lines of code when I struggle with 10. To tackle this project, I started by thoroughly planning each phase. I mapped out the code and designed the hardware setup before diving into implementation. I utilised various resources, including online tutorials, class notes, and my fellow students to understand the basics of coding for hardware systems and start to assemble my major project; my heaviest task yet most rewarding. Throughout the project, I maintained a disciplined schedule, allocating specific times for coding and testing while ensuring my work and commitments to other classes were met. I set smaller goals within the project, such as getting the LEDs to work with the buttons first, before moving on to implementing a state machine, then making sure the code follows, then making improvements, such as including a LED display that updates and shows what is happening.
high.result: Completing the project was definitely not easy, but it was incredibly rewarding. After 6 weeks, I was able to create a working traffic light system that responded to button presses, but was unable to incorporate the LED display. Although I was unable to get everything perfect, I gave everything a shot, and tried my best to get it working. In the end, when trying to configure the LED display, it started to cause issues for the other code I had previously written, so I kept trying until it was time to submit. The process of integrating coding with hardware taught me valuable lessons in system thinking and problem-solving. I learned the importance of planning and breaking down tasks into manageable parts. Additionally, I developed better time management skills, which help me in my day to day life, outside of just uni and work.

high.situation:
As a member of the ENGG1050 unit, I understand how heavily we rely on group work. During the first week of this semester, each of us was placed in a different group and given the assignment of constructing a Rube Goldberg machine by the end of the current semester. The task is challenging, but I'm excited to be a part of this project.

Our goal is to build a functioning RGM within 13 weeks while keeping the client requirements in mind. Each member of the group has to make two modules of the machine, and at the end, all these 10 sets of modules will create a simple task in a complicated way.

TASK: My role in this project is crucial. As every member has to make two modules for the machine, I need to ensure that my modules coordinate with the other modules properly. Besides that, throughout the process, we have to present our weekly updates in our cohort and circulate the minutes and agenda among our team members.

Every week, one of the team members is assigned to make a presentation regarding the progress of the project, and every week, one team member is assigned to take the minutes of the meeting and create an agenda for the next week. I was assigned multiple times for taking the minutes and to be a group chair as well as creating my own two modules for this machine.

ACTION: During the weekly presentation, I would provide the necessary details regarding the progress of the project, including the current status of our tasks and any potential obstacles we may encounter. As for taking minutes, I made sure to capture all essential details discussed in the meeting and create a comprehensive agenda for the next week, ensuring that everyone was on the same page.
high.result:
Regarding the modules, I carefully designed and constructed my two modules, ensuring that they fit in well with the other modules. I coordinated with my team members to make sure that my modules would work seamlessly with theirs.

So far, our whole team has been able to cover every week's task. As for my personal contribution to the project, I've successfully designed my two modules, ensuring that they fit in well with the other modules, and I've created and circulated the minutes during my role as a minute taker sucessfully when assigned

REFLECTION: I understand that accountability is a huge part of this whole process of building the project. Students are not making an individual project, rather they are assigned to build this project with other team members. If I were to neglect my assigned role, the whole group activity and flow of work would fall into chaos. There are so many "ifs" related to my responsibility in this project, and the whole success of the machine somewhat depends on my module. So, I'm committed to doing my best to contribute to the team's success.

Reflecting on my experience so far, I'm happy with my personal growth and sense of responsibility in this project. I believe that this project has taught me the importance of teamwork, accountability, and communication skills, which will be valuable skills for my future endeavors.

high.situation: We needed to organize the development of the RGM ideas in such a way that it would be easy to analyze and elaborate off of.
high.task_action: Undergoing 3 organized and calculated steps in the development of the RGM. The first of which was the conceptual or "brainstorming" phase, which involved a brainstorming scaffold provided by Miro in which we simply created sections for ideas. For example; a section for the main goal, a section for filler events and so on and so forth. The next phase was the elementary design phase, where a main idea goal for the RGM was decided on and was then elaborated on by introducing methods in order to achieve this; for example our idea was putting a berocca in a cup of water, so how are we going to fill the cup up or get the berocca out of the tube. Through this phase any excessively complex ideas were eliminated to keep the project achievable. The last phase was the detailed design phase, which involved fine tuning the decided ideas; for example dimensions and exact materials to be used. This step has yet to be completed but a discussion arose where a requirement to make some of these ideas plausible was to 3d print some parts which will be one of my main goals this weekend in order to absolutely confirm some ideas will work.
high.result: The result was a structured and organised design process in which we were able to view current ideas and improve on them to create one final design.

high.situation: During this whole unit time and resource management have been very important and something that is more required than other units. While during the RGM project data driven decision making has been very prevalent due to the nature of calculating the reliability, something that was done for almost 3 weeks.
high.task_action: The task of time management has been occurring ever since week one, in terms of minutes, agenda, reflection, presentations, meeting and the RGM machine. This is because every week each one of these items is due. Compared to other units where the tasks may include just having a weekly quiz with some workshop questions. Of course you don’t have to manage each responsibility every week as this is a group based unit. But many tasks require the group's involvement such as the reflection. Every week someone new writes up the collective thoughts of the group in a presentable manner requiring time management. This all is expected out of a unit though but to incorporate time for a machine that should be worked upon each week leads to a further development of this skill. As recommended by the week 8 lectures and how this unit is structured, data driven decisions were incredibly important for an effective use of our time and resources. This was done by the data collected based on the machine's reliability. For example my original segment had a reliability of 1/163 due to it being based on stacking cards and dominos, whereas it would take a huge amount of attempts at stacking cards to even get to the domino's part. This compared to my other segment having a reliability of 4/8 presented a clear allocation of time and resources needed to help the first segment before the second. This did help in the end as to spend hours upon hours of just stacking cards and dominos was not efficient in any sense. Although an easy decision, it was a decision made based on data, we did a similar structure of allocation of time for all the members where we would work on anything that had less than 30 percent reliability.
high.result: The result of all this has been very positive as many segments have improved in terms of the reliability and consistency of its function. Also my time management skills have drastically improved due to all the weekly responsibilities. We have missed one submission of a weekly agenda, minutes and reflection because someone in our group forgot to submit it, but other than that my own skills of time management of this unit with my other 3 and how I’m allocating resources has improved. As a result I have been able to provide better work then what I normally would have because I’ve had more time to work on it.

high.situation: The goal of building a Rube Goldberg machine to fill a cup with water was the focus of our ENGG1050 course project. It needed careful preparation and execution. About halfway through the project's duration, a big obstacle presented itself to our crew. Our first design's intricacy was proving to be a barrier, endangering not only the machine's dependability but also our chance to meet the deadline. This insight made it imperative to reevaluate and modify the plan.
high.task_action: In light of the circumstances, our team decided to convene a number of brainstorming meetings with the goal of streamlining our design without sacrificing the innovative spirit of the project. We understood that each member brought distinct perspectives and abilities to the table, and that it was critical to capitalize on this diversity in order to execute a successful pivot. As part of the action plan, each team member had to critically assess the parts of the machine that they had been allocated, with the aim of determining where possible simplifications may be made. The creation of a buddy system, which pairs team members to offer criticism and ideas for improvement on each other's work, was implemented to support this effort. We also decided on an updated project schedule with checkpoints for finishing the testing and redesign stages, which would allow for plenty of iteration time.
high.result: We've had success thus far with streamlining our design and introducing a more team-oriented approach to problem-solving. In addition to being more useful, our updated Rube Goldberg machine blueprint maintains the intricacy and ingenuity required to achieve the project's learning goals. Individual component functioning and dependability have increased, according to preliminary testing, which has raised team morale and confidence in our capacity to finish the project on schedule. Even though the project is still in its early stages, our team has made great strides toward overcoming the initial obstacles. The importance of cooperation, flexibility, and effective communication in engineering projects has been reaffirmed by this experience. As we proceed with our efforts towards the final demonstration, we are devoted to preserving a strong and encouraging team.

TASK: Throughout Session One, I have been given around two weeks to complete each of my assignment. ENGG2050 consisted of two case studies and an infographic. I have set a goal for this year, which was to at least have a look at the assignment notification when they get released, and hopefully get started on it a few days later. I realised that I was not focusing on my studies and not making them as my top priority. I always gave myself less than a week to complete my assignments. Although, I knew what my outcome was from this decision from last year, I ended up falling into the same old trap. high.result: As a result, I was not satisfied with my results because I expected and wanted to receive a better mark than what I got. Although, I did not get the marks that I wanted, I have learnt a very important lesson on adaptability. Since, I did not adapt back into prioritising and focusing on my study after the breaks, it has resulted in me feeling worried and concerned about my future and that I might fail the unit.

high.situation: The soft skill I want to reflect on is prioritisation and focus, especially in the areas of time and workload management. The utilisation of this skill was especially important to my situation due the pressures from various engagements and duties I would be part of in the semester. The tasks and activities that would be dividing my time, energy and mental faculty includes numerous formal engagements such as my engineering degree, casual work and volunteering and leading at church. Additionally, numerous informal or personal interests and activities would further require time and energy. These included personal hobbies, exercise and physical activity, and attempting to maintain some semblance of a social life. To heighten the complexity of the matter, each facet requiring time and attention has various sub factors that further impose demands and add complexity. Examples of this are such things as commuting to university and late-night finishes at my job. Having barely survived two semesters, which imposed similar demands, I realised that moving forward I must optimise my work efficiency and more effectively divide my time and attention. Hence, I realised the essentially of being able to effectively prioritise tasks and manage focus in dealing with my current situation.             TASK:      In attempting to maximise my work efficiency and effectively manage my time and mental resources, I had to successfully meet the requirements posed by each facet of life. In the area of my university degree, I had numerous classes that required in person attendance. Associated with this demand came travel time, which, due to living on the Central Coast, meant two and half to three hours of commuting per day for in-person attendance. Furthermore, I imposed additional goals on myself aiming for 100% or at least an HD in all academic endeavours – which dictates the amount of time spent on a task. In previous semesters this imposed goal has typically resulted in an average of spending 50 to 60 hours per week on university work (travel time is included in this). Furthermore, for my job, I am expected to work 10 to 15 hours a week, mostly nighttime work. This is conducive to university work by day but extends the working hours of each day significantly. In terms of church volunteering and leading, roughly six hours is required to satisfactorily fulfill this role, further adding to my workload Additional to the aforementioned are numerous personal hobbies, exercise and socialising. These have no set requirement but rather act as filler activities. Consequently, the main goal of applying prioritisation and focus skills to my life, is to manage and balance these different facets in such a manner that my performance goals are met and my mental resources are preserved.                    ACTION:      To deal with these immense challenges, I employed numerous prioritisation and focus strategies. My first step was to analyse the most effective way to conduct university work and then mould all others facets around this structure. Considering my case, I determined that by maximising the amount of time I spent at home vs university, I would be able to maximise my output in terms of assignments and study. This goal had to be balanced by the mandatory attendance of certain classes. Hence, in selecting my timetable at the start of the semester, I aimed to group mandatory attendance classes (e.g. Labs) on the same day and separate non-mandatory classes to different days (e.g. lectures). In aiming to maximise time spent at home, I also chose to not attend classes I later found to be non-compulsory (such as the ENGG 2050 SGTA). In terms of task execution, I chose to subjectively rank tasks and assignments based on their due date and the weighting of each task. Hence, I coordinated my focus accordingly, completing higher ranked task first. In terms of the different facets, I aimed to prioritise university, my job and church above my personal hobbies and things such as exercise and socialisation. To keep track of this I created a weekly planner with university, my job and church taking main stage. In terms of focus, I actively sought to compartmentalise the various facets of my life. Whenever I was undertaking one activity I would focus on “living in the moment” or being fully focused on that activity whilst not allowing myself to ponder on or get stressed by the various other facets. I also determined that my ability to focus has a direct correlation to the performance of the task at hand. As such I utilised numerous strategies to enhance my focus and therefore maximise my output. Such things included the use of study music, creation of quiet places to study and the elimination of distractions in said study places (such as pets, mobile phone, other family members, TV).
high.result: Numerous positive results were obtained through the application of prioritisation and focus. Some negative results were also obtained, which I consider to be indirectly positive results. Negative results offer an unbridled opportunity for growth and as such I do not believe them to be negative in any manner at all. Through careful timetable planning and skipping non-compulsory classes, I was able to minimise the time spent and university and commuting whilst maximising time at home. This improved my work output and allowed me to complete assignments – at least initially – to a level I was satisfied with. It also allowed me to review work at a faster rate than otherwise possible through methods such as watching lectures at a faster speed. As such I made good progress in the first half of the semester and even had some time to continue pursuing hobbies, exercise, and social life – which I was very pleased with. It also ensured that I did not generally feel too stressed with the demands placed on me by university, my job and volunteering and leading. I was further very satisfied with the performance of the weekly planner in keeping me accountable and up to date. It greatly aided me in effectively prioritising the execution of certain activities and focusing on one task at a time. The process of subjectively weighting and executing tasks also enable me to complete tasks to a standard that I deemed appropriate and indeed I was proud of my progress. However, despite all these steps, the first half of the semester still took a toll on my energy levels and motivation – even though I did not consciously realise it. During the mid semester break I led on a youth camp and had very little time to rest. Coming back to the second half I felt very depleted. As a result, my ability to concentrate and work efficiently were severely depleted. Consequently, the strategy of prioritising tasks often caused me to value lectures and SGTA work below assessments. I thus fell behind with content and required extra time, energy and motivation to catch up. A great example was quiz 6, where I needed to study a great deal extra for the quiz, due to being behind in the unit. Having little time and very little motivation, I effectively abandoned all pursuit of hobbies, exercise, and socialisation. Having lost this avenue of rest and recharge I felt my energy and motivation decreasing at a faster rate. This led me to abandon the use of the weekly planner which eliminated the voice of accountability and made it even harder to prioritise tasks and activities in my depleted state. As such I proceeded through much of the second semester in what I call “a zombie state” – simply trying to get things done in time and show up where I needed to be. Despite the lacklustre second half of the semester, the process of compartmentalising different facets was still greatly helpful. It reduced my overall feeling of stress, allowed me to take things as they come and aided me in slightly enjoying the task at hand.

high.situation: In a company I work for, there are issues with employee diversity and inclusion. There is a lack of people with different backgrounds and perspectives in the team, which affects the diversity of creativity and decision-making. At the same time, employees question the fairness of management, believing that there are unequal opportunities for promotion and that certain groups are marginalised.      TASKS: As a team leader, I was given the task of improving the diversity and inclusion of the team and ensuring that all employees felt fair and respected. I need to develop and implement a set of strategies to address these issues and promote positive change in the company culture.      ACTIONS: Firstly, I conducted an internal survey and employee interviews to understand specific issues and how employees felt. Second, based on the results of the survey, I worked with HR to develop a detailed diversity and inclusion policy. The policy includes equal opportunities in the recruitment process, anti-discrimination measures, and a diversity training programme. And I arranged diversity and inclusion training, inviting external experts to come in and talk about it, to raise employees' awareness of diversity and inclusion. The training covers unconscious bias, multicultural awareness and inclusive leadership. Finally, I worked with management to develop and make public transparent promotion criteria and processes to ensure that all employees are aware of promotion requirements and opportunities.
high.result: * Through the survey, I found that employee satisfaction with the company's culture increased significantly. In particular, ratings on fairness and inclusiveness improved significantly.   	* Within six months of implementing the policy, 35% of our new hires came from diverse backgrounds, and the diversity of the team improved significantly.   	* As a result of the diversity of backgrounds and perspectives of our team members, the quality of collective creativity and decision-making has improved, leading to the success of several important projects and an increase in the team's ability to innovate.   	* The establishment of Employee Resource Groups has enhanced the sense of belonging and engagement among employees, and employee turnover has decreased, resulting in a more stable and cohesive team.

high.situation: In a company I manage, we plan to enter a new global market. We needed to conduct an in-depth analysis of global market trends and economic forces to ensure that our entry strategy would be successful.
high.task_action: As the project leader, I began by assembling a cross-functional team that included market analysts, economists, and sales specialists. We identified key market trends that needed to be analysed, such as changes in consumer behaviour, competitive dynamics, economic growth forecasts and regulatory policies. Subsequently, we conducted market research and collected a large amount of quantitative and qualitative data. I organised regular team meetings to ensure that everyone was clear on their tasks and able to share the latest research findings.      To gain a more comprehensive understanding of the market, we also engaged local consultants and partners to help us understand the economic forces and cultural differences in a given region. I led the team in a SWOT analysis (i.e. Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities and Threats analysis) and developed a series of scenario plans for entry strategies.
high.result: As a result of these efforts, we were successful in developing detailed market trend maps and economic force maps. These charts helped us identify potential opportunities and risks, and developed a detailed market entry plan for us. Ultimately, we selected an optimal market entry strategy and successfully entered the new market within six months. We achieved 120 per cent of our projected sales target and established solid brand recognition in the new market.

high.situation: During my third year in the mechanical engineering program, our team was tasked with designing a new gearbox using CREO. The project required us to design the gearbox and all the components using CREO, present all the relevant calculations to all the class in regards to the components, manufacture the gearbox and test its functionality. This project was part of our course in the unit mechanical design, and it involved applying both theoretical knowledge and practical skills. Our team consisted of three members, each with different strengths.      TASK: As the project leader, my primary responsibility was to ensure that our team collaborated effectively and met all project deadlines. This involved coordinating the efforts of each team member, managing resources, and addressing any conflicts or challenges that arose during the project. Additionally, I needed to ensure that our design met the specified technical requirements and adhered to safety standards.      ACTION: To start things off we organized a meeting where we discussed the project requirements and divided the tasks based on each member's strengths. This task consisted in relation to the shafts, gears and casing. Having said that, I created a detailed project timeline with key milestones and deadlines. Each member was assigned specific responsibilities, such as CAD modeling, stress analysis, material selection, and manufacturing planning according to the selected component. I scheduled regular meetings to review progress, discuss any issues, and reassign tasks if necessary since one of our team member had been operated recently. These meetings helped keep everyone accountable and allowed us to address problems. I also propose the idea to upload all the files in a google drive such as excel files, CREO design, manufacturing drawings and reports so that all the team members could edit and have access to all the material and be aware of the process.       When disagreements arose, I heled meetings of all team member's and listen to their  perspective and find a compromise. For instance, there was a debate about the gear ratio to use for optimal performance. I encouraged the team to conduct a comparative analysis of different gear ratios and make a data-driven decision. Apart from that there was a disagreement in regards to a team member that could not do his part in the time frame proposed. To solve this issue me and the other student his part and another part was assigned to the person that was absent. For last, I made sure to recognize and celebrate small victories to keep the team motivated. I also provided support when team members faced difficulties, whether technical or personal. For example, the team member that had been operated recently could not model his part in CREO since his hand was immobilized consequently I reach out to him and helped him with his part.
high.result: As a result of these actions, our team successfully completed the gearbox design project. Our design calculations and CREO models were thorough and accurate, and we successfully manufacture the gearbox . The gearbox was tested and performed efficiently, meeting all the technical specifications and safety standards. Our team received positive feedback from our professors and classmates. But the most important thing was that we were able to identify were and at what weight the gearbox would fail  which was at the second shaft in the grove and if the gear box lifted more than 17kg.

high.situation: In my previous unit, ENGG2000/3000, students were assigned to a particular group within a team depending on their specialization (three groups: Structures, Motions, and Communications). As a mechanical student, I was placed in the ‘Structures’ group with four other civil/mechanical students. In my structures group, there were two students in their third year, and three second-year students (including me). For each of the three groups in a team, the third-year students were assigned as leaders of their group. As a result, we had two leaders for our group who were responsible for allocating roles and responsibilities out to the team. They were also responsible for preparing an agenda for each week’s SGTA and any meetings outside of class times. They also kept in regular contact with the tutors and unit convenors and importantly had constant contact with the other group leaders to ensure all groups were staying on track and sticking to deadlines. However, about week 5 our third-year students had to leave temporarily from university. One leader needed an important surgery and had to stay at the hospital for several weeks. The other leader had to go emergency trip overseas to see his family. As a result, we had no group leader so I filled the role of group leader and its associated responsibilities.
high.task_action: As a temporary group leader, I had to initially approach the other two group leaders (Communications and Motions) and explain the situation to them about the two third years who had to leave temporarily. I explained that they should direct all their messages to me and add me to the leaders’ group chat on both WhatsApp and Discord. In the weeks that followed I would send regular weekly update requests to ensure our team was meeting deadlines and staying on track with the schedule. I would at least once a week contact our group members individually and ask how they were doing their sections and if they needed any help or support. Also, as our group was down by two, me and the rest of the group had to handle more tasks to compensate for the reduced manpower. I had to prepare the team's agenda an extra week or two ahead to ensure we had sufficient time to complete the tasks. Further, I requested our group to conduct meetings (10-15 minutes briefly on Zoom 2 days after the SGTA) to discuss problems we might be having and get on top of it as soon as possible. Otherwise, members with issues would have to wait until the next SGTA to get proper assistance.      Another aspect of being a team leader meant that I had to learn about everyone’s strengths and weaknesses. This was done by constantly asking questions to members about how they would do a certain task (their suggested approach would highlight their level of understanding and demonstrate if they would struggle with that task and might need assistance) or had a preference (this would signal strength). Being an effective communicator was very important in being able to organize tasks and assign them to members with minimal hiccups, streamlining the process of the project. Additionally, I would send weekly summarized text to the on-leave third-year members of the tasks done that week and the agenda for next week. This would not only keep them posted but also allow them to remind me of tasks that I may have neglected.
high.result: By the time the third-year students returned to class, we had almost completed the marble machine. They were updated about the progress the team had made and what was remaining on the to-do list. The marble cube was built but the testing needed to be conducted and documented as well as some aesthetic additions to the cube. My time as group leader was very important in our progress. My leadership kept us on track, and we were able to successfully finish the entire cube promptly. If I had not filled in for the group leader, there was a considerable chance of our team not completing the cube in time. My effective communication with the other group leaders allowed for a smooth transition without many disruptions.

Notably, my involvement as the founder of the latest engineering student project, MQ Rover, our university's long-running CubeSat team, MQ Orbital, as well as a variety of other initiatives have all exposed me to the interconnectedness of the space sector on a global scale.
high.task_action: In these roles, I've had the opportunity to engage in the global space industry and foster connections that I intend to leverage in the future. For instance, with MQ Rover, our focus on creating a lunar-styled rover to compete in the Australian Rover Challenge has allowed me to understand the industry on a global scale. Through collaboration with peers, industry professionals, and international partners, I've gained insights into the challenges and opportunities of the global space market.      Furthermore, my involvement in the Astra white paper provided an opportunity to research how the global space market is addressing global Earth-based challenges using space technologies.
