mid.situation: We were asked to build a gearbox for Mechanical Design 2 which involved a simple casing, bearings, shafts, and gears. The idea is that the gearbox will be used as part of a crane to lift a maximum mass of 28kg at a certain rate.
mid.task_action: We were a group of three with each team member being assigned a certain component of the gearbox. The customer had multiple factors that we had to consider. This included the cost, the mass, the volume, the efficiency, and lastly the aesthetics. With all these factors in mind, it is hard to understand where to start. Moreover, it becomes easy to fall into the trap of tinkering or trial and error without a systematic process to track improvement or compromises. Therefore, to initiate an engineering process, we initially started off with brainstorming all the ideas without any consideration of the constraints provided by the customer. Next, with the bank of ideas, we could eliminate the ones with the most apparent problems or the ones that clearly would not meet the customer considerations and this put our critical reasoning into play. Next, we worked on a top-down design starting with the result and working our way back to the smallest of components. Not only that, but we also used spreadsheets to go through multiple iterations of our design and analyse how the final product would be affected.      Testing procedures were also crucial to understand if our product would get better through the iterations and this would involve setting performance indicators, be it qualitative or quantitative. These procedures also allowed us to understand the compromises that we need to make, and which components would be prioritized over the others.      And throughout all this, it was important to make sure that team members were sticking to these procedures instead of deviating to the easier methods of just tinkering which would produce a subpar product.
mid.result: We were able to successfully create an order list for the gearbox for assembly and start the more in-depth testing procedures.

mid.situation: During my third year of university, our engineering design course required us to complete a semester-long team project. The project involved developing a prototype for a new product, but our team initially struggled with coordination and direction, leading to confusion about tasks and responsibilities.      Task:      As the team leader, my task was to organize the group, clarify roles, and establish a clear plan to ensure we completed the project on time and to a high standard. This included improving communication, resolving conflicts, and keeping the team motivated and focused.      Action:      To address these issues, I first organized a team meeting to reassess our approach. During this meeting, I encouraged each member to share their strengths and preferences regarding different aspects of the project. Based on this input, I reallocated tasks to align better with each member’s skills and interests. I then created a detailed project plan with specific goals and milestones, using project management software to outline our timeline and sharing this with the team.      To ensure consistent and effective communication, I set up a group chat on a messaging app and organized a shared online workspace for all project documents. I also scheduled regular in-person meetings every week to discuss progress, address any issues, and adjust plans as needed. When disagreements arose about the design and functionality of the prototype, I facilitated discussions to ensure all viewpoints were considered, encouraging compromise and focusing on our common goals.
mid.result: The result of these actions was a significant improvement in our team's productivity and collaboration. Clear role assignments and a structured timeline helped everyone stay on track. Enhanced communication ensured that all team members were informed and engaged throughout the project. Conflict resolution strategies helped maintain a positive team dynamic, allowing us to work more effectively together. Ultimately, we completed our prototype ahead of schedule and received commendations from our professor for both the quality of our work and our demonstration of strong team leadership and project management skills.

mid.situation: In my COMP3310 Secure Application Development course, our group project involved developing a secure messaging application. The project's requirements included implementing robust security features like end-to-end encryption, secure user authentication, and data protection. Balancing these stringent security needs with maintaining a smooth user experience and efficient performance, all within a tight deadline, posed a significant challenge for our team.      TASK:      As the project lead, my primary task was to guide the team in making strategic trade-offs, exercising sound judgment, and finding a balance between security, usability, and performance. This entailed coordinating our efforts, prioritizing features, and ensuring adherence to secure coding best practices while meeting our project deadlines.      ACTION:      I began by organizing an initial meeting to gather and prioritize project requirements and conducted a risk assessment to identify critical security features. Responsibilities were delegated based on team members' strengths: cryptography to one member, security protocols to another, and user interface development to a third. I managed the integration of components and testing. We balanced security and usability through regular discussions, opting for a highly secure encryption algorithm optimized for performance. We simplified user authentication with an easy-to-use two-factor authentication system and employed an iterative development process with frequent testing and user feedback sessions to refine our approach.
mid.result: The project culminated in the successful delivery of a secure messaging application that met all specified security requirements on time. The application was praised for its robust security measures without significant performance trade-offs and received positive feedback for its intuitive user interface. This experience honed our ability to make critical trade-offs and balance competing priorities, emphasizing the importance of judgment and communication in resolving complex challenges.

mid.situation: We had to build and construct a high-precision DC motor drive system for lift control as part of our ELCT3024 team project. Acquiring accurate speed control was necessary to guarantee seamless functioning and adjust to different load scenarios, both of which are essential for the dependability and efficiency of lift systems. The project was difficult because it required very precise motor control to strike a balance between passenger comfort and operational efficiency.      TASK:      Specifically, we had to design a DC motor control system with closed-loop control mechanisms to achieve accurate speed regulation. To make sure the system satisfied the needs for high accuracy, comfort, and dependability, we had to develop and test it. Elevator applications often include dynamic situations that the control system has to be able to handle.      ACTION:      	*   INITIAL RESEARCH AND DESIGN: We started by researching the necessary components and control strategies. The Pololu 4824 brushed DC motor with an integrated gearbox was selected for its balance of torque, speed, and efficiency. We designed a schematic diagram for the DC motor drive system, incorporating a motor driver controlled by an Arduino UNO, which received feedback from the motor’s speed and position sensors.      	*   MATHEMATICAL MODELLING: We developed the mathematical models to describe the motor’s behaviour, including torque and back EMF equations. We derived the open-loop transfer function and used MATLAB to perform root locus and frequency domain analyses (Bode plots) to understand the system dynamics.      	*   CONTROLLER DESIGN: The primary challenge was designing a PI controller to meet the specified performance parameters: a settling time of 0.3 seconds ± 20%, overshoot less than 10%, and steady-state error less than 1%. We used root locus techniques and Bode plots to iteratively adjust the controller parameters. We also utilized Simulink’s PI block auto-tuner to refine these parameters.      	*   SIMULATION AND TESTING: We built both open-loop and closed-loop systems in Simulink to verify our design. The closed-loop system incorporated the PI controller with the calculated values (Ki = 0.03194 and Kp = 0.00192). We tested the system under various conditions, including changes in load, to ensure stability and performance.      	*   EXPERIMENTAL SETUP: We assembled the hardware, connecting the DRV8871 driver chip and Pololu 4824 motor to the Arduino UNO. We conducted multiple experiments to validate the system’s performance, adjusting the control parameters as needed based on the experimental results.
mid.result: Our efforts culminated in a high-precision DC motor drive system that met the project’s requirements. The system demonstrated minimal steady-state error (

mid.situation: In the second semester of 1st year I was part of the formation of an engineering friend group, we formed the group out of a connection of engineering as well as to have a support group to study with and learn with. At the beginning of this year, I had to understand the importance of thinking for myself specifically in a group setting as teamwork was important but that should not be the main method of problem-solving.
mid.task_action: What we as a group would do is work on our assignments together and bounce off the solutions of each other, this made sure that our ideas never got too rigid and the way of finding the solutions to problems was diversified. This way of learning works well enough as we test each other but I realized that it should not be our way of learning. There is a danger in using only this method of learning and storing information, as it makes the student complicit and doesn't prompt self-learning. It then makes the student reliant on others inputting answers and solutions which makes the individual lacking in training of the mind. The solution to this problem was to first do the problem and assignment on your own and then afterward come together to discuss each other's solutions. This only works with a degree such as engineering as it deals almost exclusively with mathematical problems and there is usually only one correct answer to the said problem. Emphasizing self-learning as well as group-style learning and thinking is equally important and to be done in equal measure. In our group, we have started to realize that the work that is done by one individual gets reused and there is no self-thought and reflection concerning the answer to the assignment of the problem. This also calls for a balance in group effort and attitude toward learning before coming together and discussing the answer.
mid.result: The result is still in progress but can be seen at the same time as the improvement in the group come to understand this balance of attitude towards work, team effort in learning and thought. The realization of this comes slowly but there are tangible improvements in attitudes, thoughts, and learning within this community. It also comes with the benefit of improving as an engineer in all aspects of study and work, which is the ultimate goal for engineers in the workplace.

mid.situation: During one of my weekly tasks in the tutorial, we were assigned to learn about intellectual property (IP) and patent law related to your own specialisation. For me this was software engineering. This task was crucial to understanding how to protect our future software innovations.      TASK: The task was to gain a basic understanding of intellectual property rights, specifically focusing on patent laws as they apply to software engineering. This included researching how patents and IP protection works and how it is applied to specific software products and system ideas.      ACTION:      	* INITIAL RESEARCH: I began by reading introductory materials on intellectual property rights, focusing on the differences between copyrights, trademarks, and patents.   	* APPLICATION TO SOFTWARE: I explored how patent laws specifically apply to software engineering by looking at examples of patented software products and system ideas.
mid.result: By the end of the week, I had gained a basic but solid understanding of intellectual property rights and patent laws as they apply to software engineering.

mid.situation: In another of my units, we had to design a motor using an arduino and driver. For example, if I was better at solving equations to find a specific variable that we'd need, I would take the initiative and lead the group for that specific task. mid.result: The result of our efforts to have diverse leadership in our group was an efficient completion of our project. The unique skills each of our members possessed aided in every aspect, and our project report reflected this. Knowing how to implement an equity and diversity driven leadership structure will be important moving forward, as it will encourage a wide range of talent and experience and increase workplace productivity.

mid.situation: In our journey to enhance our professional skills, my team faced a significant challenge regarding our attitudes, thoughts, and learning processes. We discovered that our initial approach to problem-solving and collaboration was not as effective as it could be. This realisation came to light during a critical project where we were tasked with developing a marble machine. Our attitudes towards the task, preconceived notions about each other's capabilities, and the methods we employed for learning and development all needed reassessment.
mid.task_action: To address these underlying issues, we adopted a systematic approach to reevaluate and improve our attitudes, thought processes, and learning strategies. The first step was to acknowledge the existing problems and define the core question: "How can we cultivate a positive mindset, enhance our critical thinking, and adopt effective learning techniques to improve our project outcomes?" We started with a team meeting where everyone shared their thoughts and experiences regarding the project and our working dynamics. Using a combination of reflective practices and feedback sessions, we encouraged each team member to voice their perspectives. We employed the Six Thinking Hats method to explore various angles of our attitudes and thoughts towards the project. This encouraged each member to think critically and creatively, considering different viewpoints and potential impacts. Every idea and observation was documented for further discussion. Post-analysis, we focused on implementing changes. An essential component of this process was taking log booking seriously. By consistently recording our activities, ideas, and reflections, we created a valuable resource that allowed us to track our progress, identify patterns, and learn from our experiences systematically. Furthermore, we adopted a more structured approach to learning by setting clear goals, seeking feedback from tutors, and reflecting on our progress each week.
mid.result: The outcome of these efforts was transformative for my team. We developed a more positive and open-minded attitude towards challenges, which significantly boosted our morale and productivity. Our critical thinking skills improved, enabling us to analyse problems more effectively and devise innovative solutions. The structured learning approach, along with log booking, led to better retention and application of knowledge, which was evident in the quality of our project outcomes. We also learned the value of fostering a collaborative environment where everyone feels heard and respected. This shift in attitude and thought process not only enhanced our project results but also contributed to our personal and professional growth. The practice of log booking was particularly beneficial as it provided a clear and detailed account of our journey, helping us to reflect accurately on our successes and areas for improvement. Moving forward, I am committed to maintaining this positive mindset, encouraging critical thinking, and adopting effective learning strategies to achieve my goals.

mid.situation: Startup companies such as Oxford Nanopore need to come up with technological advancements in their field of expertise to stay ahead of the market and develop new and more efficient technologies
mid.task_action: The importance of technological advancements is apparent, humans need better and more efficient technologies for both to ease human life and to improve it. There is a lot we don't know about DNA and other molecules inside our bodies and it is important to improve our technologies to be able to understand and when issues arise, such as getting sick, we can treat it. Oxford Nanopore Tech has been able to do such things as a startup company since it was founded in 2005. They have been advancing the technologies for DNA sequencing and reading to assist doctors and researchers in understanding and studying DNA more efficiently and effectively. Although their technologies are very impressive, with both their size, efficiency and effectiveness, it arises the question of what implications could such amazing tech have other than just its intended purpose, an ethical question and how you should deal with when this technology is used in an unintended purpose or important and private information is stolen, an important ethical dilemma of whether such fast and quick innovations in DNA technology is good or dangerous.
mid.result: It is important that, as Oxford Nanopore Tech has done, to make rules and regulations but also patents to protect their IP not only from other people and companies but to have ways to stop and prevent data or technology leaks. It might be impossible to stop the negative implications of such technologies completely but they can be minimised and have contingencies for such events. Oxford Nanopore Tech not only limits its technology to specific researchers and universities they have contracts with but keeps a close eye on their products and has not faced any issues since being founded.

mid.situation: As a mechanical engineering college student with a growing interest in Python and other programming languages, I was presented with a project involving the optimization of a propeller design using computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulations.
mid.task_action: Recognizing the potential of Python in streamlining engineering analyses, I took the initiative to leverage these tools for the propeller design optimization task. Firstly, I dedicated time to familiarize myself with Python libraries commonly used in engineering math, such as NumPy, SciPy, and Matplotlib. Additionally, I acquired knowledge on CFD principles and techniques through online resources and textbooks. With a solid foundation in Python and CFD. I identified key parameters to optimize, such as blade geometry and pitch distribution, to enhance propeller performance. Using Python, I developed scripts to preprocess the design parameters and interface with the CFD software for simulation setup and post-processing. I iteratively refined the propeller design based on the simulation results, as well as the parameters to come to an optimized solution that interfaced well with the hardware we chose.
mid.result: Through my utilization of Python and CFD, I successfully optimized the propeller design, achieving improved performance metrics such as thrust efficiency and cavitation resistance. The Python scripts facilitated efficient data processing and analysis, enabling me to explore a wide range of design configurations within a reasonable timeframe. Additionally, the integration of Python with CFD streamlined the optimization process, allowing for seamless iteration between design adjustments and simulation evaluations.

mid.situation: * CONTEXT: During my part-time job at Coles, I worked as a cashier during busy weekend shifts.   	* CHALLENGE: The checkout lines were often long, and customers were impatient due to the wait times.      	*   TASK:      	* OBJECTIVE: My task was to efficiently process customer transactions while maintaining a positive customer experience.      	* CHALLENGES:      	* Balancing speed with accuracy.   	* Handling frustrated customers.   	* Ensuring smooth flow at the checkout.      	*   ACTION:      	* SITUATION AND ADAPTATION:      	* On a particularly busy Saturday, the checkout lines extended down the aisles.   	* I quickly adjusted my mindset, knowing that maintaining a calm demeanor was crucial.      	* TASK AND EFFICIENCY:      	* I focused on scanning items accurately but swiftly.   	* When faced with a price discrepancy or an item without a barcode, I promptly called for assistance.   	* I communicated with customers, explaining any delays and thanking them for their patience.      	* CONFLICT RESOLUTION AND CUSTOMER SERVICE:      	* Some customers expressed frustration due to the wait.   	* I actively listened to their concerns, empathized, and apologized sincerely.   	* I offered solutions, such as opening additional registers or suggesting express lanes for small baskets.      	*
mid.result: * POSITIVE OUTCOMES:      	* Customers appreciated my efforts to expedite the process.   	* The checkout lines gradually decreased, and the flow improved.   	* I received positive feedback from both customers and my supervisor.      	* LESSONS LEARNED:      	* Effective communication and conflict resolution are essential in customer-facing roles.   	* Balancing speed and accuracy requires adaptability and a customer-centric mindset.

mid.task_action: Recognizing the complexity of the task and the necessity for effective team leadership, I initiated a collaborative effort. Firstly, I convened a team meeting where we collectively defined the scope and requirements of the project. Task allocation was crucial, and I ensured that each team member had a clear understanding of their responsibilities. Utilizing a participative approach, I encouraged open discussion and brainstorming sessions to explore various design possibilities. We conducted thorough research to understand the mechanics involved and reviewed past similar projects for insights.      Taking inspiration from the STAR framework, I facilitated a structured brainstorming session using techniques such as mind mapping and idea generation exercises. We discussed factors like torque requirements, gear ratios, material selection, and safety considerations. As a result, we generated multiple design concepts, each addressing different aspects of the problem.      Following the brainstorming session, we transitioned into the evaluation phase. Using criteria such as feasibility, cost-effectiveness, and performance, we objectively assessed each design concept. Through iterative discussions and simulations, we narrowed down the options to the most promising ones. I ensured that every team member had the opportunity to contribute their insights and opinions during the decision-making process.
mid.result: The outcome of our collaborative efforts was the selection of a robust gearbox design capable of meeting the project requirements. By leveraging the diverse expertise within the team and employing effective communication strategies, we achieved consensus on the final design direction. With clear roles and responsibilities established, we proceeded to allocate tasks for detailed design, prototyping, and testing phases. This approach not only fostered innovation but also enhanced team cohesion and motivation. Ultimately, our successful implementation of team leadership principles resulted in the development of a functional gearbox assembly that surpassed the weight lifting target of 28kg, showcasing the effectiveness of our collective efforts.

mid.situation: As an engineering student, I was part of a team tasked with designing and implementing a feeder mechanism for a marble machine project. The feeder mechanism's goal was to efficiently transport marbles collected at the bottom of the machine back to the top, where they would enter a series of pathways leading to a collection box.      TASK: In this project, my role as the team leader was to oversee the design, fabrication, and testing of the feeder mechanism. This involved coordinating the team's efforts, delegating tasks according to each member's skills, and ensuring the feeder mechanism integrated seamlessly with the rest of the marble machine.      ACTION: To begin, I facilitated a brainstorming session with the team to generate ideas for the feeder mechanism design. We considered factors such as efficiency, reliability, and ease of integration with the existing marble machine components. After evaluating several concepts, we settled on a design that utilized a conveyor belt system to transport marbles from the collection area to the top of the machine.      I assigned tasks to team members, such as designing the conveyor belt system, fabricating mechanical components, programming any automation features, and conducting testing and optimization. Throughout the design and fabrication process, I provided guidance and support, ensuring clear communication and collaboration among team members. I also coordinated with other teams working on different aspects of the marble machine to ensure compatibility and alignment of our respective components.      During testing, I led the team in identifying and addressing any issues or performance bottlenecks, iterating on the design as needed to optimize efficiency and reliability.
mid.result: Through effective teamwork and leadership, we successfully designed and implemented a feeder mechanism that efficiently transported marbles from the collection area to the top of the marble machine. The conveyor belt system operated smoothly, ensuring a continuous flow of marbles through the machine's pathways. During the project presentation, we demonstrated the feeder mechanism's functionality and explained its design principles to our peers and tutors. Our project received positive feedback for its innovation, technical complexity, and contribution to the overall functionality of the marble machine.

mid.situation: I have completed my degree in terms of classes. As part of the capstone unit for software engineering, I was tasked to choose a software engineering-related project to complete that aligns with my current skill set.
mid.task_action: As I expected, this session has been my final semester for classes in my degree. I only have the PACE unit left, in which I have 90 hours remaining. This semester consisted of 2 first-year units as well as ENGG3050 and COMP4060 (capstone unit for Software Engineering). I was stuck doing the first-year units (COMP1050, ENGG1000) as a result of a scheduling issue after switching degrees in between syllabus changes. In my capstone unit, COMP4060, I was tasked with completing a 6-week software engineering-related project of my own choosing. This was in place to add to my future portfolio and job prospects as I leave the degree. I chose to build a web application using Golang and NoSQL as it aligns with my own interests and skill set moving forward.
mid.result: Although I was not able to achieve my own set technical goals from the continual professional development project, I was able to achieve the majority of my own learning outcomes.

mid.situation: Vuluring my research on Vulcan Energy, I identified that the company was at a critical juncture. They faced increasing pressure to meet the rising demand for lithium, essential for electric vehicle batteries, while maintaining their commitment to zero-carbon lithium extraction. This dual challenge of scaling production sustainably was pivotal for their growth and market positioning.
mid.task_action: My task was to analyse Vulcan Energy’s existing processes and identify potential technological advancements that could enhance their lithium extraction efficiency and sustainability. I conducted an extensive review of current and emerging lithium extraction technologies. This involved analysing academic papers, industry reports, and case studies to understand the advantages and limitations of various methods. I benchmarked Vulcan Energy’s processes against industry best practices. This helped identify gaps and areas for improvement, particularly focusing on the efficiency and environmental impact of their current technology.
mid.result: The research and subsequent actions led to several significant outcome as the literature review and benchmarking exercise identified DLE using geothermal energy as the most promising technology for Vulcan Energy. This method showed potential for higher lithium yield and lower environmental impact. The stakeholder interviews provided critical insights that informed the feasibility analysis, ensuring that the proposed solution was both practical and aligned with industry trends.

mid.situation: During my internship at a 3D printing company, I worked on a team that developed a new product called Voron 2.4R2. Team members have varied cultural origins. They have different levels of skill, resulting in distinct working styles and communication patterns. These discrepancies resulted in communication breakdowns and misunderstandings. The discrepancy hampered the project's pace and presented hurdles that we needed to overcome to ensure its success.
mid.task_action: My goal was to make the most of our team's diversity. It is significant for me to increase collaboration and production. I was responsible for initiating and implementing strategies. These would improve communication and capitalize on varied viewpoints.      My goal was to strengthen interpersonal interactions and create trust among team members. The first step was to organize a cultural exchange session where we discussed information about our backgrounds, work habits, and communication preferences. Furthermore, I instituted a regular 'cultural moment' at the opening of each team meeting, in which one team member would contribute something distinctive about their culture or professional experience.      Second, I developed a structured communication protocol to solve communication barriers. This includes agendas and action items for meetings. There are specific times for open discussion and input. I also recommend the usage of collaboration tools like project management software. These steps were made to ensure transparency and easy access to information within the team.      Finally, I led courses on active listening and practical communication techniques. These sessions sought to provide team members with the skills required to better understand and collaborate.
mid.result: These actions improved the team's communication and collaboration. Trust and understanding were enhanced. The cultural exchange activies helped us to know each other's working sytle and values. The collaborative tools increased transparency and efficiency. Also it reduces misunderstandings. The workshops helped team members handle disagreements and slove problems. These efforts enabled us to execute the project on schedule while exceeding the specified quality requirements.

mid.situation: Needed to make a infographic for ENGG2050 explaining a key concept from the textbook we have learnt so far.
mid.task_action: Firstly, needing to read the textbook, and then afterwards understanding the topic in the textbook. Once the topic is understood, pick a key topic to make it into a bite size infographic. Analyse example infographic and see the characteristics that made it optimal to given criteria. Once understanding what made it optimal infographic plan out your infographic. Make a draft infographic and revise and modify until you have made the most optimal infographic.
mid.result: The result was being able to make an infographic that is designed well to the criteria and is able to portray a key concept simply to anyone that's reading it.

mid.situation: When learning the content for Quiz 6 I wanted to learn, practice, and take notes on the relevant content, but was struggling to fully understand some of the topics using only the course content.
mid.task_action: Following my viewing of content relevant to Quiz 6 within the ENGG2050 course, including lectures and the Blanchard Systems Engineering and Analysis textbook, I decided that I would have to view additional content and integrate it into my knowledge to enhance my understanding of the content. The additional content I viewed consisted primarily of YouTube videos explaining and walking through example problems, as well as websites demonstrating how you would apply relevant concepts (such as the Hurwicz Criterion, Minimin, Minimax, and Maximin decision making).
mid.result: The result was that I was able to take short effective notes on the content I was previously struggling to understand, as well as being able to complete practice questions (including those in the Quiz 6 Sample) which were previously more difficult to approach. The notes and practice I completed were additionally helpful during Quiz 6, as I was able to check them if I was unsure on any related questions.

mid.situation: During the weekly STGA class for ENGG2050 “Engineering systems and design thinking”, we were recommended to work in a group to brainstorm ideas and create a detail design plan along with the testing, maintenance for a tea maker. During the brainstorming, our group had come up with multiple ideas for the tea maker, however with the given budget could not implement all our ideas into the tea maker.      Task:      In that situation, all the team member from the group had decided to write down all the ideas from the brainstorming session in a shared google docs. Our goal was to note down all the possible ideas from the brainstorming session into a shared google docs so that we could trace back to the ideas later and to select the ideas that were most compatible with the tea maker’s requirements and budget.      Action:      After noting down all the ideas, the group decided to write down all the functional requirement of the tea maker.  This allowed us to better understand the fundamental requirements and short list ideas which were necessary for the machine to fulfil the client’s requirement, and which would also fit into the given budget.
mid.result: The outcome was positive as it allowed to set clear goal for the tea maker by fulfilling all our client’s need and remands while being within a budget. This also ensured that our creative ideas were also implemented into the machines.

mid.situation: We set roles for everyone on in the team so that we know what everyone has to do. We also set alternative roles incase someone was sick or could not complete their task.
mid.task_action: One team member was tasked with being the presenter of our presentation from the week before hand which we had all worked on. Towards the end of our meeting we all went over the presentation to ensure everyone was happy. When it was time to present the team member was not able to present the presentation.
mid.result: We set alternative roles at the start of our meeting to ensure that we would be ready for a situation like this. We were able to count on our other team members to step in and take over the role to present to the class.

mid.situation: Interviewers ask this question to discover what your own approach is to diversity, equality and inclusion. It's helpful to draw from your own work experiences, but it's also an excellent opportunity to look into what these values mean to you, and what these values look like in your industry.      Task: 'To me, diversity means a variety of backgrounds, people and perspectives. I have had experience working in both organisations that value diversity and those that don't, and the difference is clear — workplaces need diversity if they want to succeed. Valuing equality means that everyone has an equal right to a voice in the company, and companies that value this have a much fairer and more positive internal process.
mid.result: Inclusion means that everyone in the workplace feels valued and welcomed. You can't have a functioning workforce without it.'

A side effect of using critical thinking was fixing the overall flow of our Rube Goldberg Machine, as certain elements of our machine did not work well enough with each other.

mid.situation: As part of my university degree I have been required to undertake numerous presentations and group tasks to complete my study and this has meant stepping outside of my comfort zone.      TASK: My primary task was to overcome my apprehension and improve my public speaking and teamwork skills. This involved researching and understanding the topic, coordinating with team members, creating a compelling presentation, and effectively communicating my ideas to the audience.      ACTION: To address the challenge, I took several strategic steps. First I practiced my presentation multiple times to gain confidence. To improve my teamwork skills, I took the initiative to communicate openly and effectively with my group members. I made sure to actively listen to their ideas and contributions and encouraged a collaborative approach where everyone's input was valued. Furthermore, I took advantage of resources such as public speaking workshops and online tutorials to improve my presentation skills.
mid.result: As a result of my proactive approach, I was able to significantly improve my public speaking and teamwork skills. additionally, the experience has had a positive impact on my personal development. I have gained more confidence and have become more comfortable with stepping outside of my comfort zone, which has already proved to be valuable in providing me a new job opportunity and will likely be beneficial in the future.

mid.situation: I needed to interview students about their pain points in the university and provide suitable solutions as a part of the task given for this unit.
mid.task_action: The university has a diversified culture of students but can be divided into two main types i.e. domestic and international. I tried to interview multiple students. Once I got some related pain points like the PACE unit for almost all my fellow international students and accommodation, I decided to brainstorm solutions upon hearing the suggestions of the students I interviewed.
mid.result: The result was that I believe I was able to give suggestions like the university can reduce its on-campus accommodation and also increase its capacity to cater to the huge number of students. For the PACE unit, the employers don't want to give internships to students who do not have full working rights. Macquarie University can provide placements by partnering with related industries.

mid.situation: The task assigned to us by our tutors consisted of selecting a pain point and solving the issue. The pain point I desired to solve was the difficulty of maintaining a healthy work-life balance.
mid.task_action: During the tutorial classes, I chose a pain point from the provided list and began taking steps to realise how I would solve it. This included promoting flexibility (flexible hours, telecommunication), prioritising tasks (completing tasks based on urgency and importance), setting boundaries (setting specific hours to work) and encouraging self-care (exercise, breaks in between work and meditation).
mid.result: The results of incorporating these actions were noticeable tangible improvements in both work performance and overall well-being for ones self.

mid.situation: We faced a decision as a team on a project involving certain materials whose use could have an influence on the environment and society.
mid.task_action: Acknowledging the significance of ethical considerations, I started a team conversation regarding the moral ramifications of our design decisions. I described the assignment of assessing substitute materials that would lessen adverse effects while still fulfilling project specifications. We investigated sustainable materials, taking into account aspects like biodegradability, recyclability, and ethical sourcing. We also examined the design's possible social repercussions, including how it would affect underprivileged groups or nearby towns.
mid.result: We found ecologically friendly materials that matched our project goals and moral values as a consequence of our actions. We reduced the project's ecological impact and enhanced social responsibility by choosing sustainable alternatives. Our choice to place a higher priority on sustainability and ethics not only made our project seem more credible, but it also helped team members see the value of ethical considerations in engineering practice.

mid.situation: In a previous engineering project I was tasked with designing the CAD model for the final project we did for a unit. As someone who has done a few CAD designs before and did quite well in my CAD and design engineering unit, I was confident in undertaking this role. The challenge was adhering to the engineering design standards and presenting it in a professional manner as well as taking the initiative in design and input while our team designed the component for the project.      	*   TASK: My task was to research and apply design principles to create a simple-to-assemble, easy-to-operate part, and aesthetically pleasing part for our engineering project.      	*   ACTION: I began by conducting thorough research on the engineering design standards relevant to our project. I then used my CAD skills to create initial designs, keeping in mind the need for simplicity, ease of operation, and aesthetic appeal. I also sought input from my team members, incorporating their ideas and feedback into the design. I iteratively refined the model, ensuring each version improved upon the last.      	*
mid.result: The final CAD model was well-received by both my team and our project supervisor. It adhered to the required engineering design standards and was praised for its simplicity, functionality, and aesthetic appeal. The component was successfully manufactured and integrated into the final project, contributing significantly to the overall success of our unit project. This experience further solidified my CAD design skills and reinforced the importance of team collaboration in design projects.

mid.situation: We needed to be well versed of the required readings for the SGTA in order to have the knowledge to answer the questions and help us prepare for our presentations. Main problem was there was too many readings to go through and we needed to figure out how we can do it as fast as possible why still be well verse in them.
mid.task_action: So what we did was brainstormed and discussed on how we can counter this problem as we did not have time individually to read all the readings while balancing all of our responsibilities outside of university. We came up with having each person to a set number of readings and making summary notes on those and another person works on another set of readings and so on and so forth. We also thought all talking about the readings rather then reading them separately as by talking about the readings out loud it allowed has to retain the information way better while the others took in that information. Basically to sum up what we did, we just made notes and dot points of important information of the readings and from our notes had discussions about it, mainly assisted us in our SGTA questions during class while having the tutors input with it as well with the questions.
mid.result: It worked to an great extent, it saved us time and improved our work efficiency on completing the SGTA questions by doing the readings in a more effective way by making summary notes and having discussions of them. It gave us the knowledge to hopefully help us significantly for out presentation in weeks 9 to 13.

mid.situation: During the marble machine project we were tasked with designing and creating a working marble box machine that moved marbles from point A to B in an aesthetically pleasing manner. To tackle this problem my group and I assigned group roles to ensure member accountability in the project.       TASK: As a group we assigned a team leader in order to oversee the project's execution and ensure that every part of the project was completed on time and functioned correctly. This involved coordinating among group members each responsible for a specific aspect of the machine which included: designing the track, sourcing materials and assembling the structure.      ACTION: To ensure accountability the group leader established clear responsibilities for each team members and set up weekly meetings for progress updates. This encouragement of open communication allowed team members to discuss challenges and identify problems. Furthermore weekly meetings allowed us to track the performance of each member where every member was required to log their progress and upcoming tasks. By facilitating open communication networks everyone was able  to stay informed of each others work and this provided a sense of accountability among team members.
mid.result: Open communications channels fostered a strong sense of accountability among team members. Everyone in the group understood their assigned roles and their impact towards the project's success. The project was completed on time without delay and functioned according to expectations. This experience illustrates the importance of accountability in achieving team success, especially in large group projects.

mid.situation: our group of 6 needed to complete a lab experiment however felt unorganised with everyone working on one section of the exercise. This would lead to mainly 2 members working on the experiment with the remainder not contributing with little discussion or ideas thrown around.
mid.task_action: we realised this wouldn’t be an efficient way of working so we held a team meeting and devised our group for each person to have their own roles e.g. one measures, one records, one reads the instructions, etc... We would do this by letting each person choose what part they would like to be held accountable for and then once our work is completed, we would reflect and discuss on our findings.
mid.result: once everyone was accountable for their work productivity went up as well as general communication within the group. We would have each member ask more questions about our experiment, getting more involved in order to complete their role. This resulted in our experiment being completed faster then we first thought and engaged every member to contribute due to each person not wanting to let down the group.

mid.situation: During the second half of my ENGG2000 unit, our team had a change in leadership roles, where our 3rd year peers changed involvement. As a result of this, there was now a lack of communication between our structures, motions, and comms sub-teams, and this severely hindered progress on the marble machine.
mid.task_action: Faced with this opportunity I stepped up to become the main development lead and communication between the 3 sub-teams. Even before this, I was tasked with designing and implementing certain 'motions' related aspects of the machine, so I had a good knowledge about the system, on top of this I had already been conversing with the structures team from time to time, so with this change in the leadership dynamic, it just seemed like I would be the right person for the job. Following this, I took on more and more responsibilities, regularly checked in with individual people and their progress, and at times even helped them out (as I had a wide knowledge of CAD, programming, designing, electronics, manufacturing I was able see the entire project to completion). When faced with problems I discussed with team members, before coming to a solution based upon my own and others' suggestions.
mid.result: By having a designated (not officially in this case) person who has an over arching knowledge about the system, and with good communication between sub-teams, the marble machine was successfully completed, and as it turned out, ours was reliable and robust and thus had earned its spot on the marble machine wall.

mid.situation: We needed to talk together about what previous leadership positions we had in the past and how those could help us lead in engineering.

mid.result: The result of this task was that each of us could gain ideas for what we could do and not do in future leadership positions as we would be accountable for it.

mid.situation: I was required to present an oral presentation for university subject CIVL2301 Structural Analysis, talking about a wooden truss project undertaken in that unit. The presentation was an outline of materials used, forces, design and loads.
mid.task_action: My role was to present to the audience the calculations done in the project, by orally speaking to students and staff, and going through presentation slides. I chose a fixed point in the room, in order to block out the stares and stay focused to what i need to say.

mid.situation: During a group project at university, our team of engineering students faced a challenging situation. We were tasked with developing a  marvelous marble machine, a complex and intricate contraption designed to be stackable with other HD styled boxes.  As the project deadline approached, tensions arose within the team regarding the balance between meeting the deadline and ensuring the functionality and creativity of the machine.      TASK: As the team leader, my task was to address the ethical concerns surrounding the development of the marvelous marble machine while ensuring that we met the project deadline set by our professors.      ACTION: Recognizing the importance of upholding ethical principles in engineering, I called for an urgent team meeting. During the meeting, I openly shared my concerns about compromising the creativity and functionality of the machine to meet the deadline. Together, we brainstormed alternative strategies to balance project timelines with ethical considerations. We revisited our design, conducted additional tests, and consulted with our professors and tutors for guidance.
mid.result: Through our collective efforts, we successfully delivered the marvelous marble machine that not only met the project deadline but also showcased innovative design and functionality. Our machine did all the HD requirements from vertical movements and to LED features. Our team happy and had a sense of accomplishment for our commitment to upholding ethical principles in our work, as well as praise from peers for the creativity and ingenuity of our machine. Furthermore, this experience deepened our understanding of the importance of ethical integrity in engineering projects.

mid.situation: I was tasked with creating a presentation for a specific company with several teammates . The presentation needed to effectively convey our company's value proposition, market opportunity, and financial projections.
mid.task_action: To tackle this challenge, I first researched information that I want online then outlined the key points that needed to be covered in the presentation. I approached the task systematically. My task was to craft a presentation that effectively communicated our company's potential to prospective audience. I collaborated with members to gather relevant data and insights. We then worked together to design best presentation layout for each individual.
mid.result: The result of our efforts was a comprehensive presentation is composed and achieved our goal. During the research part, our presentation effectively captured useful information, clearly articulating our value proposition and growth strategy. As a result, I got a finalised presentation draft.

mid.situation: Our team was tasked with delivering an oral presentation on a complex research project to a panel of experts and stakeholders at a conference.
mid.task_action: First, I identified the key objectives of the presentation, including clearly communicating our research findings, demonstrating the significance of our results, and engaging the audience effectively. Next, I coordinated with my team to divide responsibilities, ensuring each member had a specific role aligned with their expertise. We conducted thorough research on the topic, gathering relevant data and examples to support our arguments. In preparation for the presentation, we rehearsed multiple times, refining our delivery and timing. Additionally, I organised a mock presentation session where we received constructive feedback from peers to further improve our performance.
mid.result: The result was a successful oral presentation that effectively communicated our research findings and engaged the audience. Our presentation received positive feedback from the panel of experts, who praised our clear communication style, compelling arguments, and thorough research. As a result, our team's work was recognised and we were invited to present our findings at additional conferences, expanding the impact of our research. Additionally, the experience of preparing and delivering the presentation enhanced our teamwork and communication skills, preparing us for future collaborative projects.

mid.situation: I had completed a 3D puzzle project that took me six hours to complete. I left it on the table and when I came back home after 7 hours it was damaged. The walls collapsed and pieces fell on the floor.
mid.task_action: I took time and reevaluated my choices in materials and types of glue used in the project. I also researched the potential reasons why it may have collapsed so easily- due to lack of drying time, the wrong type of glue mixing with the materials, excessive wind, an external reason (a person or animal knocking it down), or bad placement in my home.
mid.result: Gathering my research I concluded that I must use the E6000 glue in accordance with the material types, I should place it in an area with no windows near (preferably my cupboard or desk), and I should let it dry for a good 8 hours before attempting to relocate it.

mid.situation: As a part of our weekly SGTAs, we were tasked with deriving common student pain points through interactions with students on campus.
mid.task_action: Using our learning from the SGTA activities and the Online LinkedIn Learning, we formulated a strategy to extract genuine pain points from on-campus students by asking open-ended questions about various aspects of university life. Once we had exhausted our list of pre-prepared questions and delved deeper into particular points on a case-by-case basis, we regrouped to compare our research and siphoned out the most common root pain points.
mid.result: The result was the achievement of the SGTA's key goal, which was the identification of entrepreneurial opportunities that would allow for the solving of most, if not all, of the common student pain points.

mid.situation: For an open project for a unit last year I chose to make an EVM (electronic voting machine) using Arduino UNO, with the intention of making it accessible to all members.
mid.task_action: Firstly I started brainstorming how to start with the code and trying to understand what hardware will be required to make the EVM. I started putting all the hardware together like lcd screen, buttons etc. Then accordingly I made the code and started doing tests with it and finalized everything and made it work.
mid.result: After weeks of hard work I made the EVM working and was open for everyone in our unit to use and contribute for improvement. The EVM was user friendly and most importantly secure so people could trust it for votes.

mid.situation: I have had to run meetings for my job in construction management.
mid.task_action: We have a few weekly meetings which I have been in charge of running. This meetings included many engineers, tradesmen, project managers and the client. mid.situation: In my role as a bar supervisor, I encountered recurring inventory shortages during peak hours, negatively impacting customer satisfaction and revenue.      TASK: My objective was to overhaul our inventory management system to mitigate shortages and ensure seamless operations during busy shifts.      ACTION: First, I conducted a comprehensive analysis of our inventory processes, identifying discrepancies and inefficiencies. I then implemented a new system, which included regular stock audits, standardized recording procedures, and automated reorder alerts. Concurrently, I organized training sessions for staff, emphasizing the importance of accurate inventory management and providing guidance on utilizing the new system effectively. Additionally, I assigned specific team members to oversee inventory duties during shifts to ensure accountability and accuracy. Collaborating closely with the kitchen and management team, I established clear communication channels to optimize stock ordering and minimize wastage.
mid.result: These proactive measures yielded significant improvements. Stock shortages during peak hours were noticeably reduced, leading to smoother operations and heightened customer satisfaction. The revamped inventory management system facilitated data-driven decision-making and trend analysis, further optimizing stock levels. Moreover, staff morale soared as they gained confidence in their roles and appreciated the structured approach to inventory management. Overall, the strategic interventions not only addressed the pain points we faced but also fostered a more productive and cohesive work environment. The success of the revamped inventory system translated into better service delivery and enhanced profitability for the establishment.

mid.situation: While overseeing the construction of a marble machine, I encountered a challenge with the spiral mechanism. Due to a miscommunication in the initial design phase, the spiral was not aligning properly with the rest of the machine, causing marbles to frequently jam and disrupt the flow of the entire system.      TASK: Recognising the importance of accountability in ensuring the success of the project, my task was to address the issue swiftly and effectively. It was imperative to take ownership of the problem and devise a solution that would rectify the misalignment and optimise the functionality of the spiral mechanism.      ACTION: To tackle the issue, I initiated a thorough assessment of the spiral mechanism, meticulously examining its components and identifying the root cause of the misalignment. Collaborating closely with the design team, I facilitated open communication to discuss possible modifications and adjustments needed to realign the spiral with precision. Implementing a structured plan, I coordinated with the engineering team to execute the necessary changes, which involved recalibrating certain elements of the mechanism and fine-tuning its positioning within the machine
mid.result: Through proactive accountability and decisive action, the issue with the spiral mechanism was successfully resolved. The realignment efforts led to a significant improvement in the functionality of the marble machine, eliminating the occurrence of jams and ensuring smooth operation. As a result of my actions, the project progressed seamlessly, meeting its objectives within the specified timeframe. Additionally, fostering a culture of accountability within the team enhanced overall cohesion and effectiveness, paving the way for future success in our collaborative endeavours.

mid.situation: Tasked with constructing a data investigation based on a letter of interest, I encountered the challenge of demonstrating initiative and making decisions amidst uncertainty.      TASK:       My responsibility was to navigate through ambiguity and take proactive steps to initiate a data investigation based on the letter of interest. This required me to make decisions despite incomplete information and unclear objectives.      ACTION:       To tackle the task, I embraced uncertainty as an opportunity for innovation and problem-solving. Instead of waiting for all details to be clarified, I took the initiative to gather relevant information and define initial goals for the investigation.      I began by closely examining the letter of interest, extracting key points, and identifying potential areas of interest for analysis. Despite the lack of explicit guidance, I proactively reached out to stakeholders for clarification and insights to inform my decisions.      Drawing on my experience and expertise, I made informed assumptions and judgments to fill in gaps and establish a direction for the investigation. This required a willingness to take calculated risks and trust in my capabilities to navigate through uncertainty.      Collaborating with colleagues, I solicited diverse perspectives and input to validate my decisions and refine our approach. Through open communication and collaborative problem-solving, we collectively addressed uncertainties and built consensus on the path forward.
mid.result: By demonstrating initiative and decisiveness in the face of uncertainty, we successfully initiated a data investigation aligned with the organization's needs. Our proactive approach enabled us to overcome challenges, adapt to evolving circumstances, and lay the groundwork for a comprehensive analysis.      REFLECTION:       This experience reinforced the importance of taking initiative and making decisions in uncertain situations. By embracing ambiguity as an opportunity for action rather than hesitation, I was able to drive progress and contribute to meaningful outcomes. Moving forward, I am committed to maintaining a proactive mindset and leveraging uncertainty as a catalyst for innovation and growth.

mid.situation: In a university group project, there was a disparity in the distribution of workload and decision-making among group members. The reason for this is certain individuals with dominant personalities often took control of the significant roles to receive more recognition for their contributions       TASK:      In this situation, I felt the need to address the disparities and introduce a more equitable and inclusive environment for the team. The process involved talking to different members and discussing ways to equity among the group independent of personalities.      ACTION:      Several actions were implemented to address the task and fix the situation. Firstly, I initiated discussions with team members about the distribution of the workload and the importance of being equitable about workload. I also discussed the need to address the disparities and reflect on how we could all successfully impact the group and the common goal. Secondly, I redistributed responsibilities with the whole group present to ensure equal workloads. Thirdly, I addressed bias and authoritarian behaviour. Recognising this behaviour helped the team to address it and raise awareness of unconscious and conscious behaviour promoting more equitable interactions.
mid.result: As a result of these actions, the group became more equitable and everyone was provided equal opportunity to participate. The redistribution of responsibilities led to an equitable workload and all team members being recognised for their contributions. This improvement created a more suitable team environment which helped us achieve a higher grade than I thought possible.

mid.situation: In my third-year unit, CIVL3301 (Design of Concrete Structures), my group was tasked with designing a carpark structure on campus. This project required us to plan and virtually design a structurally sound carpark that adhered to relevant Australian Standards (AS).       We faced significant challenges in identifying the appropriate standards to follow.       There were also uncertainties regarding the minimum/appropriate measurements for car spaces, parking spaces, turning radii, and the design of pedestrian access points such as lift shafts, stairs, and ramps.      TASK: Our group had to overcome these uncertainties by identifying the correct Australian Standards and making informed assumptions about the various measurements needed for our design.       We aimed to develop a comprehensive and compliant design report for the parking lot structure that addressed all specifications and constraints.      ACTION: To tackle these challenges, we took the initiative to organise a series of focused meetings within the group. In these meetings, we divided the project into smaller, manageable sections, assigning specific tasks to each group member based on their strengths and areas of expertise.       For the Australian Standards, I reached out to our convenor for guidance and also consulted online databases (i.e. SAI Global and MQ Multisearch) to gather the necessary information and AS documentation.      With the assumptions regarding measurements, we conducted a comparative study of existing parking lot structures on and off campus (i.e. comparing the South 2 Carpark with Bella Vista Metro Station's carpark), collecting data on average dimensions and layouts (tape measuring and counting spaces) and taking photos for future reference. This research allowed us to make educated assumptions for our design.
mid.result: Our proactive and multipronged approach and willingness to navigate through uncertainty resulted in the design report being completed. We identified all the relevant Australian Standards for our project and made cautious assumptions about the design measurements. The final design was compliant with the necessary standards.

mid.situation: In my ENGG4001 Professional Practice course, my team were confronted with the theoretical challenge of designing a water purification plant. A significant pain point in such infrastructure is the reliance on non-renewable energy sources, which conflicts with modern sustainability goals.      TASK: With my major in Electronics and Electrical Engineering, I was tasked with investigating potential energy sources for the plant. The goal was to propose a primary energy source complemented by a backup system, ensuring the plant's operational reliability in various scenarios without compromising environmental values.      ACTION: I conducted extensive research into renewable energy sources and backup systems that could support the energy needs of a theoretical water purification plant. After considering several alternatives, I proposed solar panels as the main energy source due to their suitability for the geographical location we had selected for our project. For the backup system, I chose a diesel generator for its reliability and ease of integration. I prepared a conceptual design that included an intelligent control system to manage the energy supply between the solar panels and the generator, ensuring maximum efficiency and minimal waste.
mid.result: My solution addressed the central pain points of sustainability and reliability, reducing the hypothetical plant's carbon footprint and operational costs. The project, while conceptual, was a practical exercise in pinpointing and solving engineering pain points through innovative thinking and application of electrical engineering principles. It demonstrated a proactive approach in resolving key industry challenges, paving the way for more sustainable engineering practices.

mid.situation: During the project of my engineering course, my team faced a significant challenge. We were tasked with designing a cost-effective water purification system for rural communities. The initial concept was promising, but initial tests revealed it was less effective in removing contaminants than required by safety standards.      Task:As the project leader, my primary task was to enhance our design to meet safety standards without escalating costs prohibitively. It was crucial to maintain the project's viability by keeping the solution both effective and economically feasible.      Action: I initiated a series of critical thinking sessions with the team. We began by analyzing the existing design to pinpoint inefficiencies and potential areas for improvement. Using a structured problem-solving approach, we listed all possible modifications, such as integrating a more efficient filtration material or redesigning the flow system to increase contact time with the filter. Each idea was critically evaluated based on its potential impact on contaminant removal and cost implications. We also consulted with our professors and industry experts to gain insights and validate our findings.
mid.result: Through these critical thinking exercises, we selected and implemented a dual-stage filtration system that significantly increased the purification efficacy without a substantial cost increase. The redesigned system not only met the safety standards but also remained within the budget constraints. This approach not only resolved the initial problem but also enhanced our team's ability to tackle complex issues systematically. The project received commendable feedback for its innovation and practical applicability. This experience underscored the value of critical thinking in engineering, enabling us to deliver a solution that was both technically sound and economically viable.

mid.situation: Designing a Customer-Centric IoT Tea Machine      Our team was tasked with designing a new internet of things (IoT) tea machine for a client (Teacher). The client envisioned a machine that catered to a variety of tea preferences while offering remote control and brewing customization options.      Task: Understanding User Needs and Translating Them into Design Features      Our primary challenge was to translate the client's broad vision into specific functionalities and features for the tea machine. While the client desired an advanced IoT tea machine, we lacked specific details regarding user preferences and brewing habits.      Action: Implementing Critical Thinking Through User Research and Collaboration      To address this challenge, we employed critical thinking through a multi-step process:      	*   User Research: We conducted in-depth user research through surveys and questionaries with the client. This helped us gather valuable data on tea preferences, brewing habits, and desired functionalities in their IoT tea machine.      	*   Feature Brainstorming: Based on the user research findings, we held brainstorming sessions as a team every SGTA for 5 weeks. We used techniques like mind mapping and role-playing to generate a wide range of potential features, from remote brewing control via a smartphone app to pre-programmed settings for different tea types.      	*   Critical Evaluation and Prioritization: Following the brainstorming session, we critically evaluated each proposed feature against the clients requirements. We considered factors like user needs, technical feasibility, and potential impact on the overall design and user experience. We then prioritized the features based on their importance and contribution to the client's vision.      	*   Iterative Design: We adopted an iterative design approach. We developed initial prototypes focusing on core functionalities and then presented them to the client for feedback. This feedback loop allowed us to refine the design and ensure it aligned with user needs and expectations.
mid.result: A User-Centric IoT Tea Machine Design      Through critical thinking and a user-centric design approach, we delivered a comprehensive design for the IoT tea machine. The final design incorporated features like:      	*   A mobile app for remote control and brewing customization.      	*   Pre-programmed settings for various tea types.      	*   Automatic water level detection and refill reminders.      	*   Voice control integration for hands-free operation (optional).      The client was highly satisfied with the final design, which not only addressed their initial vision but also prioritized user needs and ensured a positive user experience. This project exemplified the importance of critical thinking in design, particularly in translating broad client visions into user-centric and well-defined product functionalities.

mid.situation: IN MY SECOND YEAR OF ENGINEERING, OUR TEAM WAS TASKED WITH DESIGNING AN AUTOMATIC TEA MAKER. THE PROJECT REQUIRED US TO INTEGRATE VARIOUS ENGINEERING DISCIPLINES, INCLUDING MECHANICAL DESIGN FOR THE BREWING MECHANISM, ELECTRICAL ENGINEERING FOR THE CONTROL SYSTEM, AND SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT FOR USER INTERFACE AND AUTOMATION. MIDWAY THROUGH THE PROJECT, WE ENCOUNTERED A SIGNIFICANT CHALLENGE: OUR INITIAL DESIGN FOR THE CONTROL SYSTEM WAS NOT RELIABLE, LEADING TO THEORETICAL INCONSISTENCIES IN THE BREWING PROCESS.      Task:      As a team member, my role was to contribute to the analysis and redesign of our control system to ensure that our automatic tea maker would theoretically produce high-quality tea consistently. This involved collaborating with my teammates to identify the root causes of the inconsistencies and propose effective solutions.      Action:      We organized several group troubleshooting sessions where we critically analyzed our initial control system design. Using critical thinking techniques, we identified potential issues such as sensor inaccuracies and timing mismatches. I actively participated in questioning our initial assumptions and exploring alternative solutions. Together, we tested various components and systems through simulations and theoretical models. We also sought feedback from our professors and peers to refine our design. This iterative process included brainstorming sessions to generate new ideas and using decision matrices to evaluate the feasibility and impact of each proposed solution.
mid.result: Through our collaborative critical thinking approach, we were able to identify and address the theoretical inconsistencies in our control system. We made several design changes, such as improving sensor accuracy and refining the timing algorithms. After thorough theoretical testing and validation, our design for the automatic tea maker consistently met the desired specifications. The project was completed successfully and received positive feedback for its innovative approach and thoroughness. This experience highlighted the importance of critical thinking and teamwork in solving complex engineering problems.

mid.situation: To complete quiz 2 on "The House of Quality" by John R. Hauser and Don Clausing, I came across a real-world situation that called for employing the concepts covered in the class. In particular, the quiz asked participants to compare Toyota's improved rust prevention techniques with the design choices made by an American fast car maker on where to put the emergency brake lever.      Task/ Action: I read the information in the magazine from May 1988 in great detail before taking the quiz. It was essential to comprehend the House of Quality idea and how it applied to quality function deployment (QFD). I answered the quiz by dissecting the prerequisites since I knew the material well. Firstly, I examined the provided scenario, which contrasted Toyota's improved rust prevention strategy with the location of the emergency brake lever on an American sports vehicle. Secondly, I determined the engineering characteristics (ECs) and customer attributes (CAs) pertinent to each scenario by using the House of Quality's tenets. In the case of the emergency brake lever, CAs may include driver accessibility, whereas ECs might include safety and ergonomic design. thirdly, I created a connection matrix map that showed how every technical choice affected how customers saw it. This required assessing each EC's importance in reaching the targeted CAs. Finally, Based on the data, I came to certain conclusions and made some main ideas in my mind, This will help me in answering quiz 2.
mid.result: After this article, My ability to demonstrate a thorough comprehension of the principles and their practical ramifications was demonstrated by applying the House of Quality framework to the exam scenario. The end product was a thorough study and a solution proposal that skillfully combined engineering concerns with customer objectives. This strategy not only demonstrated my mastery of the subject matter but also offered insightful information on the significance of quality control and customer-centric design in product development. and I receive a decent grade for it as well.

mid.situation: I was to make an internet-based tea-maker machine, as a project under the ENGG2050 course. It was a multi-discipline process of design and strategy, picking the best from several potential designs. The project also considered specifics of system engineering in terms of conceptual design, detailed design, as well as logistic planning.      T-TASK:      The main responsibility was to use models to make a decision on what the more cost-effective way is to design an Internet-based tea machine that fits all the technical requirements and maximizes the profit. Because the alternatives of the design structure were different possible profits and costs, I used the Maximin and Maximax Criteria from Decision Making under Uncertainty; it allowed me to evaluate predetermined designs in their worst-case and best-case outcomes. Such methods provide a clear comparison, assuring that results fulfill financial goals and the technical specifications of the project.      A - ACTION:   During this task, the following models of decision-making were used:      MAXIMIN CRITERION: The model considered a worst-case scenario of each design alternative. By analyzing the minimum possible profit each design could generate, I ensured that our final choice would be the most prudent under the most pessimistic conditions. This approach is particularly useful when the reduction of risks is critical, e.g., in the allocation of expensive materials or technologies that can drive costs.      MAXIMAX CRITERION: On the other hand, the Maximax Criterion was applied to select the kind of design alternative that could yield maximum profits in the best-case scenario. The model would highlight the most optimistic outcomes and key to keep the stakeholder engaged who are interested in high potential returns on an investment.      Such models were important in our decision-making process, and we enabled them to:      	*   Evaluate the financial feasibility of each alternative and compare them effectively in the design.       	*   Clearly present the pros and cons of each option to the stakeholder from a financial angle so they could properly understand the potential economic impacts of their choices.      	*   Predict and plan for potential financial results, thus reinforcing our project's overall financial strategy.      R -
mid.result: Risk implications considered, the decision-making models allowed us to pick a design that was cost-effective and at the same time met all the technical requirements with fewer risk implications. This demonstrates the effect of systematic decision evaluation in engineering design.
